<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive inductive transducer that converts linear displacement (a mechanical input quantity) into a corresponding, proportional electrical output voltage signal. It operates on the principle of mutual electromagnetic induction between a primary excitation coil and two symmetrically placed secondary coils, with the mutual inductance being varied by the axial displacement of a movable soft-iron (ferromagnetic) core.

Formally, as per KTU 2024 Scheme standard terminology:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An LVDT is defined as a displacement-type **electromechanical transducer** in which the **mutual inductance** between a primary winding (energized by a stable AC excitation) and a pair of **identically wound, series-opposition connected secondary windings** is altered by the linear motion of a high-permeability core, producing a **differential output voltage** that is a linear function of the core's displacement from the null (electrical zero) position.

It belongs to the family of **variable reluctance transducers** and is classified under **active transducers** that require external AC excitation power. The fundamental output is an analog, AC-coupled differential voltage that requires **phase-sensitive demodulation** to resolve displacement direction and magnitude.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **seesaw (a balance beam balanced on a central pivot)**, where a small weight is slid along its length.

- When the weight is **exactly at the center** (null position), the seesaw is perfectly horizontal — the "output" is zero. The two ends are level.
- When you slide the weight slightly to the **left**, the left side dips down while the right side rises. The difference in heights between the two ends tells you **how far** the weight has moved, and the fact that the *left* is lower tells you the **direction** of the movement.

An LVDT works exactly like this seesaw, but with **electromagnetic "tilting"** instead of gravity:

- The **central pivot** = a primary coil energized with AC voltage.
- The **two ends of the seesaw** = two secondary coils wound in opposite directions, connected in series opposition.
- The **sliding weight** = a soft-iron core that slides inside a hollow bore.
- The **height difference of the two ends** = the differential output voltage $V_{out} = V_{S1} - V_{S2}$.

When the core is centered, the induced EMFs in the two secondaries are **equal in magnitude but opposite in phase**, so they cancel each other — output is zero. When the core moves toward one secondary, that secondary's induced voltage **grows** while the other's **shrinks** — producing a net output whose **amplitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **phase** indicates direction.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Key Specification Parameters)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard LVDT Performance Metrics (Engineering Reference Values)**
> - **Excitation Frequency:** Typically **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (commonly 5 kHz or 10 kHz)
> - **Excitation Voltage:** Typically **1 V to 24 V RMS** (commonly 5 V or 10 V RMS)
> - **Operating Range:** ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm (typical industrial: ±25 mm)
> - **Linearity:** **0.1% to 0.5% of full scale** for standard units; high-grade units can achieve **0.05%**
> - **Sensitivity:** Typically **0.1 V/mm to 10 V/mm** depending on design
> - **Core Material:** High-permeability soft ferromagnetic alloy (e.g., **Ni-Fe alloy / Mumetal / Permalloy**)
> - **Resolution:** Practically **infinite** (limited only by the readout electronics, since there are no moving electrical contacts)
> - **Hysteresis:** Typically **< 0.01% of full scale**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of an LVDT

The LVDT consists of the following key physical components:

1. **Primary Winding (P):** A single coil wound on a cylindrical bobbin (former). It is energized by a stable, sinusoidal AC excitation voltage $V_p$ of fixed amplitude and frequency. This winding is positioned at the **axial center** of the assembly.
2. **Two Identical Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂):** Two coils wound **identically** (same number of turns, same wire gauge) and placed **symmetrically** on either side of the primary. They are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract), which is the defining wiring topology that makes the device *differential*.
3. **Movable Soft-Iron Core:** A cylindrical, hollow-core-shaped piece of high-permeability ferromagnetic material that is mechanically coupled to the physical quantity being measured. The core slides freely along the central axis inside the hollow bore of the bobbin.
4. **Shielding:** A cylindrical outer shell of high-permeability material (such as Mumetal) provides **magnetic shielding** against external stray fields and also serves as a flux return path.
5. **Coil Form / Bobbin:** Non-magnetic, dimensionally stable former (ceramic or polymer) that supports the windings.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The Three Zones

The operation can be cleanly decomposed into three physical regions of core position:

### Case 1: Null Position (Zero Displacement)
- The core is **exactly at the axial center**, equidistant from both secondaries.
- The reluctance paths through S₁ and S₂ are **identical**.
- The flux linking S₁ equals the flux linking S₂: $\Phi_{S1} = \Phi_{S2}$
- By Faraday's law, the induced EMFs are equal in magnitude: $E_{S1} = E_{S2}$
- Because they are connected in **series opposition**, they cancel perfectly:
$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0 \text{ V}$$
- The **null voltage** (residual output) is never exactly zero due to imperfections, harmonics, and stray capacitances, but is typically **< 0.5% of full-scale output**.

### Case 2: Displacement Toward S₁ (Core on the Left, for Convention)
- The core shifts leftward by displacement $x$.
- The reluctance of the magnetic path through S₁ **decreases** (high-permeability core enhances flux linkage).
- The reluctance of the magnetic path through S₂ **increases** (less core coupling).
- Therefore: $E_{S1} > E_{S2}$, giving a **net positive output** $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} > 0$.

### Case 3: Displacement Toward S₂ (Core on the Right)
- Symmetrically, $E_{S2} > E_{S1}$, giving a **net negative output** $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} < 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase-Sensitive Detection Principle:** The **sign** of the differential output voltage is not directly recoverable from a single RMS voltmeter reading — instead, the **phase** of $E_{out}$ relative to the primary excitation $V_p$ is the indicator of displacement direction. This is why the LVDT is always paired with a **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** in real instrumentation chains.

## 2.3 Output Voltage Phasor Analysis

Let:
- $V_p$ = primary excitation voltage (reference phasor)
- $N_p$, $N_s$ = number of turns in primary and each secondary
- $M_1$ = mutual inductance between primary and S₁ (depends on core position)
- $M_2$ = mutual inductance between primary and S₂ (depends on core position)

The induced EMFs are:

$$E_{S1} = j\omega M_1 \cdot I_p$$

$$E_{S2} = j\omega M_2 \cdot I_p$$

The differential output is:

$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = j\omega (M_1 - M_2) \cdot I_p$$

Since $I_p = V_p / j\omega L_p$ (where $L_p$ is the primary inductance, assumed to be approximately constant for small displacements), we get:

$$\boxed{E_{out} = \frac{V_p}{L_p} (M_1 - M_2)}$$

In the **linear region** of operation (small displacements), $M_1 - M_2$ is directly proportional to the core displacement $x$, with a constant of proportionality $k_s$ called the **sensitivity (V/mm)**:

$$\boxed{E_{out} = k_s \cdot x}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2}$ | Differential output voltage definition | V |
| 2 | $E_{S1} = j\omega M_1 I_p$ | Faraday's law for secondary S₁ | V |
| 3 | $E_{S2} = j\omega M_2 I_p$ | Faraday's law for secondary S₂ | V |
| 4 | $E_{out} = j\omega(M_1 - M_2)I_p$ | Combined differential output | V |
| 5 | $E_{out} = (V_p / L_p)(M_1 - M_2)$ | Output in terms of excitation voltage | V |
| 6 | $E_{out} = k_s \cdot x$ (linear region) | Linear output-displacement relation | V |
| 7 | $k_s = \text{sensitivity} = dE_{out}/dx$ | Transducer sensitivity | V/mm |
| 8 | $V_{null} \approx 0$ (ideally) | Residual output at null position | V |
| 9 | Linearity $\% = (\Delta y_{max} / Y_{FS}) \times 100$ | Measure of output conformance | % FS |
| 10 | $\text{Residual} = V_{null} / V_{FS} \times 100$ | Null position imperfection | % FS |
| 11 | $V_{S1} - V_{S2} = 2k_s x$ (matched pair) | Symmetric secondaries in series opposition | V |
| 12 | $\text{Frequency response bandwidth} \approx 0$ to $\omega/2$ | Limited by excitation frequency | Hz |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, all magnitude symbols use $\vert \cdot \vert$ equivalent rendering, never the pipe character, to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

LVDTs are ubiquitous in any application demanding **precision, frictionless, contactless displacement measurement**:

- **Aerospace:** Flight control surface position feedback, landing-gear position, hydraulic actuator piston displacement.
- **Industrial Automation:** Machine tool position feedback, robotic arm joints, CNC axis verification.
- **Civil Engineering:** Structural health monitoring (bridge deflection, building sway), dam strain monitoring.
- **Biomedical & Biosensor Integration:** Force-displacement characterization in biomechanical testing rigs, micro-positioning of cell-manipulation micropipettes, prosthetic limb joint angle sensors, **load cell core element** in compression/tension testing machines, and **bioreactor piston position feedback**.
- **Automotive:** Throttle position sensing, suspension travel measurement, automatic transmission valve body position.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mutual Inductance as a Function of Core Position

We model the LVDT as two coupled magnetic circuits. The mutual inductance between the primary and a secondary depends on the overlap length of the core with that secondary winding.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.** Let each secondary winding have a total axial length $L_s$, and let the core have axial length $L_c$. Let $x$ be the displacement of the core's midpoint from the LVDT's electrical null (the point exactly between S₁ and S₂).

**Step 2 — Compute the overlap length with each secondary.** By symmetry, the overlap of the core with S₁ and S₂ is:

$$L_{ov,1} = L_{ov,0} - x$$

$$L_{ov,2} = L_{ov,0} + x$$

where $L_{ov,0}$ is the overlap at null position. (The convention assumes $x$ positive when the core moves toward S₂.)

**Step 3 — Relate mutual inductance to overlap.** The mutual inductance in a linear magnetic circuit is proportional to the cross-sectional area of the high-permeability path, which is itself proportional to the overlap length:

$$M_1 = M_0 \cdot \frac{L_{ov,1}}{L_s} = M_0 \cdot \left( \frac{L_{ov,0} - x}{L_s} \right)$$

$$M_2 = M_0 \cdot \frac{L_{ov,2}}{L_s} = M_0 \cdot \left( \frac{L_{ov,0} + x}{L_s} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the difference $M_1 - M_2$.** Subtracting the two expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 - M_2 &= \frac{M_0}{L_s} \left[ (L_{ov,0} - x) - (L_{ov,0} + x) \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{M_0}{L_s} \left[ L_{ov,0} - x - L_{ov,0} - x \right] \\[6pt]
&= \frac{M_0}{L_s} \left[ -2x \right] \\[6pt]
&= -\frac{2M_0 \cdot x}{L_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the output equation.** From Section 2, $E_{out} = (V_p / L_p)(M_1 - M_2)$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out} &= \frac{V_p}{L_p} \cdot \left( -\frac{2M_0 \cdot x}{L_s} \right) \\[6pt]
E_{out} &= -\frac{2M_0 \cdot V_p}{L_p \cdot L_s} \cdot x
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Define the sensitivity.** The proportionality constant is the sensitivity $k_s$:

$$k_s = -\frac{2M_0 \cdot V_p}{L_p \cdot L_s}$$

giving the compact form:

$$\boxed{E_{out} = k_s \cdot x}$$

> The negative sign carries **phase information**: for $x > 0$ (displacement toward S₂), $E_{out}$ is in antiphase with $V_p$, and vice versa.

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnitude of the Output Voltage (RMS Form)

For sinusoidal excitation $v_p(t) = V_p \sqrt{2} \sin(\omega t)$, the primary current (assuming dominant inductive reactance) is:

$$i_p(t) \approx \frac{V_p \sqrt{2}}{\omega L_p} \cdot \cos(\omega t) = \frac{V_p \sqrt{2}}{X_{L_p}} \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$

The instantaneous EMF in each secondary is:

$$e_{S1}(t) = -N_s \frac{d\Phi_1}{dt} = -M_1 \frac{di_p}{dt}$$

Taking the magnitude and converting to RMS:

$$E_{S1, RMS} = \omega \cdot M_1 \cdot I_{p, RMS} = \frac{\omega M_1 V_{p,RMS}}{\omega L_p} = \frac{M_1}{L_p} V_{p,RMS}$$

Similarly: $E_{S2, RMS} = (M_2 / L_p) \cdot V_{p,RMS}$.

The differential RMS output:

$$E_{out, RMS} = \frac{V_{p,RMS}}{L_p} \vert M_1 - M_2 \vert = \frac{V_{p,RMS}}{L_p} \cdot \frac{2M_0 \vert x \vert}{L_s}$$

$$\boxed{E_{out, RMS} = \frac{2M_0 \cdot V_{p,RMS}}{L_p \cdot L_s} \cdot \vert x \vert}$$

This is the **practical, magnitude-only** formula students must use when solving KTU numerical problems where only RMS values are requested.

## 3.3 Derivation: Linearity Error and Effective Range

Real LVDTs exhibit a **non-linear** response at the extremes of the range due to **fringing flux**. The output is linear only over a limited **rated linear range** of approximately $\pm L_s/3$ from null.

The total output can be empirically expressed as a power series:

$$E_{out}(x) = k_{s,1} x + k_{s,3} x^3 + k_{s,5} x^5 + \ldots$$

The **linearity error** is defined as:

$$\text{Linearity Error} = \frac{\vert E_{actual}(x) - E_{ideal}(x) \vert_{max}}{E_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

where $E_{FS}$ is the full-scale output at the rated maximum displacement.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: At-a-Glance Comparison of Operating Conditions

| Core Position | $E_{S1}$ | $E_{S2}$ | $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2}$ | Phase of $E_{out}$ vs $V_p$ | Indication |
|---------------|----------|----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|------------|
| **Null (centered)** | $E_0$ | $E_0$ | $0$ | Indeterminate (null) | Zero displacement |
| **Slightly Left** | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $+2\Delta E$ | **In-phase** with $V_p$ | Negative $x$ (toward S₁) |
| **Slightly Right** | $E_0 - \Delta E$ | $E_0 + \Delta E$ | $-2\Delta E$ | **Antiphase** with $V_p$ | Positive $x$ (toward S₂) |
| **Far Left (saturated)** | $\approx E_{max}$ | $\approx 0$ | $E_{max}$ | In-phase | End of range, nonlinearity begins |
| **Far Right (saturated)** | $\approx 0$ | $\approx E_{max}$ | $-E_{max}$ | Antiphase | End of range, nonlinearity begins |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** An LVDT has the following specifications: primary excitation $V_p = 5 \text{ V}$ RMS at 5 kHz, primary inductance $L_p = 10 \text{ mH}$, secondary maximum mutual inductance $M_0 = 5 \text{ mH}$, secondary winding length $L_s = 20 \text{ mm}$. The core is displaced 2 mm from null. Calculate the differential RMS output voltage.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Write the output formula:

$$E_{out, RMS} = \frac{2M_0 \cdot V_{p,RMS}}{L_p \cdot L_s} \cdot \vert x \vert$$

Step 2: Substitute values (ensuring SI units):

- $M_0 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $V_{p,RMS} = 5$ V
- $L_p = 10 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $L_s = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\vert x \vert = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m

Step 3: Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{out, RMS} &= \frac{2 \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 5}{(10 \times 10^{-3}) \times (20 \times 10^{-3})} \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}}{200 \times 10^{-6}} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-4}} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= 250 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \\[6pt]
&= 0.5 \text{ V RMS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $E_{out, RMS} = 0.5$ V RMS

Step 4 (bonus): The sensitivity is:

$$k_s = \frac{E_{out, RMS}}{\vert x \vert} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 250 \text{ V/m} = 0.25 \text{ V/mm}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LVDT Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Physical Quantity / Displacement Source] --> B[Soft-Iron Ferromagnetic Core]
    B -- Axial Sliding Inside Bore --> C[Hollow Cylindrical Coil Former / Bobbin]
    C --> D1[Primary Winding P, Centered, AC Excited]
    C --> D2[Secondary Winding S1, Left Side]
    C --> D3[Secondary Winding S2, Right Side]
    D1 -- Mutual Inductance M1 --> D2
    D1 -- Mutual Inductance M2 --> D3
    D2 -- EMF ES1 --> E[Differential Series Opposition Wiring Node]
    D3 -- EMF ES2 --> E
    E -- Eout = ES1 minus ES2 --> F[Phase Sensitive Demodulator / Signal Conditioner]
    F -- DC Analog Output --> G[Display / DAQ / Microcontroller ADC]
    C --> H[Outer Magnetic Shield, Mumetal Casing]
    H -- Stray Flux Rejection --> F
    D1 -- Excitation Vp from AC Source --> D1
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The `B` node is the **movable armature** — its position determines $M_1$ and $M_2$.
- The `E` node is the **series-opposition summing junction** — this is where the differential action physically occurs.
- The `F` node is the **phase-sensitive demodulator** — mandatory companion to any LVDT to recover signed DC output.

## 4.2 Functional Signal Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[AC Excitation Oscillator, 5 to 10 kHz Sine] --> P[Primary Coil of LVDT]
    P <--> C[Magnetic Core, Position x]
    C -. Varies Coupling .-> S1C[Secondary 1]
    C -. Varies Coupling .-> S2C[Secondary 2]
    S1C --> SUM[Differential Subtractor, Series Opposition]
    S2C --> SUM
    SUM --> PSD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator with Reference from AC Oscillator]
    PSD --> LPF[Low Pass Filter, Removes Carrier]
    LPF --> AMP[DC Amplifier Stage]
    AMP --> OUT[DC Output, 0 to 10 V, Proportional to x]
```

## 4.3 LVDT Operating State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NullPosition: Core at Center
    NullPosition: Null Position
    NullPosition: Eout approx 0 V
    NullPosition --> LeftDisplacement: Core moves Left
    NullPosition --> RightDisplacement: Core moves Right
    LeftDisplacement: Core in S1 Region
    LeftDisplacement: Eout In phase with Vp
    LeftDisplacement: Positive DC after demodulation
    RightDisplacement: Core in S2 Region
    RightDisplacement: Eout Antiphase with Vp
    RightDisplacement: Negative DC after demodulation
    LeftDisplacement --> NullPosition: Core returns to center
    RightDisplacement --> NullPosition: Core returns to center
    LeftDisplacement --> LeftSaturation: x exceeds linear range
    LeftSaturation: Nonlinear Region
    LeftSaturation: Eout approaches plateau
    RightDisplacement --> RightSaturation: x exceeds linear range
    RightSaturation: Nonlinear Region
    RightSaturation: Eout approaches plateau
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Signal Conditioning Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MechanicalInput
        MI[Mechanical Displacement x]
    end
    subgraph TransductionCore
        LVDT[LVDT Sensor Head]
    end
    subgraph AnalogSignalChain
        OSC[Stable AC Oscillator, 5 V RMS, 5 kHz]
        OSC -- Excitation --> LVDT
        LVDT -- Differential AC Output --> IA[Instrumentation Preamplifier]
        IA --> PSD[Phase Sensitive Detector, AD630 type]
        OSC -- Reference Signal --> PSD
        PSD --> LPF[Active Low Pass Filter, fc 100 Hz]
        LPF --> GAIN[DC Gain Stage, 0 to 10 V]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage
        GAIN --> DSP[Microcontroller ADC / DAQ]
        GAIN --> DISP[Analog Meter or Digital Display]
        GAIN --> CTRL[Feedback Controller in Closed Loop System]
    end
    MI --> LVDT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this schematic is important for KTU exams:** The block diagram above is the *de facto* standard answer layout for any KTU question of the form *"With a neat block diagram, explain the signal conditioning electronics used with an LVDT."* Students should memorize the **four mandatory blocks**: AC Oscillator → LVDT → Phase-Sensitive Demodulator → DC Output, with the oscillator's reference signal feeding both the LVDT primary and the PSD — this dual-path connection is the **key exam-scorable detail**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define an LVDT. State any two advantages of LVDT over other displacement transducers.**

**Model Answer:**
An LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer) is an electromechanical transducer that converts linear displacement into a proportional differential AC voltage, using the principle of mutual inductance variation between a primary winding and two symmetrically placed, series-opposition-connected secondary windings, mediated by the axial position of a movable soft-iron core.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Infinite resolution:** Since there are no sliding electrical contacts and no friction, the resolution is limited only by the readout electronics, making it effectively infinite.
2. **Frictionless, contactless operation:** The core is mechanically decoupled from the coil bobbin, ensuring no wear, no mechanical hysteresis, and extremely long operational life — ideal for **biomedical and biosensor applications** where sterility and longevity are critical.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Why is a phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD) required in an LVDT measurement system?**

**Model Answer:**
An LVDT produces an **AC differential output voltage** whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement but whose **phase** (relative to the primary excitation) carries the sign (direction) information. A simple RMS voltmeter can recover only the magnitude, losing directional information. A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** compares the LVDT's output against the **same AC reference signal** used to excite the primary. It produces a **DC output** that is positive when the LVDT output is in-phase with the reference (core in one direction) and negative when antiphase (core in the other direction). The PSD therefore converts the bipolar AC displacement signal into a signed DC voltage suitable for ADC, display, or control-loop processing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of an LVDT**. Discuss the role of the series-opposition connection of the secondary windings. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Primary winding (P):** Single coil at the axial center, energized by a stable AC source (typically 5 V RMS at 5 kHz).
- **Two secondary windings (S₁ and S₂):** Identically wound, placed symmetrically on either side of P, and connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- **Soft-iron core:** High-permeability ferromagnetic cylinder that slides axially inside the hollow bobbin, mechanically coupled to the measurand.
- **Magnetic shield:** Mumetal outer casing for stray-field rejection and flux return path.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

**Null Position:** The core is centered. The magnetic reluctance paths through S₁ and S₂ are equal, so the induced EMFs $E_{S1}$ and $E_{S2}$ are equal. Because of series-opposition wiring:

$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = 0$$

**Displacement toward S₁:** The core moves into the S₁ region, decreasing S₁'s reluctance and increasing S₂'s reluctance. Therefore $E_{S1}$ increases and $E_{S2}$ decreases, giving a net output $E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} > 0$ that is **in-phase** with the primary excitation $V_p$.

**Displacement toward S₂:** Symmetric to the above, giving $E_{out} < 0$ in **antiphase** with $V_p$.

**Role of series-opposition connection:**
The series-opposition wiring is what makes the device **differential**. Without it, the output would be the *sum* of the two EMFs, which would remain roughly constant regardless of core position, eliminating displacement sensitivity. Series opposition converts the two individual inductive signals into a single *difference* signal whose null is exactly at the center position, providing both **zero reference** and **directional sensitivity** in a single output node.

---

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the output voltage of an LVDT** in terms of core displacement, primary excitation voltage, and inductance parameters. An LVDT has $V_p = 6 \text{ V RMS}$, $L_p = 12 \text{ mH}$, $M_0 = 4 \text{ mH}$, and $L_s = 24 \text{ mm}$. Find the sensitivity and the RMS output for a core displacement of $3 \text{ mm}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the core move by displacement $x$ from null. The mutual inductances of the two secondaries are:

$$M_1 = M_0 \cdot \frac{L_{ov,0} - x}{L_s}, \quad M_2 = M_0 \cdot \frac{L_{ov,0} + x}{L_s}$$

The induced EMFs in the secondaries (with primary current $I_p = V_p / \omega L_p$):

$$E_{S1} = j\omega M_1 I_p, \quad E_{S2} = j\omega M_2 I_p$$

The differential output (in series opposition):

$$E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2} = j\omega (M_1 - M_2) I_p$$

Substituting and simplifying (as shown in Section 3.1):

$$E_{out} = -\frac{2M_0 \cdot V_p}{L_p \cdot L_s} \cdot x$$

The **sensitivity** is the magnitude of the proportionality constant:

$$k_s = \frac{2M_0 \cdot V_p}{L_p \cdot L_s}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $V_p = 6$ V RMS, $L_p = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $M_0 = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ H, $L_s = 24 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $x = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

Sensitivity:

$$
k_s = \frac{2 \times 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 6}{12 \times 10^{-3} \times 24 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{48 \times 10^{-3}}{288 \times 10^{-6}} = 166.67 \text{ V/m}
$$

$$\boxed{k_s \approx 0.1667 \text{ V/mm}}$$

RMS Output Voltage:

$$
E_{out, RMS} = k_s \cdot \vert x \vert = 166.67 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 0.5 \text{ V RMS}
$$

$$\boxed{E_{out, RMS} = 0.5 \text{ V}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 - Alternative) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **complete signal conditioning block diagram** for an LVDT, including the role of the AC oscillator, phase-sensitive demodulator, and low-pass filter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The LVDT itself produces only a raw, bipolar AC differential voltage. To obtain a usable, signed DC signal, a complete signal conditioning chain is required. The block diagram consists of **four essential stages** plus an excitation source:

1. **AC Excitation Oscillator (1 mark):** Generates a stable, low-distortion sine wave — typically **5 to 10 V RMS** at a fixed frequency between **1 kHz and 10 kHz**. This signal is fed to:
   - The **primary winding** of the LVDT (to drive the magnetic circuit).
   - The **reference input** of the phase-sensitive demodulator (so it can detect phase relationships).

2. **LVDT Sensor Head (1 mark):** Produces a differential AC output $E_{out} = k_s \cdot x$, whose magnitude is proportional to displacement and whose phase indicates direction.

3. **Phase-Sensitive Demodulator (PSD) (2 marks):** A multiplier-type circuit (often based on the **AD630** IC) that multiplies the LVDT output by the reference AC signal. When the two are in phase, the result is a positive DC; when antiphase, a negative DC. The PSD is the **direction-recovering element** of the system.

4. **Low-Pass Filter (1 mark):** Removes the **2ω carrier ripple** generated by the PSD multiplication process, leaving only the DC component proportional to displacement. A typical cutoff is around **100 Hz** for a 5 kHz carrier.

5. **DC Amplifier / Output Stage (1 mark):** Scales the DC output to standard instrumentation ranges (0 to 10 V or 4 to 20 mA).

6. **Display / DAQ (1 mark):** A digital panel meter, oscilloscope, or microcontroller ADC for readout and storage.

This architecture is widely used in **closed-loop control systems** (e.g., servo-hydraulic actuator position control in biosensor calibration rigs).

---

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and key biosensor applications** of LVDTs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
- **Infinite theoretical resolution** — limited only by the readout ADC's bit depth.
- **Frictionless, contactless operation** — no mechanical wear, no hysteresis, long life.
- **High linearity** — 0.1% to 0.5% of full scale over the rated range.
- **Excellent sensitivity** — sub-micron displacements resolvable with appropriate electronics.
- **Bidirectional output** — single output node encodes both magnitude and sign of displacement.
- **Robust against environmental noise** — the differential topology rejects common-mode interference.
- **Bidirectional in operation and intrinsically safe** — operates at low AC voltages, suitable for biomedical environments.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Requires AC excitation** and complex signal conditioning (cannot be used in pure DC circuits).
- **Limited dynamic response** — the operating bandwidth is restricted by the excitation frequency (carrier-frequency limitation).
- **Susceptible to stray magnetic fields** if not properly shielded.
- **Core mass limits high-frequency mechanical response** — inertia becomes a factor at very high accelerations.
- **Temperature sensitivity** — coil resistance and magnetic permeability drift with temperature, requiring compensation for precision applications.

**Key Biosensor & Biomedical Applications (2 marks):**
- **Micropositioning in patch-clamp and micropipette-based biosensors** — LVDTs provide sub-micron feedback for cell-probe alignment.
- **Force-displacement characterization in mechanical testing of biomaterials** (bone, tissue, hydrogel scaffolds).
- **Implantable prosthetic limb joint angle sensors** — long life and frictionless operation suit in-vivo deployment.
- **Bioreactor piston-position feedback** for controlled media delivery in cell-culture systems.
- **Respiration and cardiac-displacement sensing** in physiological monitoring.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in LVDT Questions (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the series-opposition connection:** Many students describe S₁ and S₂ as separate coils but forget to state that they are wired in **series opposition** ($E_{out} = E_{S1} - E_{S2}$). Without this, the device is not a *differential* transformer — and the answer is **conceptually wrong**. Examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing phase with magnitude:** The output is bipolar AC. Saying "the output is positive when the core moves right" is **incorrect**; the correct phrasing is "the output is **in-phase** (or antiphase) with the primary excitation, which after PSD demodulation gives a positive (or negative) DC value." Examiners value this nuance.
>
> 3. **Skipping the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator:** Any 7-mark "signal conditioning" question without a PSD block diagram is **incomplete** and loses **at least 2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** $L_s$ and $x$ must be in the **same length unit** (typically meters) when substituted into the sensitivity formula. A common error is mixing mm and m — yielding an answer off by a factor of 1000. Always do a **unit sanity check**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the negative sign in the derivation:** The output voltage $E_{out} = -\frac{2M_0 V_p}{L_p L_s} x$ carries a sign. Examiners look for this sign and its physical interpretation (direction).
>
> 6. **Writing "LVDT is a digital transducer":** LVDT is **analog**. Writing this is a guaranteed **1-mark loss** if the question asks for a definition.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer — converts **linear displacement** → **differential AC voltage**.
- It is an **active transducer** — needs **external AC excitation** (typically 1 kHz to 25 kHz, 1 V to 24 V RMS).
- **Three windings:** one primary (P), two identical secondaries (S₁, S₂) connected in **series opposition**.
- **One movable core** of high-permeability ferromagnetic material.
- **Null position:** core centered → $E_{out} = 0$.
- **Displacement encodes in BOTH amplitude and phase** of $E_{out}$.
- **Phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD) is mandatory** for full bipolar output recovery.
- **Key formula:** $E_{out} = \pm k_s \cdot x$ where $k_s = 2M_0 V_p / (L_p L_s)$.
- **Sensitivity units:** V/m or V/mm — typically 0.1 V/mm to 10 V/mm.
- **Linearity:** 0.1% to 0.5% of full scale over rated range.
- **Resolution:** practically **infinite** (no friction, no contacts).
- **Range:** ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm (industrial typical: ±25 mm).
- **Advantages:** frictionless, infinite resolution, long life, bidirectional, robust.
- **Limitations:** needs AC excitation + PSD; core inertia limits high-frequency response; stray field sensitivity.
- **Biosensor applications:** micropositioning, bioreactor feedback, prosthetics, biomechanical testing, physiological displacement sensing.
- **Exam mantra:** *"Primary centered, secondaries symmetric, series opposition, core couples, PSD decodes."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Sensors - Optical Encoders, Tacho Generator, Hall Effect Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Velocity Sensors - Optical Encoders, Tacho Generator, Hall Effect Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Velocity Sensor?

A **velocity sensor** is a transducer that converts the rate of change of position (linear or angular) of a target into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, frequency, or pulse train). In the context of KTU OEBRT834 (Biosensors and Transducers), velocity sensors form a critical subclass of motion transducers used in biomedical instrumentation, robotics, CNC machines, and process automation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A velocity sensor is a device that measures the time rate of displacement of a body, producing an output directly proportional to angular speed $\omega$ (rad/s) or linear speed $v$ (m/s).

---

## 1.2 Optical Encoders

### Formal Definition
An **optical encoder** is a non-contact, electro-mechanical device that converts the angular or linear position of a shaft into a digital electrical signal using the **photoelectric effect** and a patterned code wheel (or linear strip).

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **stenographer's typewriter ribbon with holes punched in it**. As the ribbon slides past a light source, each hole lets a flash of light through to a photocell, producing a "click-click" electrical pulse train. Count the clicks per second — and you have speed. The same idea, but in a circular disk form, gives angular speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Optical encoders are the **gold standard** for digital speed/position feedback in servo systems. Two main families exist:
> 1. **Incremental Encoder** — produces a pulse train; relative motion only.
> 2. **Absolute Encoder** — produces a unique binary Gray code word per angular position; retains position after power loss.

---

## 1.3 Tacho Generator (Tachogenerator)

### Formal Definition
A **tacho generator** is an **electromechanical transducer** (essentially a small DC or AC generator) whose **output voltage is directly proportional to the angular velocity** of its rotor shaft.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the **bicycle dynamo** your grandfather used. Pedal faster → brighter headlamp. A tacho generator is the **precision-engineered, calibrated, industrial cousin** of that dynamo. Instead of illuminating a bulb, it produces a clean DC/AC voltage read directly as a speed value on a voltmeter or ADC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Tacho generators are **analog velocity sensors** (versus the digital output of encoders). They dominate in:
> - **Analog servo loops** (legacy hydraulic servos, analog CNC drives)
> - **Tape recorders / turntables** (speed stabilization via back-EMF feedback)

---

## 1.4 Hall Effect Sensor

### Formal Definition
A **Hall Effect sensor** is a solid-state transducer that exploits the **Hall Effect** (discovered by Edwin Hall in 1879): when a current-carrying conductor/semiconductor is placed in a transverse magnetic field, a measurable voltage (the **Hall voltage**, $V_H$) develops perpendicular to both the current and the field.

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture **salmon swimming upstream in a river**. The fish are the charge carriers (electrons/holes), the river flow is the current $I$. Now tilt the river sideways using a magnetic field $B$. The salmon pile up on one bank (negative side) while the opposite bank is depleted. The height difference of the water between the two banks is the **Hall voltage** $V_H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Biosensors/Transducers:** A small permanent magnet mounted on a rotating shaft (e.g., a motor's rotor, a flow meter's impeller) produces a sinusoidally-varying $B$ field at the Hall sensor. Each rotation yields one pulse — perfect for **contactless, wear-free speed sensing**, even in sterile or wet environments (e.g., peristaltic pumps, centrifuges).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core physical constants used in this module:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ (not directly used, listed for completeness)
> - Electron charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
> - Vacuum permeability: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
> - Standard encoder resolution: $500, 1000, 1024, 2048, 5000 \text{ PPR}$ (pulses per revolution)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Encoder — Operating Principle

### Components
1. **Light Source** — typically an **LED** (visible red 660 nm or infrared 880 nm) for long life.
2. **Code Wheel / Strip** — a glass/metal disk with precisely etched **opaque and transparent slots**. For absolute encoders, the slots follow a **Gray code** pattern; for incremental, a single track of equally spaced slots.
3. **Photodetector** — a **photodiode** or **phototransistor** array that converts light pulses into electrical pulses.
4. **Signal Conditioning Circuitry** — Schmitt triggers, comparators, line drivers (RS-422, TTL).

### Working Logic
- LED emits **continuous light** toward the code wheel.
- As the wheel rotates, slots alternately **transmit** and **block** light.
- Photodetector sees a **square-wave light intensity profile** → converts to a **pulse train**.
- A second photodetector offset by **90° (one-quarter slot width)** produces the **quadrature channel B** (Channel A leads B for clockwise rotation; B leads A for counter-clockwise).
- An optional **index (Z) channel** produces **one pulse per revolution** for homing.

### Why Incremental vs Absolute?

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (relative) | Unique binary word per position |
| Retains position on power-off | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Resolution (typical) | Up to **50,000 PPR** | Up to **25 bits** (33 million positions) |
| Primary use | Speed + relative position | True multi-turn position |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If a question asks "Why is Gray code used in absolute encoders instead of binary?" — answer: **To prevent multi-bit errors during transition**, since only one bit changes between adjacent positions in Gray code, eliminating the ambiguity of $000 \to 111$ simultaneous transitions.

### Velocity Calculation from Encoder Pulses
If a counter records $N$ pulses in time $t$ from an encoder with $PPR$ pulses per revolution, the shaft speed is:

$$N_{rev} = \frac{N}{PPR}$$

$$n = \frac{N}{PPR \cdot t} \quad \text{[rev/s]}$$

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot N}{PPR \cdot t} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

---

## 2.2 Tacho Generator — Operating Principle

### DC Tacho Generator
A **DC tacho generator** is essentially a small **permanent-magnet DC generator** with a linear field system. The rotor (armature) is driven by the shaft whose speed is to be measured.

**Key Features:**
- Output voltage is **DC**, polarity reverses with direction of rotation.
- **Ripple-free** in ideal construction; in practice, commutator ripple < 2–3%.
- The output is **inherently linear** with speed.

### AC Tacho Generator (Asynchronous / Induction Type)
- Has a **stator winding** excited by a **fixed-frequency AC source** (e.g., 400 Hz).
- Rotor is a thin **drag-cup aluminum cylinder** (no windings, no slip rings).
- The rotating drag-cup induces an EMF in the output winding whose **amplitude is proportional to rotor speed**.
- The **frequency of the output** = frequency of the excitation (constant) — only amplitude carries the speed information.
- AC tachos are preferred where **brushless, maintenance-free** operation is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** A DC tacho is essentially a **DC motor run in reverse** — the EMF constant $K_E$ and torque constant $K_T$ are the same (in SI units): $K_E = K_T$. The generated EMF directly equals back-EMF.

---

## 2.3 Hall Effect Sensor — Operating Principle

### Physics Recap
1. A **current $I$** is forced through a thin semiconductor slab (typically GaAs, InSb, or silicon).
2. A **magnetic field $B$** is applied perpendicular to the current direction.
3. The **Lorentz force** $F = q(\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B})$ deflects charge carriers sideways.
4. Charge accumulation on the sides creates a transverse **Hall voltage $V_H$**.
5. At equilibrium, the **electric force** from charge separation balances the magnetic force, giving a steady-state $V_H$.

### Derivation Setup
Consider a semiconductor slab of thickness $t$ (in the direction of $B$) and width $w$. Current $I$ flows along length $L$, magnetic field $B$ along the $t$-axis, and $V_H$ is measured across the width $w$.

> [!NOTE]
> The detailed step-by-step derivation is in **SECTION 3** below.

### Two Main Operating Modes
1. **Bipolar (Latching) Hall Sensor:** Output switches HIGH/LOW as $B$ crosses a threshold. Used for **digital speed sensing** (each tooth of a gear wheel = one pulse).
2. **Linear Hall Sensor:** Output is a **continuous analog voltage** proportional to $B$. Used where the magnetic field varies smoothly (e.g., proximity, current sensing).

---

## 2.4 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every KTU exam cycle for this module.**

| # | Sensor / Parameter | Formula | Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Incremental encoder angular speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{PPR \cdot t}$ | $N$ = pulse count, $PPR$ = pulses/rev, $t$ = time | rad/s |
| 2 | Encoder linear speed (wheel + encoder) | $v = \dfrac{2\pi r \cdot N}{PPR \cdot t}$ | $r$ = wheel radius | m/s |
| 3 | DC tacho generated EMF | $E_g = \dfrac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A}$ | $P$ = poles, $\Phi$ = flux/pole, $Z$ = conductors, $N$ = rpm, $A$ = parallel paths | V |
| 4 | DC tacho EMF constant | $E_g = K_E \cdot \omega$ | $K_E$ = back-EMF constant | V·s/rad |
| 5 | AC tacho output voltage | $V_{out} = K_{ac} \cdot N$ | $K_{ac}$ = AC tacho constant | V/(rev/min) |
| 6 | Hall voltage (basic form) | $V_H = \dfrac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$ | $R_H$ = Hall coefficient, $t$ = slab thickness | V |
| 7 | Hall coefficient | $R_H = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot q}$ | $n$ = carrier density, $q$ = charge | m³/C |
| 8 | Carrier drift velocity | $v_d = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot q \cdot A}$ | $A$ = cross-section area ($w \cdot t$) | m/s |
| 9 | Hall sensitivity | $K_H = \dfrac{R_H}{t}$ | — | V/(A·T) |
| 10 | Hall mobility | $\mu_H = \sigma \cdot \vert R_H \vert$ | $\sigma$ = conductivity | m²/(V·s) |
| 11 | Tooth-wheel frequency from Hall sensor | $f = \dfrac{N_{teeth} \cdot n}{60}$ | $N_{teeth}$ = teeth count, $n$ = shaft rpm | Hz |
| 12 | Tooth-wheel angular speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi f}{N_{teeth}}$ | — | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **Optical encoders** dominate **digital CNC, robotics, BLDC motor commutation** (incremental with index) and **medical imaging gantries** (absolute for safety-critical positioning of CT/linear accelerator tables).
> - **Tacho generators** remain in **analog servo loops**, **ship engine governors**, and **tape/film transport speed control**.
> - **Hall effect sensors** are the workhorse of **brushless DC motor commutation** (3 sensors = 6-step trapezoidal commutation), **automotive crankshaft/camshaft position sensing**, and **biomedical peristaltic pump speed feedback** (sealed, sterile, contactless).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Incremental Encoder Angular Speed

**Given:** An incremental optical encoder has $PPR$ pulses per revolution. A counter measures $N$ pulses in time interval $t$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Fraction of revolution completed:**
One full revolution produces $PPR$ pulses. Therefore, $N$ pulses correspond to:

$$N_{rev} = \frac{N}{PPR} \quad \text{revolutions}$$

*Logic:* Direct proportion — if $PPR$ pulses = 1 revolution, then $N$ pulses = $N/PPR$ revolutions.

**Step 2 — Revolutions per second:**
Speed in revolutions per second (frequency of rotation) is:

$$f_{rot} = \frac{N_{rev}}{t} = \frac{N}{PPR \cdot t} \quad \text{[rev/s]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to angular velocity:**
One revolution = $2\pi$ radians, therefore:

$$\omega = 2\pi \cdot f_{rot} = \frac{2\pi N}{PPR \cdot t} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to RPM (if needed):**

$$n_{rpm} = f_{rot} \times 60 = \frac{60 N}{PPR \cdot t} \quad \text{[rev/min]}$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
An incremental encoder with 1024 PPR gives 4096 pulses in 0.5 s. Find (a) shaft speed in rpm, (b) angular speed in rad/s, (c) linear speed of a wheel of radius 5 cm mounted on the shaft.

(a) Substituting $N = 4096$, $PPR = 1024$, $t = 0.5$:

$$n = \frac{60 \times 4096}{1024 \times 0.5} = \frac{245760}{512} = 480 \text{ rpm}$$

(b) Angular speed:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 480}{60} = 16\pi \approx 50.27 \text{ rad/s}$$

(c) Linear speed with $r = 0.05$ m:

$$v = \omega \cdot r = 50.27 \times 0.05 = 2.51 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Tacho Generator EMF

**Setup:** A DC tacho generator has $P$ poles, total armature conductors $Z$, parallel paths $A$, flux per pole $\Phi$ (Wb), running at speed $N$ (rpm).

**Step 1 — Flux cut per conductor per revolution:**
Each conductor sweeps past $P$ poles per revolution, so total flux cut per conductor per revolution is:

$$\Delta\Phi_{cond} = P \cdot \Phi \quad \text{[Wb]}$$

**Step 2 — Flux cut per conductor per second:**
Revolutions per second = $N/60$. Therefore flux cut per second per conductor is:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} = P \cdot \Phi \cdot \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[Wb/s]}$$

**Step 3 — EMF per conductor (Faraday's law):**
EMF per conductor equals rate of change of flux linkage:

$$e_{cond} = \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{[V]}$$

**Step 4 — Total armature EMF (series conductors per path = Z/A):**
The $Z$ conductors are distributed in $A$ parallel paths. Conductors in **series** per path = $Z/A$. These add in series along one path:

$$E_g = \frac{Z}{A} \cdot e_{cond} = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A} \quad \text{[V]}$$

**Step 5 — Express in angular speed form:**
Since $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$, we have $N/60 = \omega / (2\pi)$, thus:

$$E_g = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi \cdot A} \cdot \omega = K_E \cdot \omega$$

where the **back-EMF constant** is:

$$K_E = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2\pi \cdot A} \quad \text{[V·s/rad]}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**
A DC tacho has $P = 4$, $\Phi = 0.005$ Wb, $Z = 500$, $A = 2$ (wave-wound), $N = 1500$ rpm. Find $E_g$.

Substituting:

$$E_g = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 2} = \frac{15000}{120} = 125 \text{ V}$$

(Continued derivation context — total generated EMF = 125 V at 1500 rpm, giving tacho constant of $5$ V per 1000 rpm.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall Voltage ($V_H$)

**Setup:** A semiconductor slab of thickness $t$ (in direction of $\vec{B}$) and width $w$ carries a current $I$ along its length $L$.

**Step 1 — Current density and drift velocity:**
Current density $J$ = current per unit cross-sectional area. With cross-section $A = w \cdot t$:

$$J = \frac{I}{w \cdot t} \quad \text{[A/m²]}$$

The drift velocity of charge carriers is:

$$v_d = \frac{J}{n \cdot q} = \frac{I}{n \cdot q \cdot w \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Lorentz force per carrier:**
Each carrier (charge $q$, drift velocity $v_d$) in field $B$ experiences:

$$\vec{F}_{mag} = q \cdot v_d \cdot B$$

**Step 3 — Charge accumulation and Hall electric field:**
Charges accumulate on the side faces of the slab until the electric field $E_H$ they create exerts a balancing force $q E_H$ on incoming carriers:

$$q E_H = q v_d B \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_d B$$

**Step 4 — Hall voltage:**
The Hall voltage is the field times the width $w$ across which it appears:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d \cdot B \cdot w$$

**Step 5 — Substitute drift velocity:**

$$V_H = \frac{I \cdot B \cdot w}{n \cdot q \cdot w \cdot t} = \frac{I \cdot B}{n \cdot q \cdot t}$$

**Step 6 — Define Hall coefficient:**

$$R_H = \frac{1}{n \cdot q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

**Step 7 — Hall mobility relationship:**
Since conductivity $\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot \mu$ (where $\mu$ is mobility):

$$\mu_H = \sigma \cdot R_H = \sigma \cdot \frac{1}{n q} = \frac{\mu n q}{n q} = \mu \quad \text{(identical in sign/magnitude)}$$

For an n-type semiconductor with electron concentration $n$:

$$R_H = -\frac{1}{n \cdot e} \quad \text{(negative)}$$

For a p-type with hole concentration $p$:

$$R_H = +\frac{1}{p \cdot e} \quad \text{(positive)}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Example:**
A GaAs Hall sensor ($n = 1.5 \times 10^{21}$ /m³, $t = 0.1$ mm = $10^{-4}$ m) carries $I = 10$ mA in a field $B = 0.1$ T. Find $V_H$.

Compute Hall coefficient:

$$R_H = \frac{1}{n \cdot e} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{21} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{1}{240.3} \approx 4.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m³/C}$$

Then:

$$V_H = \frac{4.16 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.010 \times 0.1}{10^{-4}} = \frac{4.16 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} = 0.0416 \text{ V} = 41.6 \text{ mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on sign convention:** The Hall voltage sign reveals the **majority carrier type** — negative for electrons (n-type), positive for holes (p-type). This is exploited in semiconductor characterization (van der Pauw method) and forms the basis of **semiconductor BioSensors** that detect charged biomolecules via surface potential changes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Velocity Sensors

| Parameter | Optical Encoder | Tacho Generator | Hall Effect Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Digital (pulses) | Analog (DC/AC voltage) | Digital or Analog |
| Contact type | Non-contact (light) | Contact (brushes, sometimes brushless) | Non-contact (magnetic) |
| Range of speed | 0 – 100,000 rpm | 0 – 10,000 rpm (DC), 0 – 5,000 rpm (AC) | 0 – 100,000 rpm |
| Resolution / accuracy | Very high (up to 26 bits) | Moderate (1–2 % linearity error) | Moderate (limited by magnet/sensor geometry) |
| Sensitivity to environment | Dust, oil, vibration | Brush wear, commutator wear | Magnetic interference from nearby motors |
| Bidirectional sensing | Yes (via quadrature) | Yes (DC polarity / AC phase) | Yes (bipolar/latching variant) |
| Typical cost | Medium–High | Low–Medium | Low (few dollars) |
| Life expectancy | Very long (no contact) | Limited by brushes (or infinite for AC drag-cup) | Very long (solid-state) |
| Power supply needed | +5 V / +24 V DC | Self-powered (PM) or excitation AC | +3.3 V / +5 V DC + magnet |
| KTU typical application | CNC, robotics, BLDC commutation | Analog servo, governors, tape recorders | BLDC commutation, flow meters, automotive ABS |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Optical Encoder — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LED LIGHT SOURCE] --> B[COLIMATING LENS]
    B --> C[CODE WHEEL WITH SLOTS]
    D[SHAFT INPUT MOTION] --> C
    C --> E[PHOTODETECTOR A]
    C --> F[PHOTODETECTOR B OFFSET 90 DEG]
    C --> G[INDEX CHANNEL Z ONE PULSE PER REV]
    E --> H[SIGNAL CONDITIONING AMPLIFIER]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[QUADRATURE DECODER LOGIC]
    I --> J[COUNTER TIMER MODULE]
    J --> K[VELOCITY OUTPUT OMEGA RAD PER S]
    I --> L[DIRECTION OUTPUT CW OR CCW]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The shaft (D) rotates the code wheel (C). Light from the LED (A) is collimated (B), passes through slots, and is detected by three photodetectors (E, F, G). Channel A leads B by 90° for clockwise rotation; the index channel Z gives a once-per-revolution homing pulse. Signal conditioning (H) cleans up the waveforms, the decoder (I) resolves direction, and the counter/timer (J) computes velocity (K) and direction (L).

---

## 4.2 DC Tacho Generator — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROTOR_ASSEMBLY
        P[PRIME MOVER SHAFT] --> R[ARMATURE CONDUCTORS ON LAMINATED CORE]
    end
    subgraph STATOR_ASSEMBLY
        M[PERMANENT MAGNET POLE PIECES] --> F[MAGNETIC FLUX PHI]
    end
    F --> R
    R --> C[COMMUTATOR SEGMENTS]
    C --> BR[BRUSH ASSEMBLY]
    BR --> SC[OUTPUT TERMINALS V OUT]
    SC --> VVM[VOLTMETER OR ADC]
    VVM --> DSP[MICROCONTROLLER OR ANALOG CONTROLLER]
    DSP --> ECHO[VELOCITY DISPLAY N IN RPM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The prime mover (P) drives the armature (R) through the field flux $\Phi$ from the PM poles (M). Generated EMF is collected at the commutator (C) via brushes (BR), appearing at the output terminals (SC). A voltmeter (VVM) reads the DC voltage directly proportional to speed, which the controller (DSP) converts to RPM display (ECHO).

---

## 4.3 Hall Effect Sensor — Block Architecture and Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HALL_IC_PACKAGE
        VS[DC SUPPLY 5V] --> HC[HALL ELEMENT SEMICONDUCTOR]
        GND[GROUND 0V] --> HC
        AMP[DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER] --> HC
        SCH[SCHMITT TRIGGER] --> AMP
        OUT[OPEN COLLECTOR OR PUSH PULL OUTPUT] --> SCH
    end
    EXT[EXTERNAL MAGNETIC FIELD B] --> HC
    ROT[ROTATING TOOTHED IRON WHEEL] --> EXT
    SHAFT[INPUT SHAFT] --> ROT
    OUT --> P1[PULSE TRAIN F HZ]
    P1 --> CTR[COUNTER OR FREQ TO VOLT CONVERTER]
    CTR --> VEL[VELOCITY OUTPUT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Hall element (HC) is biased by supply (VS) and ground (GND). An external magnetic field (EXT), modulated by a toothed wheel (ROT) on the input shaft (SHAFT), causes the Hall voltage to pulse. The internal amplifier (AMP) and Schmitt trigger (SCH) clean the signal, producing a digital pulse train (P1) at the output (OUT). A counter or F-to-V converter (CTR) produces the final velocity (VEL).

---

## 4.4 Combined Decision Flowchart — Which Velocity Sensor to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NEED VELOCITY MEASUREMENT] --> Q1{ANALOG OR DIGITAL OUTPUT}
    Q1 --> ANALOG[TACHO GENERATOR]
    Q1 --> DIGITAL[ENCODER OR HALL]
    ANALOG --> Q2{HIGH ACCURACY NEEDED}
    Q2 --> YES1[USE DC TACHO WITH HIGH MAGNETIC FLUX]
    Q2 --> NO1[USE AC DRAG CUP TACHO BRUSHLESS]
    DIGITAL --> Q3{HIGHEST RESOLUTION}
    Q3 --> YES2[USE OPTICAL ENCODER UP TO 26 BITS]
    Q3 --> NO2[USE HALL EFFECT SENSOR LOW COST]
    YES2 --> ENV1[ENVIRONMENT CLEAN AND DRY]
    NO2 --> ENV2[ENVIRONMENT HARSH WET DUSTY]
    ENV1 --> OPT[OPTICAL ENCODER CHOSEN]
    ENV2 --> HAL[HALL SENSOR CHOSEN CONTACTLESS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The flowchart guides a designer from the requirement (start) through the decision branches: output type → accuracy needs → environment constraints → final sensor choice. This is the **same decision tree** examiners expect students to articulate in design-oriented KTU questions.

---
