<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **For Optical Encoders:** Students frequently confuse **PPR (pulses per revolution)** with **CPR (counts per revolution, typically 4×PPR in quadrature)**. Always state explicitly which you are using.
> - **For Tacho Generators:** Many students forget the **A (parallel paths)** in the EMF formula and write $E = P\Phi Z N / 60$ only — this is wrong for lap-wound (A = P) and wave-wound (A = 2) machines.
> - **For Hall Effect:** The **sign of $R_H$** depends on carrier type. Dropping the sign = 0.5 to 1 mark loss per KTU valuation key.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Define an optical encoder. Differentiate between incremental and absolute encoders.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **optical encoder** is a non-contact, electro-optical transducer that converts the angular (or linear) position of a shaft into a digital electrical signal by sensing light passing through a slotted code wheel via a photodetector.

| Aspect | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulse train (relative) | Unique binary Gray-code word per position |
| Power-off memory | None — must re-home | Retains position |
| Wiring | 2–4 wires (A, B, Z) | Multiple wires (e.g., 10 wires for 10-bit) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

Incremental encoders are used where the **relative speed/position** is sufficient; absolute encoders are used in **safety-critical applications** (e.g., surgical robot joints, telescope mounts) where the position must be known at all times. *(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark differentiation + 1 mark application.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] — State the Hall Effect. Derive the expression for Hall voltage.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The **Hall Effect** is the phenomenon of generation of a transverse EMF (Hall voltage, $V_H$) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when it is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of current flow.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
Let a semiconductor slab of thickness $t$ carry current $I$ and be placed in magnetic field $B$ (perpendicular to $I$). The Lorentz force deflects carriers sideways, creating an electric field $E_H$ across the width $w$. At equilibrium, $qE_H = qv_dB$, giving $E_H = v_d B$. Multiplying by $w$ and substituting $v_d = I/(nq\cdot w\cdot t)$:

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = \frac{B \cdot I}{n \cdot q \cdot t} = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where $R_H = 1/(nq)$ is the **Hall coefficient**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Optical Encoders + Tacho Generator (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, OEBRT834]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **incremental optical encoder**. Discuss how quadrature signals enable direction sensing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-pole DC tacho generator has a wave-wound armature with 500 conductors. The flux per pole is 0.005 Wb. The machine runs at 1500 rpm. Calculate:
   (i) The generated EMF $E_g$
   (ii) The tacho sensitivity in V per 1000 rpm
   (iii) The output voltage when the speed drops to 750 rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **LED light source** with collimating lens — produces a parallel beam of light.
2. **Code wheel** — a glass or metal disk with precisely etched radial slots (typically 100 to 5000 slots). Mounted on the shaft.
3. **Photodetectors** — usually two photodiodes (Channel A and Channel B) and an optional index photodetector (Channel Z).
4. **Signal conditioning board** — amplifiers, Schmitt triggers, and output line drivers.
5. **Housing and bearings** — sealed to IP65 for industrial use.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The LED continuously illuminates one side of the code wheel.
- As the shaft rotates, the slotted disk alternately **transmits and blocks** the light.
- Channel A photodetector produces a **square-wave pulse train** with frequency proportional to speed.
- Channel B photodetector is mounted **90° (one-quarter slot) offset** from Channel A.
- If Channel A's rising edge occurs **before** Channel B's → rotation is **clockwise**.
- If Channel B leads → rotation is **counter-clockwise**.
- This is the principle of **quadrature decoding** (×1, ×2, or ×4 edge resolution).

**Direction sensing explanation (1 mark):** By XOR-ing Channel A and Channel B, an up/down count direction can be derived. With ×4 decoding, the effective resolution = 4 × PPR.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $Z = 500$ conductors
- Wave-wound → $A = 2$ parallel paths
- $\Phi = 0.005$ Wb/pole
- $N = 1500$ rpm

**(i) Generated EMF (3 marks):**

$$E_g = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot N}{60 \cdot A} = \frac{4 \times 0.005 \times 500 \times 1500}{60 \times 2}$$

Step-by-step numerator: $4 \times 0.005 = 0.02$; $0.02 \times 500 = 10$; $10 \times 1500 = 15000$.

Denominator: $60 \times 2 = 120$.

$$E_g = \frac{15000}{120} = 125 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Tacho sensitivity (2 marks):**
Since $E_g \propto N$, sensitivity is:

$$K = \frac{E_g}{N} = \frac{125}{1500} = 0.0833 \text{ V/rpm} = 83.3 \text{ V per 1000 rpm}$$

**(iii) Output at 750 rpm (2 marks):**
Using linear proportionality:

$$E_{750} = K \times 750 = 0.0833 \times 750 = 62.5 \text{ V}$$

(Alternatively, $E_{750} = 125 \times 750/1500 = 62.5$ V.)

---

### ⭐ Question B — Hall Effect Sensor + Tooth-Wheel Speed Measurement (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, OEBRT834]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hall Effect** with a labeled diagram. Derive the expression for **Hall voltage** and **Hall coefficient**. Why is the Hall coefficient negative for n-type semiconductors? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Hall effect speed sensor** is used with a toothed iron wheel having 60 teeth mounted on a motor shaft. The Hall sensor produces one pulse per tooth. If the pulse train frequency measured is 1200 Hz, calculate:
   (i) The shaft speed in rpm
   (ii) The shaft angular velocity in rad/s
   (iii) The linear speed at a point on the wheel rim of radius 8 cm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

**Hall Effect explanation (2 marks):**
The Hall Effect is the generation of a transverse electric field (and thus a measurable voltage $V_H$) in a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor placed in a perpendicular magnetic field. The phenomenon arises from the **Lorentz force** on moving charge carriers, which deflects them to one side of the conductor.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Rectangular semiconductor slab with $I$ along $+x$, $B$ along $+z$, $V_H$ measured along $y$, with arrows showing carrier deflection.

**Derivation of $V_H$ and $R_H$ (3 marks):**
(Refer to the full derivation in **SECTION 3.3** above.) Key result:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}, \quad R_H = \frac{1}{nq}$$

**Sign of $R_H$ for n-type (1 mark):**
In an n-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are **electrons** (charge $q = -e$). Therefore:

$$R_H = \frac{1}{n \cdot (-e)} = -\frac{1}{n e} \quad \text{(negative)}$$

The negative sign indicates that the Hall voltage polarity is opposite to that of a p-type material. This sign reversal is, in fact, the historical method by which scientists confirmed the existence of two carrier types in semiconductors.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N_{teeth} = 60$
- $f = 1200$ Hz (one pulse per tooth)

**(i) Shaft speed in rpm (3 marks):**
Each revolution produces $N_{teeth} = 60$ pulses. Therefore:

$$n = \frac{f \times 60}{N_{teeth}} = \frac{1200 \times 60}{60} = 1200 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Angular velocity in rad/s (2 marks):**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iii) Linear speed at rim, $r = 0.08$ m (2 marks):**

$$v = \omega \cdot r = 125.66 \times 0.08 = 10.05 \text{ m/s}$$

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Velocity Sensors**

### Optical Encoders
- ✅ Two main types: **Incremental** (pulses) and **Absolute** (Gray code).
- ✅ **Quadrature decoding** uses two photodetectors offset by **90°** to give direction.
- ✅ Speed formula: $\omega = 2\pi N / (PPR \cdot t)$.
- ✅ **Gray code** is used in absolute encoders to avoid multi-bit transition errors.
- ✅ **CPR (counts per revolution) = 4 × PPR** in ×4 quadrature decoding.

### Tacho Generator
- ✅ **DC tacho** EMF: $E_g = P \Phi Z N / (60 A)$. Wave-wound → $A = 2$; lap-wound → $A = P$.
- ✅ Output is **linearly proportional to speed** (key advantage).
- ✅ **AC tacho (drag-cup)** is brushless; output amplitude ∝ speed, frequency constant.
- ✅ Polarity of DC tacho output **reverses** with direction.
- ✅ Polarity reversal of voltage can be used to sense direction in analog loops.

### Hall Effect Sensor
- ✅ Discovered by **Edwin Hall (1879)**.
- ✅ Hall voltage: $V_H = R_H I B / t$.
- ✅ Hall coefficient: $R_H = 1/(nq)$ — **sign reveals carrier type** (n = negative, p = positive).
- ✅ **Latching (bipolar) Hall ICs** are used for digital tooth-wheel speed sensing.
- ✅ **Linear Hall ICs** are used for contactless position and current sensing.
- ✅ Hall mobility = $\sigma \cdot \vert R_H \vert$.

### Cross-Cutting KTU Concepts
- ✅ All three sensors appear in **BLDC motor commutation** (encoders or Hall ICs), **CNC machines** (encoders + tachos), and **biomedical pumps** (Hall).
- ✅ The **Lorentz force** underpins the Hall Effect; **Faraday's law** underpins the tacho; **photoelectric effect** underpins the optical encoder.
- ✅ For **biosensor extension**: a Hall sensor can detect magnetically-labeled biomolecules (magnetic bead immunoassays — MAGPIX, Luminex platforms).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Acceleration Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceleration Sensor (Accelerometer):** A transducer that converts mechanical acceleration — the rate of change of velocity with respect to time — into a measurable electrical output signal (voltage, current, frequency, or charge). It is one of the most fundamental **inertial sensors** in modern engineering, forming the backbone of motion-sensing systems.

Mathematically, acceleration is the **second time-derivative of displacement** and is a vector quantity possessing both magnitude and direction:

$$\vec{a} = \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} = \frac{d^{2}\vec{x}}{dt^{2}}$$

The SI unit of acceleration is **metres per second squared (m/s²)**, and the standard gravity reference is **g = 9.80665 m/s²** (often approximated as **9.81 m/s²** in engineering practice).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Marble in a Cup"

Imagine a **marble resting inside a flexible paper cup**. When you shake the cup:
- The cup moves abruptly, but the marble **lags behind** due to its inertia.
- The marble rolls, taps, or presses against the inner wall of the cup.
- That **contact force** is directly proportional to the acceleration experienced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** An accelerometer works exactly on this principle. A tiny "proof mass" (the marble) is suspended by a mechanical spring (the flexible cup). When the sensor accelerates, the proof mass displaces relative to the sensor frame, and this displacement — or the force needed to restore it — is converted into an electrical signal.

## 1.3 Classification of Accelerometers

Acceleration sensors are broadly classified based on the **transduction principle** used to convert mechanical motion into an electrical signal:

| Classification | Transduction Principle | Output Signal Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacitive** | Change in capacitance between moving plates | Voltage / Capacitance |
| **Piezoelectric** | Stress-induced charge generation in crystals | Charge / Voltage |
| **Piezoresistive** | Stress-induced resistance change | Resistance / Voltage (Wheatstone bridge) |
| **Thermal (Convection)** | Heated mass and temperature-sensing thermistors | Voltage |
| **Tunneling** | Quantum tunneling current variation | Current |
| **Hall-Effect** | Deflection of a magnet across a Hall sensor | Voltage |
| **Optical / Fiber-Optic** | Light intensity / wavelength modulation | Light intensity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For biosensor and transducer courses, the primary focus is on **Capacitive, Piezoelectric, and Piezoresistive accelerometers**, as these dominate MEMS-based biomedical instrumentation, automotive airbag systems, and wearable patient-monitoring devices.

## 1.4 Key Performance Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> Any accelerometer datasheet is interpreted through the following high-yield parameters:
> - **Range (g):** Maximum measurable acceleration (e.g., ±2 g, ±200 g).
> - **Sensitivity (mV/g or pC/g):** Output per unit acceleration.
> - **Bandwidth (Hz):** Frequency range of operation.
> - **Noise Density (µg/√Hz):** Minimum resolvable acceleration.
> - **Cross-Axis Sensitivity (%):** Unwanted response along perpendicular axes.
> - **Non-linearity (%FS):** Deviation from ideal linear output.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generic Accelerometer Model — The Mass-Spring-Damper System

The fundamental physics of **every** accelerometer reduces to a damped second-order mechanical system. A **proof mass (m)** is suspended by a **spring (k)** and surrounded by a **damper (c)** within the sensor housing.

### 2.1.1 Equation of Motion (Derivation Logic)

When the sensor frame experiences an input acceleration $a_i(t)$, the proof mass experiences a relative displacement $x(t)$ with respect to the housing. Newton's second law applied to the proof mass gives:

- **Inertial force on the mass:** $m \cdot \frac{d^{2}(x + x_i)}{dt^{2}}$ where $x_i$ is frame displacement
- **Restoring spring force:** $-k \cdot x$
- **Damping force:** $-c \cdot \frac{dx}{dt}$

Setting the sum to zero and reducing:

$$m\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + c\frac{dx}{dt} + kx = -m \cdot a_i(t)$$

This is the **governing differential equation** of an accelerometer.

### 2.1.2 Transfer Function

Taking the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions:

$$\frac{X(s)}{A_i(s)} = \frac{-m}{ms^{2} + cs + k}$$

The **natural (resonant) frequency** of the sensor is:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{rad/s} \quad ; \quad f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{Hz}$$

The **damping ratio** is:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** A well-designed accelerometer operates in the **flat region** of the frequency response, i.e., the input frequency must be much less than the resonant frequency $f_n$ (typically $f < 0.2 f_n$). This ensures linear, distortion-free measurement.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acceleration (vector) | $\vec{a} = d\vec{v}/dt = d^{2}\vec{x}/dt^{2}$ | m/s² | Fundamental definition |
| 2 | Newton's 2nd Law | $F = m \cdot a$ | N | Inertial force on proof mass |
| 3 | Spring (Hooke's) Force | $F = k \cdot x$ | N | Linear elastic regime |
| 4 | Damping Force | $F = c \cdot dx/dt$ | N | Viscous damping model |
| 5 | Governing EOM | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m a_i$ | — | Mass-spring-damper model |
| 6 | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Determines bandwidth |
| 7 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.6 – 0.7 for accelerometers |
| 8 | Static Sensitivity (displacement) | $S = -m/k = -1/\omega_n^{2}$ | s² | Output displacement per g |
| 9 | Capacitive Sensor | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | F (Farads) | Gap $d$ changes with $a$ |
| 10 | Piezoelectric Charge | $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot m \cdot a$ | C (Coulombs) | $d$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient |
| 11 | Piezoresistive Gauge | $\Delta R / R = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | $G$ = gauge factor, $\varepsilon$ = strain |
| 12 | Sensitivity (Capacitive) | $S_C = \Delta C / a$ | F·s²/m | Output per unit acceleration |
| 13 | Gauge Factor | $G = (\Delta R/R) / (\Delta L/L)$ | — | Typical Si: 100 – 200 |
| 14 | Standard Gravity | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | m/s² | Reference unit 'g' |
| 15 | Resonant Frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Hz | Avoid operation near this |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Pipe Symbols:** All absolute-value or "given that" vertical bars in the above formulas have been written as `\vert` or implicit notation to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Automotive Airbags:** The accelerometer must detect a crash deceleration (~50 g) within **< 1 ms**. This demands high resonant frequency (> 1 kHz) and wide bandwidth.
- **Wearable Health Monitors (ECG, gait analysis):** Detect micro-vibrations of the human body (~0.01 g), requiring very low noise density and high sensitivity.
- **Seismology:** Measure ground motion of $10^{-6}$ m/s² — requires ultra-low noise and active feedback.
- **Inertial Navigation (Aircraft, Drones):** Measure static gravity tilt for orientation; errors in accelerometer calibration accumulate as position drift over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Static Sensitivity of a Spring-Mass Accelerometer

**Problem Statement:** Derive an expression for the steady-state displacement of the proof mass when a constant acceleration $a_0$ is applied to the sensor frame.

**Step 1 — Set up the equation of motion in steady state.**
At steady state, the proof mass is no longer accelerating relative to the frame. Therefore, $\ddot{x} = 0$ and $\dot{x} = 0$. The governing equation becomes:

$$0 + 0 + kx_{ss} = -m a_0$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the static displacement $x_{ss}$.**

$$x_{ss} = -\frac{m \cdot a_0}{k}$$

**Step 3 — Express sensitivity as the ratio of displacement to acceleration.**

$$S = \frac{x_{ss}}{a_0} = -\frac{m}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\omega_n^{2} = k/m$.**

$$S = -\frac{1}{\omega_n^{2}} = -\frac{m}{k}$$

**Conclusion:** The static displacement is **inversely proportional to the spring stiffness** and **directly proportional to the proof mass**. To increase sensitivity (larger displacement per g), designers either:
- Increase the proof mass $m$, or
- Reduce the spring constant $k$ (softer suspension).

However, reducing $k$ also **lowers the resonant frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, which **reduces the usable bandwidth**. This is the fundamental **sensitivity-bandwidth trade-off** in accelerometer design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of a Capacitive Accelerometer

**Problem Statement:** A MEMS capacitive accelerometer has a proof mass area $A = 1 \text{ mm}^{2}$, gap $d_0 = 2 \text{ μm}$, and $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air dielectric). When a constant acceleration of 1 g is applied, the proof mass displaces by $\Delta d = 0.1 \text{ μm}$. Determine the change in capacitance.

**Step 1 — Initial (nominal) capacitance.**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

Substitute the values ($\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $A = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $d_0 = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m):

$$C_0 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(1)(1 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-18}}{2 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 4.427 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2 — New capacitance when gap decreases to $d_0 - \Delta d$.**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{1.9 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_1 = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-18}}{1.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 4.660 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 4.660 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_0 = 4.660 - 4.427 = 0.233 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitive sensitivity per g.**

$$S_C = \frac{\Delta C}{a_0} = \frac{0.233 \text{ pF}}{9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}} = 0.0237 \text{ pF per m/s}^{2}$$

Or equivalently: **$S_C \approx 0.233 \text{ pF/g}$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The capacitance varies as $1/d$. For small displacements relative to the gap ($\Delta d \ll d_0$), a linearised sensitivity formula applies: $\Delta C / C_0 \approx \Delta d / d_0$. This linearity assumption is a frequent exam topic.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoelectric Accelerometer Output Voltage

**Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric accelerometer uses a quartz crystal with piezoelectric charge coefficient $d = 2.3 \text{ pC/N}$ and is coupled to a charge amplifier of capacitance $C_a = 100 \text{ pF}$. The proof mass is $m = 5 \text{ g}$. Find the output voltage when acceleration $a = 10 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Force experienced by the crystal due to proof mass inertia.**

$$F = m \cdot a = (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg})(10 \text{ m/s}^{2}) = 0.05 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Charge generated by the piezoelectric crystal.**

$$Q = d \cdot F = (2.3 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N})(0.05 \text{ N})$$

$$Q = 1.15 \times 10^{-13} \text{ C} = 0.115 \text{ pC}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage across the charge amplifier capacitance.**

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_a} = \frac{1.15 \times 10^{-13}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 1.15 \text{ mV}$$

**Conclusion:** The output is **1.15 mV** for an input of 10 m/s² (≈ 1.02 g). The sensitivity is therefore **≈ 1.13 mV/g**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Piezoelectric accelerometers cannot measure **DC (static) accelerations** (such as constant gravity tilt), because the generated charge leaks away through the circuit. They are exclusively used for **dynamic (vibration) measurements**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Accelerometer Types

| Property | Capacitive | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output type** | Capacitance change | Charge / Voltage | Resistance change |
| **DC response (0 Hz)** | Excellent | None (AC only) | Good |
| **Sensitivity** | High (10 fF/g – 1 pF/g) | Very high (10 pC/g) | Moderate (1 mV/g) |
| **Temperature stability** | Good | Excellent | Poor (needs compensation) |
| **Power consumption** | Low (CMOS compatible) | Zero (passive) | High (bridge excitation) |
| **Frequency range** | DC to several kHz | 0.1 Hz to 50 kHz | DC to 10 kHz |
| **Typical application** | Smartphones, wearables | Vibration monitoring, modal analysis | High-g shock, airbag |
| **Fabrication** | CMOS / MEMS | Bulk crystal + mass | Silicon MEMS with diffused gauges |
| **Cost** | Low (batch MEMS) | Moderate | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Generic Accelerometer

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Input Acceleration ai t] --> MASS[Proof Mass m]
    MASS --> SPRING[Suspension Spring k]
    MASS --> DAMPER[Damping Element c]
    SPRING --> DISP[Relative Displacement x t]
    DAMPER --> DISP
    DISP --> TRANS[Transduction Element]
    TRANS --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    SIG --> OUT[Electrical Output V or C or Q]
    TRANS -.-> TYPE{Transduction Type}
    TYPE --> CAP[Capacitive Plates]
    TYPE --> PIEZO[Piezoelectric Crystal]
    TYPE --> PIEZO_R[Piezoresistive Gauges]
    CAP --> SIG
    PIEZO --> SIG
    PIEZO_R --> SIG
```

## 4.2 Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer — Sensing Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Frame [Sensor Frame Housing]
        TOP[Top Fixed Electrode]
        MASS[Movable Proof Mass with Central Electrode]
        BOT[Bottom Fixed Electrode]
    end
    ACCEL[Applied Acceleration a] --> MASS
    MASS -.->|Displaces by Delta d| TOP
    MASS -.->|Displaces by Delta d| BOT
    TOP --> CAP1[Capacitor C1 increases]
    BOT --> CAP2[Capacitor C2 decreases]
    CAP1 --> DIFF[Differential Capacitance Delta C equals C2 minus C1]
    CAP2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> ASIC[CMOS Readout ASIC]
    ASIC --> VOUT[Analog Voltage Output]
```

## 4.3 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    VIB[Mechanical Vibration] --> MASS[Seismic Proof Mass m]
    MASS --> CRYSTAL[Preloaded Piezoelectric Crystal Stack]
    CRYSTAL -->|Generates charge Q| AMP[Charge Amplifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Low-Pass Anti-Alias Filter]
    FILT --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing]
    DSP --> DISP[Output Display or Data Logger]
```

## 4.4 Frequency Response Characteristic

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph LOW [Low Frequency Flat Region]
        L1[DC to 0.2 fn] --> L2[Linear Output Region]
    end
    L2 --> RES[Resonant Peak at fn]
    RES --> HIG[High Frequency Roll Off above fn]
    HIG --> H2[Beyond Usable Bandwidth]
    L2 -.->|Used for measurement| USABLE[Operational Region of Accelerometer]
    RES -.->|AVOID| DANGER[Resonance Region Inaccurate]
```

## 4.5 Analytical Flow — Signal Path in a Piezoresistive Accelerometer

```mermaid
graph TB
    ACC[Applied Acceleration a] --> MASS[Proof Mass displaces]
    MASS --> BEAM[Flexure Beam Bends]
    BEAM --> STRAIN[Strain epsilon develops in piezoresistors]
    STRAIN --> BRIDGE[Wheatstone Bridge Unbalance]
    BRIDGE --> VOUT[Differential Voltage Output]
    BRIDGE --> VEX[Excitation Voltage Vexc applied]
    VEX --> BRIDGE
    VOUT --> AMP[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> CAL[Temperature Compensation Circuit]
    CAL --> FINAL[Calibrated Output Signal]
```

## 4.6 Cross-Sectional Conceptual Sketch (Mermaid-Compatible)

```mermaid
graph TB
    TOP[Upper Glass Cap with Anchors]
    TOP --> TOPG[Top Capacitive Plates Anchored to Frame]
    MASS[Central Silicon Proof Mass Suspended by Four Folded Beams]
    MASS --> BEAMS[Flexure Beams of Stiffness k]
    BEAMS --> ANCH[Fixed Anchors on Substrate]
    BOTSUB[Substrate with Lower Capacitive Plates]
    ANCH --> BOTSUB
    MASS -.->|Relative motion Delta x| TOPG
    MASS -.->|Relative motion Delta x| BOTSUB
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q: Define an accelerometer. List any four performance parameters used to characterise it.**

**Model Answer:**
An accelerometer is a transducer that converts mechanical acceleration into a measurable electrical output (voltage, charge, or capacitance change). It operates on the principle of measuring the inertial force on a proof mass suspended within a sensor frame.

Four key performance parameters:
1. **Range (g):** Maximum measurable acceleration.
2. **Sensitivity (mV/g):** Output per unit acceleration input.
3. **Bandwidth (Hz):** Frequency range of useful operation.
4. **Cross-axis sensitivity (%):** Unwanted response along orthogonal axes.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Noise density (µg/√Hz).

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for parameters — name and brief purpose)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample]`
**Q: Why cannot a piezoelectric accelerometer measure DC (static) acceleration? How does this limitation differ from a capacitive accelerometer?**

**Model Answer:**
A piezoelectric accelerometer generates a **charge proportional to the applied force**. Under constant (DC) acceleration, the generated charge leaks away through the input impedance of the readout circuit and the internal resistance of the crystal, resulting in zero steady-state output. Hence, it is suitable **only for dynamic (vibratory) measurements**.

In contrast, a **capacitive accelerometer** measures a steady displacement of the proof mass that produces a sustained change in capacitance, allowing it to measure both **DC and AC accelerations** (e.g., gravity tilt).

*(3 marks: 2 for piezoelectric explanation, 1 for the capacitive contrast)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Module 1]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a capacitive MEMS accelerometer. Derive its governing equation and discuss the sensitivity-bandwidth trade-off.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **capacitive MEMS accelerometer** is fabricated using silicon micromachining. The key structural elements are:

- **Proof Mass (m):** A central silicon mass suspended by **four folded flexure beams** that act as the spring (stiffness $k$).
- **Capacitor Plates:** The proof mass carries **fingers (movable electrodes)** that interdigitate with **fixed electrodes** anchored to the sensor frame. Two differential capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are formed on either side of the mass.
- **Substrate / Cap:** Glass or silicon wafer providing hermetic packaging and anchor points.

**Working Principle:**
1. When the sensor frame accelerates with input $a_i(t)$, the proof mass lags behind due to inertia, producing a relative displacement $x(t)$.
2. This displacement **increases** the gap on one side and **decreases** the gap on the other.
3. The differential capacitance $\Delta C = C_2 - C_1$ becomes a linear function of $x(t)$, and hence of $a_i(t)$.
4. A CMOS readout ASIC converts $\Delta C$ into an analog voltage output.

A **block diagram** of the working (as shown in Section 4.2 above) summarises this signal chain.

#### Part (b) — Governing Equation & Trade-off (7 Marks)

**Governing Equation:**
Applying Newton's second law on the proof mass:

$$m\frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} + c\frac{dx}{dt} + kx = -m \cdot a_i(t)$$

The **static sensitivity** is:

$$S = \frac{x_{ss}}{a_0} = -\frac{m}{k} = -\frac{1}{\omega_n^{2}}$$

**Sensitivity-Bandwidth Trade-off:**

- The resonant frequency is $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$.
- For accurate measurement, the input frequency must be **well below $f_n$** (typically $f < 0.2 f_n$).
- Increasing sensitivity requires **lowering $k$** (softer spring) or **raising $m$** (heavier mass).
- Both actions **decrease $f_n$**, thereby **reducing the usable bandwidth**.
- Conversely, increasing $k$ for higher bandwidth reduces the displacement per g, decreasing sensitivity.

This is the **fundamental sensitivity-bandwidth trade-off** in accelerometer design, and it is typically resolved by choosing $k$ and $m$ such that $f_n$ is at least 5× the maximum input frequency of interest.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Module 1]`

**Q: Compare piezoelectric and piezoresistive accelerometers. Derive the output voltage of a piezoelectric accelerometer with a seismic mass of 10 g, charge coefficient 2.3 pC/N, and amplifier capacitance 200 pF, when subjected to an acceleration of 5 g.**

#### Part (a) — Comparison (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Piezoelectric | Piezoresistive |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing element** | Crystal (quartz, PZT, PVDF) | Diffused silicon gauges on flexure |
| **Output** | Charge $Q$ (Coulombs) | Resistance change $\Delta R$ |
| **DC response** | None — AC only | Yes — DC capable |
| **Sensitivity** | Very high (10 – 100 pC/g) | Moderate (~1 mV/g with bridge) |
| **Power needed** | None (passive) | Yes (bridge excitation) |
| **Temperature drift** | Low | Significant (needs compensation) |
| **Frequency range** | 0.1 Hz to 50 kHz | DC to 10 kHz |
| **Use case** | Vibration, modal analysis, engine monitoring | Crash sensors, high-shock, biomedical |
| **Cost** | Moderate | Low |

Both convert mechanical strain into an electrical signal, but the **piezoelectric** type directly generates charge, whereas the **piezoresistive** type alters the resistance of a strained silicon element, requiring a **Wheatstone bridge** to produce a usable voltage output.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Proof mass $m = 10 \text{ g} = 0.01 \text{ kg}$
- Piezoelectric charge coefficient $d = 2.3 \text{ pC/N} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- Amplifier capacitance $C_a = 200 \text{ pF} = 200 \times 10^{-12}$ F
- Input acceleration $a = 5g = 5 \times 9.81 = 49.05 \text{ m/s}^{2}$

**Step 1 — Force on the crystal:**

$$F = m \cdot a = (0.01)(49.05) = 0.4905 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Charge generated:**

$$Q = d \cdot F = (2.3 \times 10^{-12})(0.4905) = 1.128 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{C_a} = \frac{1.128 \times 10^{-12}}{200 \times 10^{-12}} = 5.64 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 5.64 \text{ mV}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} \approx 5.64 \text{ mV}$ (or equivalently, sensitivity ≈ 1.13 mV/g).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert grams to kg, mm² to m², pF to F. This is the **#1 cause** of mark deductions in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing the sign:** The displacement is in the **opposite** direction to acceleration (proof mass lags behind). Include the negative sign only when the problem statement asks for relative direction.
> 3. **Forgetting the 'g' factor:** When an acceleration is given in 'g' (e.g., 5 g), always convert to m/s² before computing force.
> 4. **Missing the band-limit condition:** A common theoretical error is computing $x_{ss}$ without noting that the input frequency must be $f < 0.2 f_n$ for linearity. Examiners specifically check for this.
> 5. **Forgetting DC limitation of piezo sensors:** A definition question that scores 0 if the student lists "measures static gravity" as an application of a piezoelectric accelerometer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** An accelerometer measures acceleration by detecting the inertia-driven displacement of a proof mass suspended inside the sensor.
- **Governing Equation:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m \cdot a_i(t)$ — a damped second-order system.
- **Static Sensitivity:** $S = -m/k = -1/\omega_n^{2}$ — inversely proportional to spring stiffness.
- **Resonant Frequency:** $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — sets the upper bandwidth limit.
- **Capacitive Accelerometer:** Measures DC + AC; uses $C = \varepsilon A/d$ with differential gap sensing; CMOS-compatible; dominant in consumer electronics.
- **Piezoelectric Accelerometer:** AC only; generates charge $Q = d \cdot F$; used for vibration, modal analysis, engine monitoring.
- **Piezoresistive Accelerometer:** DC-capable; uses $\Delta R/R = G \cdot \varepsilon$; requires bridge excitation and temperature compensation; used in crash/airbag sensors.
- **Sensitivity-Bandwidth Trade-off:** Higher sensitivity ⇒ lower $f_n$ ⇒ lower usable bandwidth.
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81$ m/s² is the reference unit.
- **Operational Rule:** Operate the accelerometer at $f < 0.2 f_n$ for distortion-free linear measurement.
- **Cross-axis sensitivity** should be < 5% for high-quality inertial-grade sensors.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** MEMS-fabricated capacitive accelerometers dominate biomedical wearable applications due to low power, small size, and DC response.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **mass-spring-damper** diagram before writing the equation of motion — it earns **method marks** even if the final derivation has an arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The "Heading Sensor" Family — A Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Heading Sensors):**
> A **Heading Sensor** is a class of transducers that provide information about the **orientation, angular position, rate of rotation, or absolute geographic bearing** of a host platform. In the context of biomedical and rehabilitation engineering (the domain of OEBRT834), heading sensors are the cornerstone of **inertial navigation, gait analysis, surgical tool tracking, and wearable assistive devices.** The five canonical members studied in Module 1 are: the **Magnetic Compass**, the **Gyroscope**, the **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**, the **Global Positioning System (GPS)**, and the **Real Time Differential GPS (RTK / DGPS).**

Each sensor in this family answers a subtly different question about *where the device is pointing* and *how it is moving through space*.

| Sensor | Primary Quantity Measured | Output Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Magnetic Compass | Bearing w.r.t. Earth's magnetic north | Absolute angle (°) |
| Gyroscope | Angular rate of rotation | Rate (°/s or rad/s) |
| IMU | Linear accel. + angular rate (sometimes + magnetic field) | Vector triad |
| GPS | Absolute geographic position (LLA) | Latitude / Longitude / Altitude |
| RTK / DGPS | Centimetre-grade absolute position | LLA + baseline vector |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Box — The Five-Sense Framework**
> Think of a smartphone's navigation system as a human trying to find their way through a forest at night.
> - **Magnetic Compass** is the *internal sense of smell for the Earth's iron smell (magnetic flux lines)*. It always knows where "magnetic north" is, but gets confused near big iron trees (vehicles, buildings).
> - **Gyroscope** is the *inner-ear balance (vestibular system)*. When you spin, fluid in your ear lags and tells your brain the rotation rate. It is blind to absolute direction, but masters *changes* in direction.
> - **IMU** is the *vestibular system + accelerometer* — essentially, the full balance organ. It is a self-contained "inner ear" that does not need any external reference (no satellites, no magnets).
> - **GPS** is the *ability to see a lighthouse in the distance* and triangulate your position using multiple lighthouses. It works in open sky, but fails indoors and in dense jungle.
> - **RTK / DGPS** is a *surveyor's theodolite* — a friend standing on a *known landmark* shouts correction factors to you, refining your lighthouse-based estimate to centimetre accuracy.

---

## 1.3 The Magnetic Compass — A Transducer Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A magnetic compass is a **non-contact heading sensor** that exploits the directional torque produced on a freely suspended permanent magnet (or magnetised ferrous element) by the **geomagnetic field B_E** of the Earth. The magnitude of B_E varies from approximately **25 μT near the equator to 65 μT near the magnetic poles**, with a horizontal component that points to the *magnetic north*.

**Three flavours** are studied at KTU level:

1. **Hall-Effect Compass** — uses the **Hall voltage** generated when current-carrying semiconductor is exposed to B_E.
2. **Magnetoresistive (AMR / GMR / TMR) Compass** — uses the change in electrical resistance of a ferromagnetic thin-film (e.g., Permalloy) as a function of the angle between magnetisation and current direction.
3. **Fluxgate Compass** — uses a pair of ferrite cores driven into saturation to detect the second harmonic of B_E along two orthogonal axes.

**Intuition:** Imagine a *tiny magnetic needle floating in silicon oil* (the AMR bridge). When you turn the sensor, the needle "wants" to align with Earth's field, and the change in resistance reveals exactly how much you have turned.

---

## 1.4 The Gyroscope Sensor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A gyroscope (or *gyro*) is a transducer that measures **angular velocity ω** (the rate of change of orientation) about one or more axes, based on the **conservation of angular momentum**, the **Sagnac effect**, or the **Coriolis effect** in MEMS structures.

**Three generations** used in modern biomedical instruments:

| Generation | Physical Principle | Typical Bias Drift |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mechanical / Spinning-mass Gyro | Conservation of angular momentum (L = I·ω) | 0.01 – 1 °/h (navigation grade) |
| Ring Laser Gyro (RLG) | Sagnac effect on counter-propagating laser beams | 0.001 – 0.01 °/h |
| MEMS Vibratory Gyro | Coriolis force on a vibrating proof mass | 1 – 10 °/h (consumer grade) |
| Fibre Optic Gyro (FOG) | Sagnac phase shift in optical fibre coil | 0.01 – 0.1 °/h |

**Intuition:** A *spinning bicycle wheel on a string* will not fall down — it tries to keep its spin axis pointing the same way in space. This stubbornness is **angular momentum**, and the math behind it (L = I·ω) is the foundation of every gyroscope.

---

## 1.5 The Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** An IMU is a **self-contained, multi-axis electronic package** that combines three orthogonally-mounted **accelerometers** and three orthogonally-mounted **gyroscopes** (and often a 3-axis **magnetometer** to form a 9-DoF "MARg" sensor). It provides a complete description of the **specific force** and **angular velocity** vector acting on the host body, enabling dead-reckoning navigation when external references (GPS, beacons) are unavailable.

**Why it matters in Biomedical Engineering:**
- **Gait analysis** in Parkinson's and post-stroke rehabilitation.
- **Prosthetic limb** orientation feedback.
- **Surgical tool tracking** in image-guided interventions (e.g., neurosurgical frames).
- **Wearable fall detectors** for the elderly.

**Intuition:** An IMU is *a complete balance organ on a chip*. It cannot see the sun or feel magnetic north, but it can measure every push, jerk, and twist its host experiences, and integrate these to estimate pose.

---

## 1.6 The Global Positioning System (GPS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** GPS is a **satellite-based radio-navigation system** operated by the U.S. Department of Defense (constellation of **≥ 24 MEO satellites** in six orbital planes at ~20,200 km altitude, period ~11 h 58 min), which provides a receiver with its **3-D position, velocity, and time (PVT)** by measuring the **time-of-flight** of ranging codes from at least **four** satellites in **Line-of-Sight (LoS)**.

**Intuition:** Imagine *four flashlights on four hilltops* blinking synchronously in a known pattern. By recording *exactly* how late each blink arrives, you can solve for both *where you are* and *how wrong your stopwatch is* — that is the GPS trilateration trick.

---

## 1.7 Real Time Differential GPS (RTK / DGPS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** Real Time Differential GPS is an **augmentation technique** in which a *reference (base) receiver* at a precisely-surveyed location broadcasts **real-time correction terms** (pseudorange, carrier-phase, or both) to one or more *rover* receivers, eliminating the spatially-correlated error sources (ionospheric delay, tropospheric delay, satellite clock, ephemeris) and improving position accuracy from ~3–5 m (standalone GPS) to **< 1 m (DGPS)** or **< 2 cm (RTK using carrier phase and integer ambiguity resolution).**

**Intuition:** If your lighthouse-triangulation estimate is off by 5 m because the lighthouses *wobble* due to atmospheric shimmer, and a friend at a known landmark tells you *exactly* how much they are wobbling, you can subtract that wobble from your own estimate. That friend is the *base station*. When you do this in **real time** (sub-second latency) using the *carrier wave* of the GPS signal, you get the *RTK* upgrade.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Magnetic Compass — The Physics Behind the Needle

A magnetic compass sensor outputs a voltage proportional to the projection of the Earth's magnetic field vector **B_E** onto its sensitive axis. The transduction chain is:

1. **Field sensing element** (Hall plate / AMR bridge / fluxgate core) produces a raw electrical signal.
2. **Front-end analogue ASIC** amplifies, demodulates, and compensates for temperature.
3. **Microcontroller** applies **hard-iron** and **soft-iron** calibration, computes the heading angle θ_h, and applies **magnetic declination** δ to convert magnetic north to true north.

### 2.1.1 The Heading Equation (Compass)

For a 2-axis magnetometer measuring B_x and B_y, the heading (magnetic) is given by:

$$
\theta_{mag} = \arctan\left(\frac{B_y}{B_x}\right)
$$

And the **true heading** is:

$$
\theta_{true} = \theta_{mag} + \delta
$$

where **δ** is the **magnetic declination** (positive east, negative west), which is location-dependent and slowly time-varying.

### 2.1.2 Hall-Effect Output Voltage

$$
V_H = \frac{I \cdot B_E \cdot \cos\phi}{n \cdot e \cdot t}
$$

where:
- $I$ = bias current through the Hall plate (A)
- $B_E$ = magnetic flux density (T)
- $\phi$ = angle between $B_E$ and the normal to the Hall plate
- $n$ = charge carrier density (m⁻³)
- $e$ = electron charge (1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C)
- $t$ = thickness of the active layer (m)

### 2.1.3 AMR Bridge Transfer Function

The AMR effect in a Permalloy strip yields a resistance that varies as the square of the cosine of the angle α between the magnetisation and current:

$$
R(\alpha) = R_0 + \Delta R \cdot \cos^2\alpha
$$

A **Wheatstone bridge** of four such strips (Barber-pole configuration) linearises this into a voltage proportional to sin(2α).

---

## 2.2 Gyroscope — The Three Pillars of Physics

### 2.2.1 Mechanical Gyroscope: Conservation of Angular Momentum

The spin axis of a spinning rotor obeys:

$$
\vec{L} = \mathbf{I} \cdot \vec{\omega}
$$

When an external torque τ is applied perpendicular to **L**, the spin axis **precesses** at rate:

$$
\vec{\Omega}_{prec} = \frac{\vec{\tau}}{\vec{L}} = \frac{\vec{\tau}}{\mathbf{I} \cdot \vec{\omega}}
$$

### 2.2.2 Optical Gyroscope: The Sagnac Effect

For a Ring Laser Gyro (RLG) of perimeter $P$ rotating at angular rate $\Omega$, the two counter-propagating beams accumulate a **path-length difference** ΔL:

$$
\Delta L = \frac{4 A \cdot \Omega}{c}
$$

where $A$ is the area enclosed by the ring and $c$ is the speed of light. The associated **fringe shift** or **beat frequency** is:

$$
\Delta f = \frac{4 A}{\lambda P} \cdot \Omega
$$

For a Fibre Optic Gyro (FOG) of length $L_{f}$ wound on a coil of radius $R$:

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac{8 \pi A \cdot \Omega}{\lambda c} \cdot N = \frac{8 \pi^2 R^2 N}{\lambda c} \cdot \Omega
$$

where $N$ is the number of fibre turns.

### 2.2.3 MEMS Gyroscope: The Coriolis Effect

When a proof mass $m$ moves with velocity $\vec{v}$ relative to a rotating frame with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$, it experiences the **Coriolis acceleration**:

$$
\vec{a}_{Cor} = 2 \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}
$$

The associated Coriolis **force** is:

$$
\vec{F}_{Cor} = -2m \, \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v}
$$

The MEMS structure senses the displacement caused by $F_{Cor}$ and infers $\omega$.

---

## 2.3 IMU — Sensor Fusion and Dead Reckoning

The IMU is not a single sensor but a *fused* system. Two mathematical frameworks dominate:

### 2.3.1 Strap-Down Inertial Navigation

The orientation of the body frame $\{b\}$ relative to the navigation frame $\{n\}$ is encoded as a **rotation matrix** $C_b^n$ (or quaternion $\mathbf{q}$). Its time evolution follows:

$$
\dot{C}_b^n = C_b^n \, \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ib}^b
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ib}^b$ is the skew-symmetric matrix of the gyroscope-measured body angular rates:

$$
\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ib}^b = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -\omega_z & \omega_y \\ \omega_z & 0 & -\omega_x \\ -\omega_y & \omega_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The accelerometer measures the **specific force** $f_b$, which (after removing gravity) is integrated twice to obtain velocity and position:

$$
\dot{v}^n = C_b^n \, f^b + g^n - (\boldsymbol{\Omega}_{en}^n + \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ie}^n) v^n
$$

$$
\dot{p}^n = v^n
$$

### 2.3.2 Complementary / Kalman Filter Fusion

A **complementary filter** blends the short-term accuracy of the gyroscope with the long-term stability of the accelerometer (and magnetometer):

$$
\hat{\theta}_{k} = \alpha \left( \hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \, \Delta t \right) + (1 - \alpha) \, \theta_{acc,k}
$$

A **Kalman filter** does the same in a statistically optimal way, maintaining a state vector $\mathbf{x} = [p, v, q, b_g, b_a]^T$ and covariance $P$.

---

## 2.4 GPS — The Mathematics of Trilateration

### 2.4.1 Pseudorange Equation

The *pseudorange* $\rho$ between the receiver and satellite $s$ is:

$$
\rho^{(s)} = \vert \vert \vec{r}^{(s)} - \vec{r}_u \vert \vert + c \, (dt_u - dt^{(s)}) + I^{(s)} + T^{(s)} + \varepsilon^{(s)}
$$

where:
- $\vec{r}^{(s)}$ = satellite position (ECEF) (m)
- $\vec{r}_u$ = unknown user position (m)
- $c$ = speed of light (m/s)
- $dt_u$ = receiver clock bias (s)
- $dt^{(s)}$ = satellite clock bias (s)
- $I^{(s)}$ = ionospheric delay (m)
- $T^{(s)}$ = tropospheric delay (m)
- $\varepsilon^{(s)}$ = measurement noise (m)

Expanding the norm:

$$
\rho^{(s)} = \sqrt{(x^{(s)} - x_u)^2 + (y^{(s)} - y_u)^2 + (z^{(s)} - z_u)^2} + b_u
$$

where $b_u = c \, dt_u$ is the **clock bias in metres**. With 4 satellites, 4 such equations yield the 4 unknowns $(x_u, y_u, z_u, b_u)$.

### 2.4.2 Dilution of Precision (DOP)

The accuracy of the position fix depends on satellite geometry via the **GDOP**:

$$
\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}\left( (H^T H)^{-1} \right)}
$$

where $H$ is the geometry matrix of unit vectors from receiver to satellites. Typical values: GDOP < 2 (excellent), 2–4 (good), > 6 (poor).

---

## 2.5 Real Time Differential GPS (DGPS & RTK) — Error Cancellation

### 2.5.1 Standard DGPS Pseudorange Correction

If the base station at *known* position $\vec{r}_b$ measures pseudorange $\rho_b^{(s)}$ to satellite $s$, the **pseudorange correction** (PRC) is:

$$
\text{PRC}^{(s)} = \rho_b^{(s)} - \vert \vert \vec{r}^{(s)} - \vec{r}_b \vert \vert
$$

The rover applies this correction in real time:

$$
\rho_{corrected}^{(s)} = \rho_{rover}^{(s)} - \text{PRC}^{(s)}
$$

The spatially-correlated errors (ionosphere, troposphere, satellite clock, ephemeris) largely cancel because base and rover are within ~100 km.

### 2.5.2 RTK Carrier-Phase and Integer Ambiguity

RTK uses the **carrier phase** $\Phi$ (not the code) for mm-level ranging:

$$
\Phi^{(s)} = \frac{\rho^{(s)}}{\lambda} + N^{(s)} + f \cdot (dt_u - dt^{(s)}) + \text{errors}
$$

The unknown **integer ambiguity** $N^{(s)}$ (number of whole wavelengths between satellite and receiver) is resolved using algorithms such as **LAMBDA** (Least-squares AMBiguity Decorrelation Adjustment). The **double-difference** between base and rover further cancels clock errors:

$$
\nabla \Delta \Phi^{(s_r, s_b)} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left( \rho_r^{(s)} - \rho_r^{(b)} - \rho_b^{(s)} + \rho_b^{(b)} \right) + \nabla \Delta N
$$

Once $N$ is fixed, sub-cm accuracy is achievable.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Equations on One Page)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **printable high-yield sheet** KTU examiners expect you to memorise. All entries use $\vert$ instead of the vertical bar `|` to remain compatible with markdown tables.

| # | Sensor | Equation | Variable Legend | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Magnetic Compass | $\theta_{true} = \arctan(B_y / B_x) + \delta$ | $\theta_{true}$ = true heading, $\delta$ = declination | rad / ° |
| 2 | Hall Effect | $V_H = I \cdot B \cdot \cos\phi / (n e t)$ | $I$ = current, $B$ = flux density, $\phi$ = angle | V |
| 3 | AMR Effect | $R(\alpha) = R_0 + \Delta R \cos^2 \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = angle between $M$ and $I$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Spinning Gyro | $\vec{\Omega}_{prec} = \vec{\tau} / (I \omega)$ | $\tau$ = applied torque, $I$ = MOI, $\omega$ = spin rate | rad/s |
| 5 | Sagnac (RLG) | $\Delta f = 4 A \Omega / (\lambda P)$ | $A$ = ring area, $P$ = perimeter | Hz |
| 6 | Sagnac (FOG) | $\Delta \phi = 8 \pi^2 R^2 N \Omega / (\lambda c)$ | $R$ = coil radius, $N$ = turns | rad |
| 7 | Coriolis (MEMS) | $F_{Cor} = 2m \cdot \vert \vec{\omega} \times \vec{v} \vert$ | $m$ = mass, $v$ = drive velocity | N |
| 8 | Strap-Down | $\dot{C}_b^n = C_b^n \, \boldsymbol{\Omega}_{ib}^b$ | $C$ = DCM, $\Omega$ = skew matrix | rad/s |
| 9 | Complementary | $\hat{\theta}_k = \alpha (\hat{\theta}_{k-1} + \omega_k \Delta t) + (1-\alpha) \theta_{acc,k}$ | $\alpha$ = blend factor | rad |
| 10 | GPS Pseudorange | $\rho = \vert \vert \vec{r}^{(s)} - \vec{r}_u \vert \vert + b_u + I + T + \varepsilon$ | $b_u$ = clock bias (m) | m |
| 11 | GPS Trilateration | $\sqrt{(x^{(s)} - x_u)^2 + (y^{(s)} - y_u)^2 + (z^{(s)} - z_u)^2} = \rho^{(s)} - b_u$ | 4 sats, 4 unknowns | m |
| 12 | GDOP | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}((H^T H)^{-1})}$ | $H$ = geometry matrix | dimensionless |
| 13 | DGPS Correction | $\rho_{corr} = \rho_{rover} - (\rho_{base} - \rho_{true,base})$ | spatially-correlated errors cancel | m |
| 14 | RTK Phase | $\Phi = \rho / \lambda + N + f \cdot \Delta t$ | $N$ = integer ambiguity, $\lambda$ = wavelength | cycles |
| 15 | Double-Diff RTK | $\nabla \Delta \Phi = \nabla \Delta \rho / \lambda + \nabla \Delta N$ | eliminates clock terms | cycles |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where each formula is used in real engineering practice:**
> - Equations (4)–(7) are evaluated inside the *ASIC firmware* of every consumer gyroscope in your phone.
> - Equations (8)–(9) are the **drone autopilot** and **INS/GPS fusion** backbone (Pixhawk, ArduPilot).
> - Equation (10) is solved iteratively inside every smartphone GPS chip (Broadcom BCM4775, u-blox NEO-M9N).
> - Equations (13)–(15) are the bread and butter of **land surveying** (Trimble R12i, Leica GS18) and **precision agriculture** (auto-steer tractors to 2 cm accuracy).
