> **The key limiting fact:** Inductive sensors **only** detect conductive materials. Plastics, wood, ceramics, and fluids are **invisible** to them. This is why capacitive sensors exist.

### Key Equations for Inductive Proximity Sensors

The sensing range $S_n$ (nominal range) is empirically related to the target's properties by:

$$S_n = S_{Fe} \cdot f_{material}$$

where $S_{Fe}$ is the range on a standard **mild-steel (Fe 37)** target and $f_{material}$ is a **correction factor** (between 0 and 1) that depends on the target material (e.g., stainless steel ≈ 0.6–0.85, aluminium ≈ 0.35–0.5, copper ≈ 0.25–0.45).

### Inductance of the Sensing Coil
For a solenoid coil of $N$ turns, length $l$, and cross-sectional area $A$:

$$L = \frac{N^2 \cdot \mu \cdot A}{l}$$

where $\mu = \mu_0 \cdot \mu_r$ is the permeability of the core (typically ferrite, $\mu_r \approx 500$–$5000$).

### Oscillator Frequency

$$f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C}}$$

The capacitance $C$ is a fixed capacitor in the LC tank; the inductance $L$ is the variable perturbed by the approaching target.

## 2.4 Capacitive Proximity Sensors — Working Principle

### The Two-Plate Capacitor Model
A capacitive proximity sensor behaves like a **parallel-plate capacitor** whose plates are:
- The **active electrode** (sensor face), and
- A virtual "second plate" formed by the **target object and its environment (earth/ground)**.

Any object that approaches the active face **changes the effective dielectric** or the **effective area** in the fringe field, thereby changing the capacitance.

### Capacitance of the Parallel-Plate Model

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

where:
- $\varepsilon_0$ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space),
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity (dielectric constant) of the intervening medium,
- $A$ = effective overlap area of the electric field,
- $d$ = distance between electrode and target.

### For a Cylindrical Capacitive Probe (used in level sensing)

$$C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$$

where $a$ and $b$ are the inner and outer conductor radii and $L$ is the immersion length.

### Why Capacitive Sensors Are Versatile
Unlike inductive sensors, capacitive sensors detect **any material** whose $\varepsilon_r \neq \varepsilon_{air}$:
- Metals (very strong response, $\varepsilon_r \to \infty$).
- Plastics ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2$–$5$).
- Wood, paper ($\varepsilon_r \approx 2$–$6$).
- Water ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) — used for liquid-level sensing.
- Human body / tissue ($\varepsilon_r \approx 50$–$100$) — used in biomedical touch sensors.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorise for KTU Board Exams**
>
> | Phenomenon | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Solenoid inductance | $L = \frac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$ | $N$ turns, $A$ area, $l$ length | Henry (H) |
> | LC oscillator frequency | $f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | $L$ inductance, $C$ capacitance | Hertz (Hz) |
> | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m | Farad (F) |
> | Cylindrical capacitance | $C = \frac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L}{\ln(b/a)}$ | $a,b$ radii, $L$ length | Farad (F) |
> | Bumper force model | $F = kx + c\dot{x}$ | $k$ stiffness, $c$ damping, $x$ deflection | Newton (N) |
> | Nominal sensing range | $S_n = S_{Fe} \cdot f_{mat}$ | $f_{mat}$ correction factor | mm |
> | Capacitive reactance | $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Frequency-dependent impedance | Ohm ($\Omega$) |
> | Inductive reactance | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Frequency-dependent impedance | Ohm ($\Omega$) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sensor | Industry Application | Why Chosen |
|---|---|---|
| Contact switch | Elevator door interlocks, CNC machine guarding | **Fail-safe**, certified for safety |
| Bumper | AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle) collision guard | Wide-area, low-cost |
| Inductive | Metal part counting, gear-tooth sensing, position of a piston | Insensitive to dust, oil, water |
| Capacitive | Powder level in silos, tablet counting, liquid level, touch screens | Detects non-metals, through-thin walls |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Inductance of a Multi-Turn Sensing Coil

**Starting Point — Ampère's Circuital Law for a Solenoid:**

For an ideal solenoid of $N$ turns and length $l$ carrying current $I$, the magnetic field inside is uniform and given by:

$$B = \frac{\mu \cdot N \cdot I}{l}$$

**Step 1: Compute the Flux Through One Turn**

Each turn of the coil encloses an area $A$, so the magnetic flux through one turn is:

$$\Phi_{one} = B \cdot A = \frac{\mu \cdot N \cdot I \cdot A}{l}$$

**Step 2: Total Flux Linkage (N turns in series)**

The total flux linkage $\lambda$ is the flux per turn multiplied by the number of turns:

$$\lambda = N \cdot \Phi_{one} = N \cdot \left(\frac{\mu \cdot N \cdot I \cdot A}{l}\right) = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A \cdot I}{l}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Definition of Inductance**

By definition, inductance is the ratio of total flux linkage to the current producing it:

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A \cdot I / l}{I} = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}$$

$$\boxed{L = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}}$$

**Interpretation:** Inductance scales with the *square* of the number of turns. Doubling $N$ quadruples $L$, which is why sensor coils are wound with many turns of fine wire to maximise sensitivity to small target perturbations.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency Shift in an LC Tank with a Nearby Target

**Step 1: Define the Unloaded Oscillator**

The proximity sensor's oscillator is a parallel LC tank. Its resonant frequency in air (no target) is:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_0 C_0}}$$

**Step 2: Effect of a Metallic Target**

When a conductive target approaches, eddy currents induce a *counter-flux* in the target. From Lenz's law, this counter-flux opposes the source flux, effectively **reducing the inductance** seen by the coil to $L_1 < L_0$. (For a perfect conductor at zero distance, $L_1 \to 0$.)

For a target at distance $d$ from the coil face (using a first-order model valid for $d \ll $ coil diameter):

$$L(d) = L_0 \left(1 - k \cdot e^{-d/\delta}\right)$$

where $k$ is a coupling constant and $\delta$ is the *skin depth* of the target material at the oscillator frequency.

**Step 3: New Resonant Frequency**

$$f(d) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L(d) \cdot C_0}}$$

**Step 4: Express the Frequency Shift**

$$\Delta f = f(d) - f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{L(d) \cdot C_0}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_0 \cdot C_0}}\right]$$

For small perturbations ($L(d) \approx L_0(1-\epsilon)$, $\epsilon \ll 1$), this simplifies to:

$$\Delta f \approx \frac{\epsilon}{2} \cdot f_0 = \frac{k \cdot e^{-d/\delta}}{2} \cdot f_0$$

**Result:** The frequency shift decays **exponentially** with target distance — this is why inductive sensors have a sharply defined "switch-on" point at the nominal range $S_n$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitance Change in a Capacitive Proximity Sensor

**Step 1: Capacitance in Air (No Target)**

With no target, the sensor face and the surrounding earth/ground form a capacitor with air as the dielectric:

$$C_{air} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot A}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

**Step 2: Capacitance with a Target of Thickness $t$ and $\varepsilon_r$**

When a dielectric target of thickness $t$ approaches to within gap $g$ of the sensor face, the system becomes a **series combination** of two capacitors:
- $C_g = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}$ (air gap), and
- $C_t = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$ (target slab).

**Step 3: Series Capacitance**

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{C_g} + \frac{1}{C_t} = \frac{g}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[g + \frac{t}{\varepsilon_r}\right]$$

$$C_{total} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g + t/\varepsilon_r}$$

**Step 4: Sensitivity**

The fractional change in capacitance when the target enters the field is:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_{air}} = \frac{C_{total} - C_{air}}{C_{air}} = \frac{g}{g + t/\varepsilon_r} - 1 = \frac{-t/\varepsilon_r}{g + t/\varepsilon_r}$$

**Result:** Targets with **high $\varepsilon_r$** (e.g., water, $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) produce a much larger $\Delta C$ than low-$\varepsilon_r$ materials (e.g., PTFE, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.1$). This is the physical basis for **material-selective** capacitive sensing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Between Inductive and Capacitive

| Decision Parameter | Use **Inductive** if | Use **Capacitive** if |
|---|---|---|
| Target material | Metal (ferrous or non-ferrous) | Any (metal, plastic, liquid, wood) |
| Sensing range needed | Tight (0.5–15 mm) | Wider (0.5–50 mm) |
| Environment | Dusty, oily, wet | Stable temperature, dry |
| Response time | < 1 ms (typical) | < 5 ms (typical) |
| Cost per unit | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Through-wall sensing? | **No** (metal would shield) | **Yes** (up to few mm of non-metal) |
| Sensitivity to humidity | Negligible | Significant (esp. for low-$\varepsilon_r$ targets) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Inductive Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Supply 10-30V] --> B[Oscillator Circuit]
    B --> C[LC Tank with Sensing Coil]
    C --> D[Detector with Schmitt Trigger]
    D --> E[Output Stage PNP or NPN]
    E --> F[Load Relay or PLC]
    G[Metallic Target Approaches] -.->|Eddy current loss| C
    C -.->|Amplitude drop| D
    D -.->|Threshold crossed| E
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic Capacitive Proximity Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator 50kHz to 1MHz] --> B[Active Electrode Sensor Face]
    B --> C[Fringe Electric Field]
    C --> D[Target Object any material]
    D -.->|Perturbs dielectric| B
    B --> E[Capacitance to Voltage Converter]
    E --> F[Threshold Comparator]
    F --> G[Output Stage]
    H[Supply Voltage] --> A
    H --> G
```

## 4.3 Architecture of a Spring-Loaded Bumper System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impact Event] --> B[Deflects Spring Steel Whisker]
    B --> C[Shorts Contact Pair 1]
    B --> D[Shorts Contact Pair 2]
    B --> E[Shorts Contact Pair N]
    C --> F[Signal Conditioning Mux]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Threshold Comparator]
    G --> H[Collision Flag to Controller]
    H --> I[E Stop Activated]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need to detect object?] --> B{Physical contact OK?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Binary or force value?}
    B -->|No| D{Target is metal?}
    C -->|Binary| E[Use Contact Switch]
    C -->|Force| F[Use Piezoresistive Bumper]
    D -->|Yes| G[Use Inductive Proximity]
    D -->|No| H[Use Capacitive Proximity]
    E --> I[Done]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

## 4.5 Functional Architecture of a Tactile Sensing Array (Robot Skin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Compliant Layer] --> B[Electrode Matrix 16x16]
    B --> C[Row Scanner]
    B --> D[Column Scanner]
    C --> E[Charge Amplifier]
    D --> E
    E --> F[ADC 12 bit]
    F --> G[Microcontroller]
    G --> H[Force Map Display]
    G --> I[Grip Control Loop]
    G --> J[Safety E Stop]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the working principle of an inductive proximity sensor with a neat block diagram. Mention two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An inductive proximity sensor consists of four main blocks: an **LC oscillator**, a **sensing coil** at the front face, a **detector circuit** (Schmitt trigger), and an **output switching stage** (PNP or NPN). The oscillator drives a high-frequency alternating current (typically **100 kHz to 1 MHz**) through the coil, generating an alternating electromagnetic field at the sensor face. When a **metallic target** enters this field, eddy currents are induced in the target. These eddy currents dissipate energy, damping the oscillation amplitude. Once the amplitude falls below a threshold, the detector triggers and the output switches from OFF to ON. **(2 marks)**

**Applications:** (i) Position sensing of metal pistons in hydraulic cylinders, (ii) gear-tooth speed sensing in automotive transmissions, (iii) counting metal cans on a conveyor line. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare the working of capacitive and inductive proximity sensors. When is a capacitive sensor preferred over an inductive sensor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Inductive | Capacitive |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Eddy current loss in metal target | Change in dielectric/capacitance |
| Detectable materials | Metals only | Metals, plastics, liquids, wood, humans |
| Sensing range | 0.5–15 mm typical | 0.5–50 mm typical |
| Susceptible to humidity | Low | High |

**(2 marks for comparison table)**

A capacitive sensor is preferred when the target is **non-metallic** (e.g., detecting water level in a plastic tank, sensing grain in a silo, or counting pharmaceutical tablets) or when sensing **through a non-metallic wall** (e.g., liquid level through a glass container). Inductive sensors would fail in these cases because the target does not support eddy currents. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the construction and working of an **inductive proximity sensor**. Derive an expression for the inductance of its sensing coil. State two limitations of inductive proximity sensors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **capacitive proximity sensor** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitive sensor and explain how the change in capacitance is detected. List three applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Oscillator] → [LC tank with sensing coil] → [Detector/Schmitt trigger] → [Output stage] → [Load]
                                                                                       ↑
                                                                       [Metallic target damps oscillation]
```

The four blocks are: **(1) Oscillator** — generates a high-frequency AC signal (~100 kHz); **(2) Sensing coil** — produces an alternating electromagnetic field at the sensor face; **(3) Detector** — a Schmitt trigger that monitors the oscillation amplitude; **(4) Output stage** — typically a transistor switch (PNP or NPN, NO/NC) that drives the load. **(1 mark for block explanation)**

**Working (2 marks):**
When no target is present, the LC oscillator sustains a steady oscillation with amplitude $V_0$. When a metal target approaches, eddy currents induced in the target absorb energy from the field, reducing the coil's Q-factor and lowering the oscillation amplitude. The detector senses this amplitude drop and flips the output state, signalling "target detected."

**Derivation of Inductance (2 marks):**

$$B = \frac{\mu N I}{l}, \quad \Phi_{one} = B A, \quad \lambda = N \Phi_{one} = \frac{\mu N^2 A I}{l}$$

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{I} = \frac{\mu N^2 A}{l}$$

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Detects only metallic/conductive targets; cannot sense plastics, liquids, or wood.
2. Sensing range is short (typically < 15 mm) and is further reduced for non-ferrous metals (e.g., aluminium has a correction factor of only ~0.4).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction and Diagram (2 marks):**
A capacitive sensor consists of: an **active electrode** (the sensor face), a **reference electrode** (often the housing), an **oscillator** (~50 kHz–1 MHz), a **capacitance-to-voltage converter** (charge amplifier), a **threshold comparator**, and an **output stage**. The active electrode and the target (with earth/ground return) form the two "plates" of a variable capacitor.

**Working (2 marks):**
The sensor continuously excites the active electrode with a high-frequency signal and measures the capacitance of the resulting fringe field. When a target enters the field — regardless of whether it is metal, plastic, liquid, or biological tissue — the **dielectric constant** of the field region changes, altering the capacitance. The change is converted to a voltage and compared to a threshold. Crossing the threshold triggers the output.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
For a parallel-plate model with area $A$, separation $d$, and dielectric $\varepsilon_r$:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. As the target approaches, $d$ decreases and/or $\varepsilon_r$ changes, causing $C$ to increase. The change $\Delta C$ is detected by a charge amplifier whose output voltage is $V_{out} = \Delta Q / C_f$, where $C_f$ is a fixed feedback capacitor.

**Applications (1 mark):**
1. Liquid-level sensing in tanks (water, oil, chemicals).
2. Tablet/capsule counting in pharmaceutical lines.
3. Touch-screens and proximity wake-up in consumer electronics.
4. Material sorting on conveyor belts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the different types of **contact switches** used as tactile sensors in industrial automation. Explain the terms: pole, throw, operating force, and differential travel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the construction and operation of a **bumper sensor** used in mobile robots. Derive the force equation for a spring-loaded bumper. List two advantages and two disadvantages of bumper sensors compared to proximity sensors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Types of Contact Switches (4 marks):**
1. **Limit Switches:** Heavy-duty electromechanical switches mounted at the end of a machine's travel. Operated by a cam or lever; widely used in elevators, conveyor gates, and CNC machine guards.
2. **Micro Switches:** Snap-action switches with a small differential travel and high repeatability. Used in appliance interlocks (e.g., microwave door) and as the sensing element inside many limit switches.
3. **Reed Switches:** Two ferromagnetic reeds sealed in a glass capsule; close when a magnet approaches. Used in door/window security systems and bicycle speedometers.
4. **Whisker / Bumper Switches:** Spring-steel wire with a contact pad; deflection closes the circuit. Common in low-cost mobile robots.
5. **Tactile Dome Switches (Pill Switches):** A metal dome that snaps under finger pressure, providing tactile feedback. Used in keypads and remote controls.

**Terminology (3 marks):**
- **Pole:** The number of *separate circuits* a switch can control. SP = single pole, DP = double pole.
- **Throw:** The number of *contact positions* per pole. ST = single throw, DT = double throw (changeover).
- **Operating Force (OF):** The minimum force required at the actuator to change the switch state. Expressed in **Newtons (N)**. Typical values: 0.1 N (micro) to 5 N (industrial limit).
- **Differential Travel (DT):** The distance between the *operating point* (where the switch changes state during actuation) and the *release point* (where it returns during de-actuation). Small DT = high repeatability.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Construction and Diagram (2 marks):**
A bumper sensor consists of:
- A **compliant outer shell** (rubber or foam) that absorbs impact energy.
- An **array of contact elements** (whiskers, microswitches, or conductive strips) distributed along the bumper's length.
- A **signal conditioning circuit** that aggregates the contact signals into a single "collision" line.
- Often, a **pneumatic bladder** that converts impact pressure into a measurable pressure spike.

**Operation (2 marks):**
On collision, the outer shell compresses and deflects one or more of the contact elements. Each activated element contributes a binary signal; the aggregate indicates *where* the impact occurred (spatial resolution) and *that* it occurred (binary flag). The signal is sent to the controller, which typically triggers an **emergency stop (E-stop)** or a path-replanning routine.

**Force Equation (2 marks):**
Modelling the bumper as a spring-mass-damper system with stiffness $k$ and damping coefficient $c$:

$$F_{impact}(t) = k \cdot x(t) + c \cdot \dot{x}(t)$$

where $x(t)$ is the bumper's deflection and $\dot{x}(t)$ is its time derivative (velocity). The threshold for collision declaration is $F_{impact} \geq F_{threshold}$.

**Advantages and Disadvantages (1 mark):**

| Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|
| Wide-area coverage in a single sensor | Requires **physical contact** — cannot pre-empt collision |
| Low cost and simple interfacing | Mechanical wear and fatigue over time |
| Provides approximate **location** of impact | Limited force resolution (binary) |
| Fail-safe (contact failure = "no collision") | Not suitable for high-speed robots |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "tactile" with "proximity":** Many students write that "tactile sensors work without contact." This is **wrong** and immediately loses 1–2 marks. Tactile = contact required; Proximity = no contact.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivations:** In the inductance derivation $L = \mu N^2 A / l$, students often drop the unit annotations. KTU examiners *explicitly* deduct 0.5 marks for missing SI units in formula sheets.
>
> 3. **Using $\varepsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m inconsistently:** Always carry the value of $\varepsilon_0$ in the first step of a capacitance derivation, or you will lose the substitution mark.
>
> 4. **Claiming "inductive sensors detect all materials":** This is the single most common wrong answer in Part A. The correct statement is "inductive sensors detect **metallic** materials only."
>
> 5. **Ignoring the correction factor $f_{material}$:** In a typical problem where a sensor has a rated range of 8 mm on mild steel, students often compute the range for aluminium as "8 mm" instead of $8 \times 0.4 = 3.2$ mm. This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Tactile vs. Proximity:** Tactile = needs physical touch; Proximity = field-based, no touch.
> - **Contact Switch States:** NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed), CO/SPDT (Changeover).
> - **Switching Terms:** Pole (circuits), Throw (positions), OF (operating force, in N), DT (differential travel).
> - **Bumper Model:** $F = kx + c\dot{x}$; spring stiffness $k$ and damping $c$.
> - **Inductive Sensor — Core Principle:** Eddy current loss in metallic target damps the LC oscillator.
> - **Inductive Sensor — Frequency:** $f_{osc} = 1 / (2\pi\sqrt{LC})$; typically 100 kHz–1 MHz.
> - **Inductive Sensor — Limitation:** **Metals only**; range shrinks for non-ferrous metals (Al, Cu).
> - **Inductive Sensor — Formula:** $L = \mu N^2 A / l$; range = $S_{Fe} \times f_{material}$.
> - **Capacitive Sensor — Core Principle:** Change in dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ or gap $d$ modifies capacitance.
> - **Capacitive Sensor — Formula:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ (parallel plate); $C = 2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r L / \ln(b/a)$ (cylindrical).
> - **Capacitive Sensor — Strength:** Detects **any** material — metals, plastics, liquids, biological tissue.
> - **Capacitive Sensor — Weakness:** Sensitive to humidity and temperature; longer response time than inductive.
> - **Constant to Memorise:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.
> - **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Metal target → Inductive. Non-metal target (or liquid through non-metal wall) → Capacitive. Wide-area collision → Bumper. Precise end-stop → Contact/Limit switch.
> - **Industrial Frequencies:** Inductive oscillators typically 100 kHz–1 MHz; capacitive oscillators typically 50 kHz–1 MHz.
> - **Dielectric Constants to Remember:** Air ≈ 1, Water ≈ 80, Plastics ≈ 2–5, Human tissue ≈ 50–100.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor Displacement Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Displacement Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Semiconductor Displacement Sensor** is a transducer that converts a mechanical displacement (linear or angular) into a measurable electrical signal by exploiting the **piezoresistive effect** in semiconductor materials (predominantly silicon and germanium) or by using related solid-state phenomena such as the **Hall Effect**, **MOS capacitive variation**, and **photonic displacement sensing**. Unlike conventional metal-foil or wire strain gauges, semiconductor displacement sensors exhibit a resistance change that originates primarily from a change in the **carrier mobility** (and to a lesser extent, the band-gap deformation) when the crystal lattice is mechanically strained.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic is studied under Module 2 (Transducers for Mechanical and Physical Variables) because displacement is the most fundamental mechanical measurand from which derived quantities like force, pressure, torque, acceleration, and vibration are computed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:** "A semiconductor displacement sensor is a solid-state transducer that uses the piezoresistive, Hall, or field-effect property of doped semiconductors to convert a change in mechanical position into a proportional change in resistance, voltage, or current."

### 1.2 The Three Principal Variants Studied in the KTU Syllabus

1. **Piezoresistive (Semiconductor Strain-Gauge) Displacement Sensor** — the workhorse of the family. A silicon strain gauge bonded to or diffused into a flexure element (cantilever, diaphragm) experiences a change in resistivity when bent.
2. **Hall-Effect Displacement Sensor** — a permanent magnet is mechanically coupled to the moving object; a stationary Hall element senses the varying magnetic flux density and produces a Hall voltage proportional to position.
3. **MOS / MIS Capacitive and MOSFET-Type Displacement Sensor** — the displacement alters the gate capacitance or the air-gap, which modulates the threshold voltage of a MOSFET integrated on the same chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Relevance (Course Context):** Because silicon is biologically compatible, the same technology used here is also leveraged in **catheter-tip pressure sensors**, **neural probes**, and **endoscopic position trackers** — bridging this topic directly to Module 3–5 content on biomedical applications.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge-and-Wire Analogy

Imagine a small rectangular kitchen sponge with two thin copper wires pushed into its opposite faces, and a 9 V battery connected across the wires. The current is carried by water-soaked channels inside the sponge.

- **No pressure (zero displacement):** The internal channels are open. The current flow is at its baseline value, just like the unstrained resistance $R_0$ of the gauge.
- **Pressing the sponge (positive displacement ⇒ tensile strain along the length of the strain element):** The internal channels squeeze thinner in one direction and bulge in the other. The flow of electricity (carried by "droplets" analogous to electrons/holes) becomes more or less restricted, and the resistance changes dramatically. The change is **much larger** than it would be in a metal wire of the same dimensions, because in a sponge (semiconductor) the *internal structure* of the conduction path changes — this is the **piezoresistive effect**.

In a metal, only the geometry changes (slight thinning and lengthening). In a semiconductor, the **mobility of the charge carriers themselves** changes because the crystal lattice is being deformed, which warps the energy bands.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for the Exam:** *Metals change shape → resistance changes. Semiconductors change shape AND change mobility → resistance changes MUCH more.* This is why a semiconductor gauge factor is **50–200**, while a metal gauge factor is only **~2**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient of p-type silicon ($\pi_{l}$):** $\approx \mathbf{400 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}}$
- **Longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient of n-type silicon:** $\approx \mathbf{-100 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}}$
- **Typical Gauge Factor (GF) for silicon:** **50 to 200** (semiconductor) vs **1.8 to 4.5** (metal)
- **Young's Modulus of single-crystal silicon:** **$\mathbf{E \approx 1.7 \times 10^{11}\ \text{N/m}^2}$**
- **Electron charge:** $\mathbf{q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}}$
- **Operating temperature range:** **$-55\ ^\circ\text{C}$ to $+150\ ^\circ\text{C}$** (typical)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Piezoresistive Effect — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The piezoresistive effect is fundamentally a quantum-mechanical phenomenon. To make it accessible for KTU 2024 valuation, we present it as a chain of cause-and-effect steps:

1. **Mechanical Input:** A force $F$ is applied to a flexure element (cantilever or diaphragm) causing a small displacement $\delta$ at the point of interest.
2. **Strain Generation:** The displacement deforms the flexure, producing a mechanical strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ on the surface where the semiconductor gauge is bonded or diffused.
3. **Lattice Distortion:** The silicon crystal lattice is no longer at its natural inter-atomic spacing. Bond angles and lengths are perturbed.
4. **Band-Structure Warping:** The conduction and valence band edges shift and tilt in **k-space** (momentum space). The effective mass tensor of electrons and holes becomes anisotropic.
5. **Mobility Change:** Carrier mobility $\mu$ is altered — usually reduced under tensile strain along the direction of current flow for n-type silicon (and increased for p-type along $\langle 111 \rangle$ directions).
6. **Resistivity Change:** Since $\rho = 1 / (q \cdot n \cdot \mu)$, the resistivity $\rho$ changes significantly. The fractional change in resistivity $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is **directly proportional to the applied stress $\sigma$** through the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi$:
$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \, \sigma + \pi_t \, \sigma_t$$
7. **Resistance Change:** Combining geometry change and piezoresistive change gives the total fractional resistance change.
8. **Electrical Output:** A Wheatstone bridge (or constant-current source) converts the resistance change into a measurable voltage.
9. **Signal Conditioning:** Instrumentation amplifier, offset nulling, and temperature compensation yield a clean analog output proportional to $\delta$.

### 2.2 Comparison: Metal vs. Semiconductor Strain Gauge

| Property | Metal Gauge (Constantan) | Semiconductor Gauge (Si) |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge Factor (GF) | 1.8 – 4.5 | 50 – 200 |
| Hysteresis | Negligible | Small but non-zero |
| Temperature coefficient of GF | Low | High (must compensate) |
| Strain range | $\pm 0.3\ \%$ | $\pm 0.3\ \%$ (linear region) |
| Linearity | Excellent | Moderate; needs polynomial fit |
| Output level | Low (mV range) | High (50–100× metal) |
| Fatigue life | Indefinite | Indefinite (no creep in pure Si) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (batch-fabricated) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM INSTRUCTION:** All boundary conditions and sign conventions are part of the marks. State them in your answer, not just the formula.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strain (geometric) | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Stress–Strain (Hooke) | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa; $E$ = Young's modulus |
| 3 | Resistance of a uniform bar | $R = \rho \cdot L / A$ | $\Omega$ |
| 4 | Fractional resistance change | $\Delta R / R = (1+2\nu) \varepsilon + \Delta \rho / \rho$ | Both terms contribute |
| 5 | Total Gauge Factor (GF) | $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho) / \varepsilon$ | For Si, $1+2\nu \approx 1.6$, piezoresistive term dominates |
| 6 | GF dominated by piezoresistivity | $G_F \approx \pi_l \cdot E$ | Approximation for semiconductors |
| 7 | Piezoresistive relation | $\Delta \rho / \rho = \pi_l \sigma + \pi_t \sigma_t$ | $\pi_l$ = longitudinal, $\pi_t$ = transverse coefficient |
| 8 | Cantilever tip deflection | $\delta = F L^{3} / (3 E I)$ | $I = w t^{3} / 12$ for rectangular beam |
| 9 | Max surface strain on cantilever | $\varepsilon_{\max} = (6 F L) / (E w t^{2}) = (3 t \, \delta) / (2 L^{2})$ | At fixed end ($x = 0$) |
| 10 | Bridge output, 1 active gauge | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R) / 4$ | Quarter-bridge |
| 11 | Bridge output, 2 active gauges | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R) / 2$ | Half-bridge (adjacent arms) |
| 12 | Bridge output, 4 active gauges | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ | Full-bridge (max sensitivity) |
| 13 | Sensitivity of sensor | $S = V_o / \delta$ | V/m |
| 14 | Hall voltage | $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t$ | $R_H$ = Hall coefficient |
| 15 | Hall coefficient | $R_H = 1 / (n q)$ | n-type: negative, p-type: positive |
| 16 | Hall-sensor displacement | $V_H = (k \cdot I \cdot x) / (n q t)$ | For linear $B = k \cdot x$ region |
| 17 | Threshold voltage of MOSFET | $V_T = V_{T0} + \gamma (\sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F + V_{SB} \vert} - \sqrt{\vert 2\phi_F \vert})$ | For MIS displacement sensing |
| 18 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | If $d$ changes with $\delta$ |
| 19 | Sensitivity (capacitive) | $S_C = \partial C / \partial d = -\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d^{2}$ | F/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Trap Note (KTU Valuation):** Many students write $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ directly. The examiner expects you to *state the full form* $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ and then **identify which term dominates** for semiconductors. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Airbag accelerometers, MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) sensors, suspension displacement.
- **Industrial Process:** Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder position feedback, robotic arm encoders.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone gyroscopes and accelerometers, camera autofocus, inkjet printer head position.
- **Biomedical (Course-Specific Link):** Catheter-tip pressure sensors, MEMS-based cochlear implants, intraocular pressure (IOP) sensors for glaucoma, neural micro-probes for electrophysiology.
- **Aerospace:** Structural health monitoring, wing-flex sensors, satellite antenna deployment feedback.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Gauge Factor $G_F$ of a Semiconductor Strain Gauge

We start from the fundamental resistance equation of a uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$:

$$
R = \frac{\rho \, L}{A}
$$

We want to find the fractional change in $R$ when a small longitudinal strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is applied. We take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A
$$

Differentiating both sides (because the changes are infinitesimal):

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}
$$

Now we express $dA/A$ in terms of the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$. The lateral strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$ (Poisson effect, where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio). Since $A = w \cdot t$ (width $\times$ thickness), and both dimensions change by the same factor:

$$
\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \left(\frac{dw}{w}\right) = 2 \left(-\nu \varepsilon\right) = -2 \nu \varepsilon
$$

Substituting $\varepsilon$ for $dL/L$:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2 \nu \varepsilon = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\,\varepsilon
$$

Dividing throughout by $\varepsilon$:

$$
\frac{dR / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \frac{d\rho}{\rho}
$$

The left-hand side is **defined as the Gauge Factor $G_F$**:

$$
\boxed{\,G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\,\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}\,}
$$

For a metal like constantan, $\nu \approx 0.3$ and the term $\Delta \rho / \rho$ is negligible. Hence $G_F \approx 1 + 2(0.3) = 1.6$. The measured value is slightly higher ($\approx 2.0$) because of small $\Delta \rho / \rho$ contribution.

For single-crystal silicon with p-type doping along the $\langle 111 \rangle$ direction, the piezoresistive coefficient $\pi_{44}$ is extremely large. The piezoresistive term $(\Delta \rho/\rho)/\varepsilon$ alone can reach **100–200**, dwarfing the $1 + 2\nu \approx 1.6$ geometric term. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\,G_F^{\text{semi}} \approx \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\,\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_l \, E\,}
$$

The key takeaway is that the **extraordinary sensitivity** of semiconductor gauges is **not** geometric — it is a quantum effect on mobility.

### 3.2 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Piezoresistive Displacement Sensor (Quarter-Bridge Configuration)

**Setup:** A semiconductor strain gauge is bonded to a cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$. A displacement $\delta$ is applied at the free end. The gauge is connected as one arm of a Wheatstone bridge with excitation voltage $V_{ex}$.

**Step 1 — Strain at the gauge location.**

For a cantilever beam, the maximum bending strain occurs on the top surface at the fixed end:

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3 \, t \, \delta}{2 \, L^{2}}
$$

> This is a standard beam-bending result derivable from the curvature equation $\kappa = M / (EI)$ and the strain–curvature relation $\varepsilon = \kappa \cdot y$, where $y = t/2$ at the surface.

**Step 2 — Resistance change.**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon_{\max} = G_F \cdot \frac{3 \, t \, \delta}{2 \, L^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Bridge output voltage.**

For a single active gauge in a quarter-bridge (the other three arms are fixed precision resistors), the differential output is:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \frac{3 \, t \, \delta}{2 \, L^{2}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{3 \, G_F \, V_{ex} \, t}{8 \, L^{2}} \cdot \delta\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity of the sensor.**

$$
\boxed{\,S = \frac{V_o}{\delta} = \frac{3 \, G_F \, V_{ex} \, t}{8 \, L^{2}}\ \text{[V/m]}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**

Take $G_F = 100$, $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$, $t = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $L = 50\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}$:

$$
S = \frac{3 \times 100 \times 5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times (5 \times 10^{-2})^{2}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.75}{8 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.75}{0.02} = 37.5\ \text{V/m}
$$

For a $\delta = 0.1\ \text{mm}$ displacement, $V_o = 37.5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.75\ \text{mV}$. This is well within the measurable range of a low-noise instrumentation amplifier like the AD620.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hall-Effect Displacement Sensor Output

**Setup:** A permanent magnet is attached to a moving object. The magnet produces a flux density $B$ at the stationary Hall element. The Hall element has thickness $t_h$, carries a control current $I$, and has a Hall coefficient $R_H$.

**Step 1 — Hall voltage.**

The Lorentz force deflects charge carriers to one side, creating a transverse Hall voltage:

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t_h}
$$

**Step 2 — Hall coefficient in terms of carrier concentration.**

For a single carrier type with charge $q$ and concentration $n$:

$$
R_H = \frac{1}{n \, q}
$$

(For n-type: $R_H < 0$; for p-type: $R_H > 0$. The Hall sensor IC internally inverts sign.)

**Step 3 — Magnetic field profile.**

For a small bar magnet aligned axially, the on-axis $B$ is approximately linear over a small range $x_1 \le x \le x_2$ near the magnet's edge:

$$
B(x) \approx k \cdot x
$$

where $k$ is a calibration constant with units of $\text{T/m}$.

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain displacement.**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 1:

$$
V_H(x) = \frac{I \cdot k}{n \, q \, t_h} \cdot x
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_H = K \cdot x\,,\quad K = \frac{I \, k}{n \, q \, t_h}\,}
$$

This is a beautifully linear relation. The sensor's sensitivity $K$ can be tuned via the control current $I$ and the choice of semiconductor ($n$, $q$).

### 3.4 Derivation: Sensitivity of a Capacitive (MIS) Displacement Sensor

A MIS (Metal-Insulator-Semiconductor) structure can be used as a displacement sensor if the air-gap $d$ above the gate is allowed to vary.

**Step 1 — Capacitance formula.**

For a parallel-plate structure with air gap $d$ and oxide thickness $d_{ox}$:

$$
C = \left(\frac{1}{C_{ox}} + \frac{1}{C_{air}}\right)^{-1} = \left(\frac{d_{ox}}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{ox} A} + \frac{d}{\varepsilon_0 A}\right)^{-1} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_{ox}/\varepsilon_{ox} + d}
$$

**Step 2 — Sensitivity with respect to gap variation.**

$$
S_C = \frac{\partial C}{\partial d} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\left(d_{ox}/\varepsilon_{ox} + d\right)^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Linked threshold-voltage shift.**

Because $V_T$ depends on the total gate capacitance, a small change in $d$ produces a measurable shift in the MOSFET drain current. The full expression involves the work function difference and oxide charge, which are constant for a given device; only the $d$-dependent part of $C$ varies with displacement. This is the operating principle of many integrated MEMS pressure / displacement sensors such as the Bosch BMP series.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024 Examinations:** A 14-mark question may ask you to combine a Wheatstone-bridge analysis with a cantilever-beam strain analysis. The full chain is: $\delta \Rightarrow \varepsilon \Rightarrow \Delta R / R \Rightarrow V_o$. Marks are split across **all four links** — if you skip any one, you lose 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation:** Physical semiconductor crystals, cantilevers, and Hall plates cannot be drawn physically in Mermaid. Instead, we present the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** which is the accepted substitute in KTU 2024 Scheme assignments.

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Piezoresistive Semiconductor Displacement Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Displacement Delta] --> B[Flexure Element Cantilever or Diaphragm]
    B --> C[Strain Epsilon at Gauge Location]
    C --> D[Silicon Piezoresistor Diffused or Bonded]
    D --> E[Resistance Change Delta R]
    E --> F[Wheatstone Bridge with Vex]
    F --> G[Differential Bridge Output Vo]
    G --> H[Instrumentation Amplifier AD620]
    H --> I[Offset Null and Temperature Comp]
    I --> J[Calibrated Output Proportional to Delta]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Hall-Effect Displacement Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Moving Object Carries Permanent Magnet] --> B[Air Gap to Hall Element]
    B --> C[Linear Region of B field versus x]
    C --> D[Hall Element with Control Current I]
    D --> E[Hall Voltage VH Output]
    E --> F[Chopper Stabilized Amplifier]
    F --> G[Linearization and Calibration]
    G --> H[Buffered Output Voltage or Current]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of a Capacitive (MIS) Displacement Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Displacement Changes Air Gap d] --> B[Capacitance C of MIS Structure]
    B --> C[Threshold Voltage VT of MOSFET]
    C --> D[Drain Current ID Modulation]
    D --> E[Readout Circuitry CMOS Compatible]
    E --> F[Digital Code via ADC]
```

### 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations (Quarter / Half / Full)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q[Quarter Bridge]
        Q1[R1 Fixed] --- Q2[R2 Fixed]
        Q2 --- Q3[R3 Fixed]
        Q3 --- Q4[R4 Active Semiconductor Gauge]
        Q4 --- Q1
    end
    subgraph H[Half Bridge]
        H1[R1 Active] --- H2[R2 Active]
        H2 --- H3[R3 Fixed]
        H3 --- H4[R4 Fixed]
        H4 --- H1
    end
    subgraph F[Full Bridge]
        F1[R1 Active] --- F2[R2 Active]
        F2 --- F3[R3 Active]
        F3 --- F4[R4 Active]
        F4 --- F1
    end
```

### 4.5 Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Bridge Output Vo Few mV] --> B[INA Low Noise Amplifier Gain 100]
    B --> C[Active Low Pass Filter Cutoff 1 kHz]
    C --> D[DAC Offset Trim]
    D --> E[ADC 12 to 16 bit]
    E --> F[Microcontroller Linearization]
    F --> G[Display or Bus Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 Record Books:** A block diagram is worth **2–3 marks** in any 14-mark question. Always include at least one architecture diagram with clear labels and arrows showing energy/signal flow. The arrowhead direction must match the flow of *information*, not the flow of *power*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Rule (KTU 2024):** Part A carries 3 marks for definition/short-answer. You should write **3–4 sentences** including the defining equation and one application.

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set B, Modified]**  
Define the term **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge. Why is the gauge factor of a semiconductor gauge 50–200, whereas that of a metal gauge is only ~2? *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**  
The Gauge Factor $G_F$ of a strain gauge is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied mechanical strain:

$$
G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}
$$

In **metallic gauges**, only the geometric terms ($1 + 2\nu$) contribute; Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.3$ gives $G_F \approx 1.6$. In **semiconductor gauges**, the piezoresistive term $(\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$ is very large because applied strain warps the silicon band structure and alters carrier mobility. For p-type Si along $\langle 111 \rangle$, this term can exceed 150, giving an overall $G_F$ of 50–200.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**  
State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of semiconductor strain gauges over metal foil gauges. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Very high gauge factor (50–200 vs 2), giving larger output signal for the same strain.
2. Can be batch-fabricated as a monolithic part of a silicon flexure (MEMS-compatible), eliminating bond creep and hysteresis.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High temperature coefficient of gauge factor, requiring elaborate compensation.
2. Non-linearity at higher strains (above ~0.3 %) and reduced maximum strain range.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. The student answers either OR choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Piezoresistive Cantilever Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** A semiconductor strain gauge with gauge factor $G_F = 120$ is bonded to a cantilever beam of length $L = 80\ \text{mm}$, width $w = 10\ \text{mm}$, and thickness $t = 2\ \text{mm}$. A vertical displacement $\delta = 0.5\ \text{mm}$ is applied at the free end. The gauge is connected in a **quarter-bridge** with an excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate:
   (i) the maximum strain on the beam surface at the fixed end,  
   (ii) the resulting change in gauge resistance if $R = 120\ \Omega$,  
   (iii) the bridge output voltage, and  
   (iv) the sensitivity of the sensor in V/m. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the piezoresistive and Hall-effect principles for displacement sensing. Which one is more suitable for a **non-contact** application, and why? *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:** $G_F = 120$, $L = 80\ \text{mm} = 0.08\ \text{m}$, $t = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$, $\delta = 0.5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $R = 120\ \Omega$, $V_{ex} = 5\ \text{V}$.

**Step (i) — Maximum strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3 \, t \, \delta}{2 \, L^{2}} = \frac{3 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (0.08)^{2}}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-6}}{1.28 \times 10^{-2}} = 2.34 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\varepsilon_{\max} \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{(dimensionless)}\,}
$$

**Step (ii) — Resistance change:**

$$
\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_F \cdot \varepsilon_{\max} = 120 \times 2.34 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0281
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta R = 0.0281 \times 120 = 3.37\ \Omega\,}
$$

**Step (iii) — Bridge output voltage (quarter bridge):**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{5}{4} \times 0.0281 = 0.0351\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_o \approx 35.1\ \text{mV}\,}
$$

**Step (iv) — Sensitivity:**

$$
S = \frac{V_o}{\delta} = \frac{0.0351}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 70.2\ \text{V/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S \approx 70.2\ \text{V/m}\,}
$$

##### Model Solution to (b)

| Criterion | Piezoresistive Sensor | Hall-Effect Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Sensing principle | Change in resistance due to strain | Change in Hall voltage due to magnetic field |
| Contact with object | Requires mechanical coupling (gauge bonded) | **Non-contact** (magnet attached, no electrical contact) |
| Wear and tear | Subject to fatigue | No wear (no contact) |
| Sensitivity to small displacements | High but limited to flexure range | High over linear magnetic field region |
| Susceptibility to EMI | Low | Moderate (magnetic field immunity needed) |
| Temperature sensitivity | High TC of $G_F$ | Moderate (Hall coefficient varies) |

**Verdict:** The **Hall-effect sensor** is more suitable for a **non-contact** displacement application because there is no mechanical load on the moving object, no wear, and the magnetic coupling allows for **sealed, dust-free, and even submerged** operation (e.g., hydraulic cylinder position). Piezoresistive sensors, although more sensitive, require direct physical mounting of the gauge on the moving part.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Hall Effect + Cantilever Numerical Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** A Hall-effect displacement sensor uses an n-type InSb (Indium Antimonide) Hall element with carrier concentration $n = 2.4 \times 10^{22}\ \text{m}^{-3}$ and thickness $t_h = 100\ \mu\text{m}$. A control current $I = 5\ \text{mA}$ flows through the element. A small bar magnet produces a linearly varying flux density $B = 0.1 \, x$ (with $x$ in mm and $B$ in T) at the Hall element. Calculate:
   (i) the Hall coefficient $R_H$,  
   (ii) the Hall voltage for $x = 2\ \text{mm}$, and  
   (iii) the sensitivity of the sensor in V/m. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a **block diagram**, describe the working of a semiconductor cantilever-based displacement sensor. What is the role of the **Wheatstone bridge** in this configuration? *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:** $n = 2.4 \times 10^{22}\ \text{m}^{-3}$, $t_h = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $I = 5\ \text{mA} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}$, $B = 0.1 \, x$ with $x$ in mm.

**Step (i) — Hall coefficient:**

$$
R_H = \frac{1}{n \, q} = \frac{1}{(2.4 \times 10^{22}) \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})}
$$

$$
R_H = \frac{1}{3.8448 \times 10^{3}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_H \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{C}\,}
$$

**Step (ii) — Hall voltage at $x = 2\ \text{mm}$:**

First convert $x$: $x = 2\ \text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$. Then $B = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2\ \text{T}$ (since the formula uses $x$ in mm and the result is in T).

$$
V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t_h} = \frac{(2.6 \times 10^{-4}) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.2}{1 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
V_H = \frac{2.6 \times 10^{-7}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_H \approx 2.6\ \text{mV}\,}
$$

**Step (iii) — Sensitivity:**

For $B = 0.1 \, x$ (x in mm), the general Hall voltage in terms of $x$ (in mm) is:

$$
V_H(x) = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot (0.1 \cdot x)}{t_h} = \frac{2.6 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1}{1 \times 10^{-4}} \cdot x
$$

$$
V_H(x) = 1.3 \times 10^{-3} \cdot x\ \text{(V, with x in mm)}
$$

Converting to SI: $x$ in m, $V_H$ in V:

$$
V_H(x) = 1.3 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{3} \cdot x = 1.3 \cdot x\ \text{(V, with x in m)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S = \frac{dV_H}{dx} \approx 1.3\ \text{V/m}\,}
$$

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Block Diagram (describe in text form for written exam):**

$$
\text{Displacement}\ (\delta)\ \rightarrow\ \text{Cantilever beam}\ \rightarrow\ \text{Surface strain}\ (\varepsilon)\ \rightarrow\ \text{Semiconductor gauge}\ (\Delta R)\ \rightarrow\ \text{Wheatstone bridge}\ (V_{ex}\ \text{to}\ V_o)\ \rightarrow\ \text{Amplifier}\ \rightarrow\ \text{Output}\ V_{out}
$$

**Working Description:**

1. The free end of the cantilever is mechanically coupled to the object whose displacement is to be measured.
2. A displacement $\delta$ at the free end produces a bending moment along the beam, the maximum strain being at the fixed end where the semiconductor gauge is bonded.
3. This strain, via the piezoresistive effect, changes the resistance of the gauge: $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$.
4. The gauge is connected as one arm of a Wheatstone bridge. A stable excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the bridge.
5. The bridge converts the resistance change into a small differential output voltage $V_o$ given by $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$ for a quarter-bridge.
6. This millivolt-level signal is amplified, temperature-compensated, and presented as the final output proportional to $\delta$.

**Role of the Wheatstone Bridge:**

- Converts a small resistance change into a measurable **differential voltage**.
- Provides **temperature self-compensation** because all four arms experience the same ambient temperature drift, which is rejected by the differential readout.
- Allows the use of **multiple active gauges** (half or full bridge) to **double or quadruple the sensitivity** and to cancel common-mode non-linearity.
- Provides a convenient **null-balance calibration** point: at zero displacement, $V_o = 0$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $G_F$ with sensitivity $S$:** $G_F$ is dimensionless; $S$ has units of V/m. Examiners deduct 1 mark for unit errors.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/4 in a quarter-bridge:** A common error is to write $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ instead of $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$. This single mistake costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping the linearity assumption:** The piezoresistive relation $\Delta \rho / \rho = \pi \sigma$ is strictly valid only in the **small-signal linear region** ($\varepsilon < 0.3\,\%$). State this assumption.
> 4. **Sign errors in Hall coefficient:** For **n-type** material, $R_H$ is **negative**; in many commercial Hall sensor ICs, the output is internally inverted. State both the sign convention and the type of carrier.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** Beam dimensions in mm, displacement in mm, $V_{ex}$ in V, and $B$ in T must be in **SI** before substituting. Many students skip this and get numerically wrong answers.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 End-Semester Exam**

- Semiconductor displacement sensors are based on **piezoresistive**, **Hall-effect**, or **MOS capacitive** principles.
- The **piezoresistive effect** is a quantum-mechanical change in carrier mobility due to lattice strain, distinct from the purely geometric resistance change.
- **Gauge Factor** $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + (1/\varepsilon)(\Delta \rho / \rho)$ — geometric term is small; piezoresistive term dominates in semiconductors, giving $G_F$ of 50–200.
- For a cantilever beam, the maximum surface strain at the fixed end is $\varepsilon_{\max} = 3 t \delta / (2 L^{2})$.
- The output of a quarter-bridge is $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R/R)$; for a half-bridge it is doubled; for a full-bridge it is quadrupled.
- Hall voltage is $V_H = R_H \cdot I \cdot B / t_h$ with $R_H = 1/(nq)$.
- Capacitive (MIS) sensors exploit the dependence of $C$ and hence $V_T$ on displacement-modulated air gap $d$.
- **Temperature compensation** is essential because silicon has a high temperature coefficient of $G_F$.
- Wheatstone bridge also acts as a **temperature-compensating** differential readout.
- **Full-bridge configuration** gives the maximum sensitivity and is preferred for low-magnitude displacement sensing.
- Real-world applications span **automotive**, **biomedical** (catheters, implants, IOP sensors), **industrial robotics**, **consumer electronics**, and **aerospace structural monitoring**.
- Standard numerical constants to memorize: $G_F$ of metal $\approx 2$, of silicon $\approx 100$; $E_{\text{Si}} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Pa}$; $\pi_{44}$ of p-Si $\approx 400 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{Pa}^{-1}$.
- **Examiner trap words:** always write "**in the linear region**" after any piezoresistive formula, "**at constant temperature**" for $G_F$ and $R_H$, and "**small-signal assumption**" for the bridge output.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range Sensors - IR, Sonar, Laser Range Finder, Optical Triangulation (1D), Structured Light (2D), Performance Comparison of Range Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Range Sensors - IR, Sonar, Laser Range Finder, Optical Triangulation (1D), Structured Light (2D), Performance Comparison of Range Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Range Sensor?

A **Range Sensor** is a non-contact transducer that measures the **physical distance (range)** from the sensor to a target object in its field of view. In the context of KTU's *Biosensors and Transducers* syllabus, range sensors form the perception backbone of mobile robots, biomedical instrumentation, autonomous vehicles, and laboratory automation platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Textbook-Standard):**
> A *Range Sensor* is a device that converts a physical separation between the sensor and an object into a measurable electrical signal by exploiting a known physical phenomenon — such as the propagation of acoustic, optical, or electromagnetic waves.

### 1.2 The Two Grand Families of Ranging

| Family | Working Principle | Typical Physical Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** | Measures travel time of an emitted wave | $t$ (seconds) |
| **Triangulation / Geometric** | Measures geometric shift due to parallax | $x$ (pixel offset / spot displacement) |

Every sensor in this module — IR, Sonar, Laser, Optical Triangulation, and Structured Light — is a special case of one of these two families.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Blind Man's Cane

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine a blind man trying to estimate how far a wall is.
> - **Sonar** = He claps his hands and listens for the echo (sound wave). Echo delay $\Rightarrow$ distance.
> - **Laser Range Finder** = He flashes a laser pointer and waits for the photon echo (light wave). Extremely fast echo $\Rightarrow$ distance.
> - **IR Triangulation** = He shines a torch at an angle; the spot on the wall moves sideways as the wall comes closer (geometric parallax). Spot position $\Rightarrow$ distance.
> - **Structured Light** = He throws a patterned net of light and observes how the net deforms over the object's surface. Net distortion $\Rightarrow$ 3D shape.

This "blind man" mental model will help you intuitively classify any unfamiliar range sensor you encounter in the exam.

### 1.4 Why Range Sensors Matter in Biosensors & Transducers

In modern biomedical and robotic systems, range sensors act as **proximity transducers** that allow:
- Surgical robots to maintain safe operating distances from tissue.
- Prosthetic limbs to sense environmental obstacles.
- Automated pipetting/handling systems in diagnostic labs.
- Smart wheelchairs to navigate around patients and furniture.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants (Bolded for KTU Exam Recall)

- **Speed of Light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Speed of Sound in air (20°C):** $v_{s} \approx \mathbf{343\ \text{m/s}}$
- **Standard acoustic absorption loss at 40 kHz in air:** $\alpha \approx \mathbf{1\ \text{dB/m}}$
- **Typical IR wavelength (near-IR):** $\lambda \approx \mathbf{850\ \text{nm}}$ to **940 nm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infrared (IR) Range Sensor

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

An IR range sensor is a **compact, low-cost triangulation-based device**. It contains:
1. An IR **emitter** (LED, $\lambda \approx 850\ \text{nm}$).
2. A **position-sensitive detector (PSD)** or linear CMOS array.
3. A pair of **optical lenses** — one for emission, one for reception.

The IR beam strikes the target; the reflected beam's spot position on the PSD shifts in proportion to the target's distance. Closer target $\Rightarrow$ reflected ray strikes PSD at a steeper angle $\Rightarrow$ larger spot shift.

### 2.1.2 Why "Why" and "How"

- **Why IR?** — IR is invisible to humans, safe for eyes (Class 1), and cheaply produced by GaAs LEDs.
- **How it works** — By emitting a modulated IR pulse and measuring the *angle of the reflected ray*, NOT the time. Hence it is geometric, not ToF.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Many students confuse IR with LIDAR. IR range sensors are *short-range, low-cost, triangulation-based*. LIDAR is *long-range, laser-based, ToF-based*. They are not the same.

## 2.2 Sonar (Ultrasonic) Range Sensor

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

A sonar (ultrasonic) sensor works on **acoustic Time-of-Flight (ToF)**. A piezoelectric transducer emits an ultrasonic burst (typically **40 kHz**), and the same transducer (or a paired one) listens for the echo. The round-trip time $t$ is converted to distance.

### 2.2.2 Why and How

- **Why ultrasonic?** — Sound waves travel well in air, are unaffected by color, transparency, or lighting conditions, and are cheap.
- **How it works** — Single transducer alternates between transmit and receive modes (time-division). Round-trip time $t$ gives distance.

### 2.2.3 Beam Divergence

The acoustic beam spreads with an angle $\theta$ that increases with frequency and aperture:

$$\theta = 2 \cdot \arcsin\!\left(\frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{D}\right)$$

where $\lambda$ is the acoustic wavelength and $D$ is the transducer diameter. For a 40 kHz, 16 mm transducer, $\theta$ is typically **15°–30°**, which means a 1 m distant object is "seen" as a 30 cm wide cone.

## 2.3 Laser Range Finder (LIDAR)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

Two sub-technologies exist:
- **Pulsed Time-of-Flight (Pulsed LIDAR):** Measures $\Delta t$ of a laser pulse echo. Distance is large (km).
- **Phase-Shift (Continuous Wave) LIDAR:** Modulates the laser amplitude and measures phase shift $\Delta\phi$ between emitted and received signals. Distance is short–medium (≤ 100 m).

### 2.3.2 Why and How

- **Why laser?** — Light is $\sim 10^{6}$ times faster than sound, giving millimeter precision over hundreds of meters. The narrow beam (low divergence) gives excellent angular resolution.
- **How it works** — A laser diode emits a pulse; a fast photodiode (APD) detects the faint return; a high-resolution TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter) measures $\Delta t$.

## 2.4 Optical Triangulation (1D)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

A laser line or spot is projected onto a target at a known angle. A camera (or PSD) views the spot from a second known angle. The spot's image-plane position $x$ varies monotonically with distance $d$.

This is the *one-dimensional* (1D) version — it measures distance along a single line of sight.

### 2.4.2 Derivation Setup

Let:
- $b$ = baseline (distance between laser and camera lens).
- $f$ = focal length of the camera lens.
- $\alpha$ = angle between laser axis and baseline.
- $x$ = spot displacement on the image sensor (measured from optical axis).

The geometric relationship yields a single, elegant formula (derived in Section 3).

## 2.5 Structured Light (2D)

### 2.5.1 Operating Principle

A 2D pattern (stripes, grid, or pseudo-random dots) is projected onto the target surface. A camera observes the deformation of the pattern. By computing **disparity** between the projected and observed pattern, the system recovers a full 2D **depth map** (and with motion, a 3D point cloud).

### 2.5.2 Why and How

- **Why structured light?** — A single line of laser gives 1D distance. By projecting many lines (or coded patterns) simultaneously, the system gets a *dense* 2D/3D reconstruction in a single snapshot.
- **How it works** — The classic pipeline: **Project $\to$ Capture $\to$ Decode $\to$ Triangulate $\to$ Reconstruct**.

A famous example is the **Microsoft Kinect v1**, which used a pseudo-random IR speckle pattern to map depth across an entire room in real time.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear every year in KTU ESE and internal tests.**

| # | Sensor / Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-of-Flight (general) | $d = \dfrac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | $d$=distance, $v$=wave speed, $t$=round-trip time | m, m/s, s |
| 2 | Ultrasonic / Sonar | $d = \dfrac{343 \cdot t}{2}$ | uses $v_{s}=343$ m/s | m |
| 3 | Pulsed LIDAR | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot t}{2}$ | $c=3\times 10^{8}$ m/s | m |
| 4 | Phase-shift LIDAR | $d = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta\phi}{4\pi f_{m}}$ | $f_{m}$=modulation frequency | m |
| 5 | Optical Triangulation | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x}$ | $f$=focal length, $b$=baseline, $x$=image shift | m |
| 6 | Triangulation (general) | $d = \dfrac{f \cdot b}{x \cdot \sin\alpha}$ | $\alpha$=projection angle | m |
| 7 | Structured Light depth | $Z = \dfrac{f \cdot B}{D}$ | $B$=baseline, $D$=disparity | m |
| 8 | Beam divergence (any wave) | $\theta = 2\arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{D}\right)$ | $\lambda$=wavelength, $D$=aperture | rad |
| 9 | Range resolution (ToF) | $\delta d = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$ | $\Delta f$=bandwidth | m |
| 10 | Phase resolution limit | $\delta\phi = \dfrac{1}{\text{SNR}}$ | SNR in linear scale | rad |
| 11 | Max unambiguous range (sonar) | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{v_{s} \cdot T_{p}}{2}$ | $T_{p}$=pulse period | m |
| 12 | SNR (energy form) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_{r}}{N \cdot B}$ | $P_{r}$=received power, $N$=noise spectral density | unitless |

**Real-world utility:** The same triangulation equation $d = f \cdot b / x$ governs *confocal microscopes* (used in biomedical imaging), *laser profilometers* (used in dental crown scanning), and *industrial quality-control gauges*. Memorize it once — use it everywhere.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-of-Flight Range Equation (General)

**Starting principle:** A wave emitted at time $t = 0$ travels to a target, reflects, and returns at time $t = t_{\text{rt}}$. The total path length is $2d$, where $d$ is the one-way distance.

**Step 1 — Express total path length:**
$$2d = v \cdot t_{\text{rt}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for distance $d$:**
$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_{\text{rt}}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute wave speed:**
- For **sound in air** at 20 °C: $v_{s} = 343\ \text{m/s}$, so
$$d = \frac{343 \cdot t_{\text{rt}}}{2} = 171.5 \cdot t_{\text{rt}}$$

- For **light in air** (approximately vacuum): $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$, so
$$d = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \cdot t_{\text{rt}}}{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{8} \cdot t_{\text{rt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Sense Check:** For $d = 1\ \text{m}$ of sonar, $t_{\text{rt}} = 2 / 343 \approx 5.83\ \text{ms}$. For $d = 1\ \text{m}$ of LIDAR, $t_{\text{rt}} = 2 / 3\times 10^{8} \approx 6.67\ \text{ns}$. This is why LIDAR needs picosecond timing electronics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — 1D Optical Triangulation

**Setup:** A laser projects a beam at angle $\alpha$ from a baseline of length $b$ (laser–camera separation). The camera's optical axis is parallel to the baseline, and its lens has focal length $f$.

**Step 1 — Geometry in object space:**
The illuminated point $P$ on the target lies at perpendicular distance $d$ from the baseline. By definition of the projection angle:
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{d}{b_{p}}$$
where $b_{p}$ is the horizontal offset of $P$ from the laser. We then have
$$b_{p} = \frac{d}{\tan \alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Geometry in image space:**
The camera lens forms an image of $P$ at distance $x$ from the optical axis on the sensor. By similar triangles (thin-lens geometry):
$$\frac{x}{f} = \frac{b_{p}}{d} = \frac{1}{\tan \alpha}$$

Wait — let us set up the canonical form more rigorously. The standard 1D triangulation setup gives:

$$x = \frac{f \cdot b}{d \cdot \sin \alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $d$:**
Multiply both sides by $d \sin \alpha$:
$$x \cdot d \cdot \sin \alpha = f \cdot b$$

Divide both sides by $x \cdot \sin \alpha$:
$$d = \frac{f \cdot b}{x \cdot \sin \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Special case $\alpha = 90°$:**
When the laser is perpendicular to the baseline, $\sin \alpha = 1$, and the equation simplifies to the famous:
$$d = \frac{f \cdot b}{x}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the form examiners love. Always state the assumption ($\alpha = 90°$) explicitly when using it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Structured Light (2D) Depth from Disparity

**Setup:** A projector emits a known pattern; a camera offset by baseline $B$ captures the deformed pattern. The horizontal pixel shift between projected and observed pattern is the **disparity** $D$.

**Step 1 — Pinhole camera equation for a point at depth $Z$:**
The image-plane coordinate $u$ of a 3D point $(X, Y, Z)$ is
$$u = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to projector and camera (with baseline $B$):**
For the projector (treated as an "inverse camera" at $X = 0$):
$$u_{p} = f \cdot \frac{0}{Z} = 0 \quad \text{(reference)}$$

For the camera, displaced by $B$ along $X$:
$$u_{c} = f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} = f \cdot \frac{-B}{Z}$$

Wait, the more standard treatment is: the projector pixel coordinate for the same world point is $u_{p} = f \cdot X / Z$, and the camera pixel coordinate is $u_{c} = f \cdot (X - B) / Z$.

**Step 3 — Compute disparity:**
$$D = u_{p} - u_{c} = f \cdot \frac{X}{Z} - f \cdot \frac{X - B}{Z} = \frac{f \cdot B}{Z}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for depth $Z$:**
$$\boxed{Z = \frac{f \cdot B}{D}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inverse Proportionality:** As disparity $D$ grows, depth $Z$ shrinks. A nearby object produces a large disparity; a distant object produces a near-zero disparity. This is identical to the human stereo-vision principle.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Beam Divergence (Airy Disk Argument)

A circular aperture of diameter $D$ produces a far-field beam whose first minimum lies at angle:
$$\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 1 — The Airy disk formula** (Fraunhofer diffraction):
$$\sin \theta_{1} = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 2 — For small angles**, $\sin \theta \approx \theta$:
$$\theta \approx 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Practical full-cone angle** (peak-to-peak, not half):
$$\theta_{\text{full}} = 2.44 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Numerical example:** For a 5 mm laser aperture at $\lambda = 905\ \text{nm}$:
$$\theta = 2.44 \cdot \frac{905 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.44\ \text{mrad} \approx 0.025°$$

This is the reason LIDAR beams stay narrow over kilometers.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | IR | Sonar | Laser (LIDAR) | Opt. Triangulation (1D) | Structured Light (2D) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical wave** | Near-IR photon burst | Ultrasonic (40 kHz) | Pulsed laser (905 nm) | Visible/NIR laser line | Patterned light (IR/visible) |
| **Range** | 0.1–1.5 m | 0.15–10 m | 0.5 m – 200 m | 0.05–1 m | 0.5–5 m |
| **Resolution** | mm–cm | cm | mm–cm | µm–mm | µm–mm |
| **Update rate** | kHz | 20–100 Hz | 10–100 kHz | 1–10 kHz | 30–120 Hz |
| **Cost** | Very low | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Affected by sunlight** | Moderate | No | Slight | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Affected by color/shine** | Yes | No | Slight | Yes | Yes |
| **Cross-section sensitivity** | Lambertian OK | Specular problematic | Specular bad for shiny | Lambertian best | Lambertian best |
| **Multi-path / Crosstalk** | None | Severe (reverb) | Moderate | None | Pattern interference |
| **Eye safety** | Safe | Safe | Class 1/3R only | Class 2/3R | Class 1/2 |

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark question, this matrix is the *most efficient* 7-marker. Drawing it neatly (even by hand) instantly communicates full syllabus coverage.
