**Step 1 — Compute the derivative dR/dT analytically:**

Let $u = 3500 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{298}\right)$. Then $R_T = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{u}$, and:

$$\dfrac{du}{dT} = -\dfrac{3500}{T^2}$$

By the chain rule:
$$\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{u} \cdot \dfrac{du}{dT} = R_T \cdot \left(-\dfrac{3500}{T^2}\right)$$

Therefore:
$$\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = -\dfrac{3500 \cdot R_T}{T^2}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $R_T$ at T = 310 K:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{298} = \dfrac{1}{310} - \dfrac{1}{298} = \dfrac{298 - 310}{310 \cdot 298} = \dfrac{-12}{92380}$$

$$u = 3500 \cdot \dfrac{-12}{92380} = \dfrac{-42000}{92380} = -0.4547$$

$$R_T(310) = 10{,}000 \cdot e^{-0.4547} = 10{,}000 \cdot 0.6346 = 6346 \ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute dR/dT at T = 310 K:**

$$\dfrac{dR_T}{dT} = -\dfrac{3500 \cdot 6346}{(310)^2} = -\dfrac{22{,}211{,}000}{96{,}100} = -231.1 \ \Omega/\text{K}$$

The magnitude is $\vert dR/dT \vert = 231.1 \ \Omega/\text{K} = 0.2311 \ \Omega/°\text{C}$ (since 1 K = 1 °C for a temperature *difference*).

**Step 4 — Output voltage per °C for the Wheatstone bridge:**

For a balanced bridge with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_T$, the differential output is:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R_T}$$

Substituting:
$$V_{out} = \dfrac{5}{4} \cdot \dfrac{0.2311}{6346}$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \cdot 3.64 \times 10^{-5} = 4.55 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{V/°C}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 45.5 \ \mu\text{V/°C}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks derivative, 1 mark $R_T$ evaluation, 2 marks dR/dT, 2 marks bridge output.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently write $A = 100$ mm² in the pressure formula without converting to m². **Always convert all units to SI base units before substitution.**
> 2. **Sign Error in dR/dT (–1 mark):** For a thermistor, $dR/dT$ is **negative** (NTC type). Do not drop the negative sign in intermediate work, even if the final sensitivity magnitude is asked.
> 3. **Confusing Transducer with Sensor:** A *sensor* is the broader term (detects a measurand). A *transducer* is a sensor **that produces an electrical output**. Not all sensors are transducers.
> 4. **Forgetting the Bridge Output Factor:** A common error in Part B(b) of Question B is using $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / R$ instead of the correct $V_{ex}/4 \cdot \Delta R/R$ for a quarter-bridge with all four arms equal. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Misclassifying Active vs. Passive:** A piezoelectric crystal is **active**, but an LVDT is **passive** (it requires AC excitation). Do not mix them up in classification questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "What to memorize the night before the exam":**
>
> ✅ A **transducer** = physical input → electrical output. An **inverse transducer** = electrical input → physical output. (Same device, e.g., piezo crystal, can do both depending on operating mode.)
>
> ✅ **Active** transducers self-generate electrical output (no external power): Thermocouple, Piezo, Photovoltaic. **Passive** transducers require excitation: Strain gauge, RTD, LVDT.
>
> ✅ Key static characteristics to memorize: **Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Resolution, Span, Drift, Dead zone.**
>
> ✅ Key dynamic characteristics: **Time constant $\tau$, Rise time $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$, Settling time $t_s \approx 4\tau$ to $5\tau$, Bandwidth, Natural frequency, Damping ratio.**
>
> ✅ Piezoelectric formulas: $Q = d \cdot F$ (transducer mode), $\Delta x = d \cdot V$ (inverse mode). The same constant $d$ governs both — *reversible energy conversion*.
>
> ✅ LVDT is a **passive, displacement-to-voltage** transducer with excellent linearity and infinite resolution. The output is proportional to the core displacement.
>
> ✅ Strain Gauge Factor $G_F = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ — typical value for constantan ≈ 2.0, for semiconductor gauges ≈ 100–200.
>
> ✅ In biosensors, the **transducer** is the component that converts the bio-recognition event (enzyme-substrate, antigen-antibody) into a measurable electrical signal (amperometric, potentiometric, impedimetric, or optical).
>
> ✅ In **closed-loop therapeutic biosensors** (artificial pancreas, neurostimulators), the **inverse transducer** (insulin pump, stimulation electrode) completes the feedback loop.
>
> ✅ Always state **units** in your final answer. Always **box** the final numerical answer. Always draw a **neat, labeled block diagram** wherever the question asks for "with a neat diagram."
>
> ✅ KTU 2024 ESE marking: A 7-mark sub-part typically breaks down as **2 marks theory + 3 marks derivation/calculation + 2 marks for final result with units and conclusion.** Aim your answer length accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Position Transducers, Displacement Transducer - LVDTs, Captive Armatures, Unguided Armatures, Force-Extended Armatures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Position Transducers, Displacement Transducer - LVDTs, Captive Armatures, Unguided Armatures, Force-Extended Armatures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Position Transducers — Foundational Definition

A **Position Transducer** is a sensing device that converts the spatial location (linear or angular) of a measurable object into a proportional, standardized electrical signal (voltage, current, or digital output). Position transducers are the backbone of feedback control in robotics, CNC machinery, aerospace actuation, biomedical prosthetics, and industrial automation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A position transducer is a device which provides an electrical output proportional to the linear or angular displacement of a body with respect to a reference datum. It is also called a **displacement transducer** when used for measuring small linear movements.

### 1.2 Classification of Position Transducers

Position transducers are broadly classified based on the underlying transduction principle:

| **Class** | **Principle** | **Typical Devices** |
|---|---|---|
| Resistive | Resistance change with wiper motion | Potentiometric transducers |
| Inductive | Variation in self/mutual inductance | **LVDT**, RVDT |
| Capacitive | Change in capacitance | Capacitive displacement sensors |
| Optical | Light interruption/interference | Optical encoders, Moiré fringe sensors |
| Magnetic | Hall effect / magnetostriction | Hall sensors, magnetostrictive sensors |
| Ultrasonic | Time-of-flight of sound waves | Ultrasonic position sensors |

> [!NOTE]
> Among all these, the **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is the most widely tested topic under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 because it elegantly combines electromagnetic theory with practical displacement measurement.

## 1.3 LVDT — Formal Definition

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is a passive electromechanical transducer that converts **linear displacement** into a corresponding **alternating voltage** signal. It consists of one primary winding, two identical secondary windings, and a movable soft-iron armature (core). The output is the *difference* of the two induced secondary EMFs — hence the name **Differential**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Working Principle:**
> When the armature is displaced from its central (null) position, the mutual inductance between the primary and one secondary increases while it decreases with the other. This produces a differential voltage whose **magnitude** is proportional to displacement and whose **phase** indicates the direction of motion.

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw" of Magnetic Flux

Imagine two children sitting on opposite ends of a balanced see-saw:
- The see-saw pivot is the **null (zero) position** of the armature.
- Each child represents a **secondary winding** holding an equal share of magnetic flux (weight).
- When a heavy ball (representing the **armature**) slides along the see-saw, one side gets heavier while the other gets lighter.
- The *difference in tilt* tells you how far the ball has moved, and *which side it tilted toward* tells you the direction.

The LVDT behaves exactly the same way — but instead of weight, it distributes **magnetic flux linkage** between the two secondaries.

## 1.5 The Three Armature Designs — Quick Preview

| **Armature Type** | **Also Known As** | **Typical Stroke** | **Accuracy** | **Key Use** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Captive Armature** | Guided Armature | Short (±2.5 mm to ±25 mm) | Very High (0.1% FS) | Laboratory, aerospace |
| **Unguided Armature** | Free Armature | Long (±50 mm to ±250 mm) | Moderate | Industrial gaging |
| **Force-Extended Armature** | Spring-Extended | Very Short (±0.5 mm to ±5 mm) | High | Non-contact surface measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> The **force-extended armature** is the design most commonly used in biomedical and tactile sensing applications — a key reason it appears in the Biosensors & Transducers syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LVDT Construction — Component-Wise Breakdown

An LVDT has the following internal components:

1. **Primary Winding (P)** — Located on the central limb of a three-limbed (or hollow cylindrical) bobbin. Excited by an AC source of frequency **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (typically 5 kHz).
2. **Secondary Windings (S₁ and S₂)** — Two coils, identical in number of turns, wound on the outer limbs. They are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
3. **Armature (Core)** — A high-permeability soft-iron (often nickel-iron or mu-metal) cylindrical slug that slides along the bore.
4. **Coil Form / Bobbin** — Non-magnetic, insulating material.
5. **Shielding** — External mu-metal housing to provide EMI immunity.
6. **Cable & Connector** — For signal output.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

**Step 1 — Primary Excitation:**
The primary winding is energized by a stable AC source.

$$V_p(t) = V_{pm} \sin(\omega t)$$

where $V_{pm}$ is the peak primary voltage and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency of the carrier.

**Step 2 — Flux Generation:**
This AC current produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi$ inside the core.

**Step 3 — Coupling to Secondaries:**
The flux links with both secondary coils by mutual induction. If the armature is exactly at the **null position**, equal flux $\Phi$ couples with $S_1$ and $S_2$, producing equal EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$.

**Step 4 — Differential Output:**
Because the secondaries are in series opposition:

$$V_o = E_1 - E_2$$

At null: $V_o = 0$.

**Step 5 — Displacement Produces Imbalance:**
When the armature moves *toward* S₁, mutual inductance $M_1$ increases and $M_2$ decreases, making $E_1 > E_2$. The output becomes a sinusoidal voltage **in phase** with the primary.

**Step 6 — Direction Detection:**
When the armature moves *toward* S₂, $E_2 > E_1$. The output is **180° out of phase** with the primary. A phase-sensitive demodulator converts this AC signal into a bipolar DC voltage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — LVDT Master Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination):**

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary excitation | $V_p(t) = V_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$ | V | AC input to primary |
| Mutual Inductance (each side) | $M_i = k_i \cdot N_p N_i \cdot \mu_r$ | H | Coupling between P and $S_i$ |
| Secondary EMF (RMS) | $E_i = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_i \cdot \Phi_{max}$ | V | Transformer EMF equation |
| Differential Output | $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$ | V | Net output voltage |
| Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{dV_o}{dx}$ | V/mm | Output per unit displacement |
| Output Voltage (linear region) | $V_o = S \cdot x$ | V | Linear approx. |
| Phase detection | $V_o \propto +\sin(\omega t)$ or $-\sin(\omega t)$ | — | Direction indication |
| Percentage non-linearity | $\%\text{NL} = \dfrac{\vert \Delta V_{max} \vert}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | % | Linearity error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Design Constants (use as per KTU problems):**
> - Carrier frequency $f$ = **1 kHz to 10 kHz** (commonly **5 kHz**)
> - Full scale stroke = **±0.25 mm to ±250 mm**
> - Typical sensitivity = **0.5 V/mm to 5 V/mm**
> - Linearity = **±0.1% to ±0.5% of full scale**

## 2.4 Why LVDT is Preferred — Engineering Justification

- **Frictionless:** The armature does not contact the windings → infinite mechanical life.
- **Infinite Resolution:** Output is continuous, limited only by the detector electronics.
- **High Sensitivity:** Even sub-micron displacements are detectable.
- **Robust:** Operates in harsh environments (radiation, temperature, pressure).
- **Bi-directional:** Sign of displacement directly recoverable via phase.

## 2.5 Comparison with RVDT

The **Rotary Variable Differential Transformer (RVDT)** is the angular counterpart of LVDT. Its output voltage is proportional to angular displacement within a limited range (typically ±40°). Beyond this range, the relationship becomes non-linear.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Induced in a Secondary Winding of an LVDT

The mutual inductance $M_1$ between the primary and secondary $S_1$ is governed by the magnetic circuit. If the armature has permeability $\mu_r$, cross-sectional area $A$, and overlap length $l_1$ with winding $S_1$, then:

**Step 1:** Reluctance of the magnetic path through the armature (treating it as a uniform flux tube):

$$\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l_{total}}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$$

where $l_{total}$ is the total magnetic path length.

**Step 2:** Flux produced by the primary current $I_p$:

$$\Phi = \dfrac{N_p I_p}{\mathcal{R}} = \dfrac{N_p I_p \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l_{total}}$$

**Step 3:** Mutual inductance between primary and $S_1$:

$$M_1 = \dfrac{N_1 \Phi}{I_p} = \dfrac{N_p N_1 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l_{total}}$$

This shows that $M_1 \propto \mu_r$, which is why LVDTs use high-$\mu_r$ materials like **mu-metal** ($\mu_r \approx 50{,}000$).

**Step 4:** Apply Faraday's Law for an AC primary current $I_p(t) = I_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$:

$$E_1(t) = -M_1 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} = -\omega M_1 I_{pm}\cos(\omega t)$$

Similarly for $S_2$:

$$E_2(t) = -M_2 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt} = -\omega M_2 I_{pm}\cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Differential output (series opposition connection):

$$V_o(t) = E_1 - E_2 = -\omega (M_1 - M_2) I_{pm}\cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 6:** At null position, $M_1 = M_2 = M_0$, so $V_o = 0$. ✅

**Step 7:** Define the displacement sensitivity $\Delta M = M_1 - M_2$. For small displacements $x$ from null:

$$\Delta M = \left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right)_{x=0} \cdot x$$

**Step 8:** Combining, the RMS output voltage:

$$\boxed{V_{o,\text{rms}} = \omega \cdot I_{p,\text{rms}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{dM}{dx}\right)_{x=0} \cdot x}$$

This is the fundamental governing equation of the LVDT — **linear output vs. displacement**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sensitivity in Terms of Physical Design Parameters

Starting from the EMF equation for a transformer winding:

$$E_i = 4.44 \, f \, N_i \, \Phi_{max}$$

where $N_i$ is the number of turns of secondary $i$ and $\Phi_{max}$ is the peak flux linking it.

The differential output (rms):

$$V_o = 4.44 \, f \, (N_1 \Phi_1 - N_2 \Phi_2)$$

For identical secondaries, $N_1 = N_2 = N_s$, so:

$$V_o = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, (\Phi_1 - \Phi_2)$$

Let $\Delta\Phi = \Phi_1 - \Phi_2$. Assuming linear coupling variation with displacement $x$:

$$\Delta\Phi = K_\Phi \cdot x$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{V_o = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, K_\Phi \cdot x = S \cdot x}$$

Hence the **sensitivity**:

$$\boxed{S = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, K_\Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice that sensitivity is **directly proportional to frequency $f$**. This is why LVDTs are excited at kHz frequencies — to boost sensitivity without increasing physical size.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** An LVDT has 1000 turns in each secondary winding. The primary is excited by a 5 kHz sinusoidal source producing a peak flux linkage of $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Wb-turns variation per mm of armature displacement. Calculate the sensitivity and the output voltage when the armature is displaced by **3 mm**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the parameters:
- $f = 5\,\text{kHz} = 5 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz}$
- $N_s = 1000\,\text{turns}$
- $K_\Phi = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Wb/mm}$

**Step 2:** Apply the sensitivity formula:

$$S = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_s \cdot K_\Phi$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$S = 4.44 \cdot (5 \times 10^3) \cdot 1000 \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{-3})$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$S = 4.44 \cdot 5 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.2 \times 10^{3-3} = 4.44 \cdot 5 \times 10^3 \cdot 1.2$$

$$S = 4.44 \cdot 6 \times 10^3 = 26.64 \times 10^3$$

$$S = 26.64\,\text{V/mm}$$

**Step 5:** Output voltage for $x = 3\,\text{mm}$:

$$V_o = S \cdot x = 26.64 \times 3 = 79.92\,\text{V (peak)}$$

**Step 6:** RMS value:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{79.92}{\sqrt{2}} = 56.51\,\text{V}$$

✅ **Answer:** $S = 26.64\,\text{V/mm}$ and $V_{o,\text{rms}} = 56.51\,\text{V}$ at 3 mm displacement.

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Direction Detection (Phase Discriminator)

When the armature moves toward $S_1$:

$$V_o(t) = +\vert V_o \vert \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(in-phase with primary)}$$

When the armature moves toward $S_2$:

$$V_o(t) = -\vert V_o \vert \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(180° phase-shifted)}$$

A **phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** multiplies the output with a reference square wave at the carrier frequency $\omega$:

$$V_{dc} = \dfrac{2}{\pi} \cdot V_o \cdot \text{sign}(\sin(\omega t))$$

The output of PSD is thus a **bipolar DC** voltage:

$$\boxed{V_{DC} = \pm \dfrac{2}{\pi} V_o}$$

where the polarity indicates direction.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Three LVDT Armature Types

| **Parameter** | **Captive Armature** | **Unguided Armature** | **Force-Extended Armature** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Armature support | Attached to object via rigid rod | Free, no mechanical linkage | Spring-loaded with contact tip |
| Stroke range | ±2.5 to ±25 mm | ±50 to ±250 mm | ±0.5 to ±5 mm |
| Accuracy | 0.05% to 0.1% FS | 0.5% to 1% FS | 0.2% to 0.5% FS |
| Contact type | Contact/guided | Contact (object-guided) | Contact (spring-loaded tip) |
| Lifespan | Very high | High (depends on guide) | Limited by spring fatigue |
| Best for | Precision labs, biomedical force probes | Long-stroke industrial machines | Tactile sensors, surface profile |
| Null stability | Excellent | Moderate | Good (spring forces centering) |
| External force needed | Yes (from moving object) | No | No (spring provides it) |
| Application example | Aircraft control surface feedback | Hydraulic cylinder position | Skin-pressure mapping, palpation sensors |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LVDT System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete LVDT signal-conditioning chain — from primary excitation to digital readout — which is the conceptual flow tested in KTU 2024 Scheme exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OSC[AC Oscillator 5 kHz] --> EXC[Primary Winding P]
    EXC --> FLUX[Magnetic Flux in Core]
    FLUX --> S1[Secondary S1]
    FLUX --> S2[Secondary S2]
    S1 --> SUB[Series Opposition Subtractor]
    S2 --> SUB
    SUB --> AMP[Differential Amplifier]
    AMP --> PSD[Phase Sensitive Demodulator]
    OSC --> REF[Reference Signal]
    REF --> PSD
    PSD --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> DC[Bipolar DC Output]
    DC --> DISP[Digital Display or ADC]
    ARM[Movable Armature] -.-> FLUX
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **AC Oscillator** drives the primary winding.
- The **armature** modulates the flux linking the two secondaries.
- The **subtractor** generates the differential AC signal.
- The **PSD** converts it to a bipolar DC value whose polarity reflects direction.
- The **LPF** removes carrier ripple and produces clean DC.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Captive Armature LVDT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OBJ[Moving Object] --> ROD[Connecting Rod]
    ROD --> ARM[Guided Armature inside bore]
    ARM --> WIND[Three Winding Coil Assembly]
    WIND --> SIG[Signal Conditioning Electronics]
    SIG --> OUT[Displacement Readout]
    BEAR[Bearings and Guide Bushings] -.-> ARM
```

**Key feature:** The armature is mechanically linked to the object being measured via a precision rod, with bearings ensuring purely axial motion.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Unguided Armature LVDT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OBJ1[Target Object Provides Own Guide] --> ARM1[Free Floating Armature]
    ARM1 --> WIND1[Coil Assembly with Long Bore]
    WIND1 --> SIG1[Signal Conditioner]
    SIG1 --> OUT1[Long Stroke Displacement Output]
    NOTE1[No bearings inside LVDT body] -.-> ARM1
```

**Key feature:** The bore is elongated, and the LVDT has no internal bearings — the armature is pushed/pulled by the object's own motion system.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Force-Extended (Spring-Extended) Armature LVDT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPR[Compression Spring inside housing] --> TIP[Contact Tip Button]
    TIP --> SURF[Target Surface]
    SURF -.-> TIP
    TIP --> ARM2[Armature with extension rod]
    ARM2 --> WIND2[LVDT Coil Assembly]
    WIND2 --> SIG2[Demodulator]
    SIG2 --> OUT2[Tactile / Profile Output]
```

**Key feature:** A spring pushes the contact tip outward against the target surface. The tip retracts as the surface moves closer, changing armature position. This is the design used in **digital calipers** and **bio-tactile sensors**.

## 4.5 Working Region Diagram — Linearity Curve of LVDT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NEG[Negative Stroke] --> A1[Linear Region Left]
    A1 --> NULL[Null Position Zero Output]
    NULL --> A2[Linear Region Right]
    A2 --> POS[Positive Stroke]
    NON[Non Linear Region beyond working range] -.-> NEG
    NON -.-> POS
```

> [!NOTE]
> Inside the **linear operating region**, $V_o = S \cdot x$. Beyond this, the curve saturates and the relationship becomes non-linear — students often lose marks by assuming linearity everywhere.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define LVDT. Explain the function of the series-opposition connection of the secondary windings.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Linear Variable Differential Transformer (LVDT)** is an electromechanical transducer that converts linear displacement of a movable armature into a proportional alternating voltage.

The two secondary windings $S_1$ and $S_2$ are connected in **series opposition** — meaning their EMFs subtract rather than add. This connection is essential because:

1. At the **null position**, equal and opposite EMFs are produced in $S_1$ and $S_2$. They cancel out, giving **zero output voltage**. This forms the natural zero-reference of the transducer.
2. When the armature moves, the differential between the two EMFs becomes proportional to displacement.
3. The series-opposition output is **in-phase** for one direction of motion and **180° out-of-phase** for the opposite direction — enabling direction sensing via phase detection. *(3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for function)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between captive and unguided armature LVDTs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Captive Armature | Unguided Armature |
|---|---|---|
| Support | Attached to object via rod and bearings | Free, no internal guidance |
| Stroke | Short (high precision) | Long |
| Accuracy | Very high | Moderate |
| Use | Labs, biomedical | Industrial |

The **captive armature** LVDT provides extremely accurate readings because the armature's motion is fully guided and constrained. The **unguided armature** LVDT allows long-stroke measurements because the moving object provides its own linear guidance, eliminating friction inside the LVDT body. *(3 Marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, **answer any ONE full question** from the choice provided. Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an LVDT. Derive the expression for its output voltage.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 Marks):**

An LVDT consists of:
- A **primary winding** $P$ wound on the central portion of a hollow cylindrical bobbin, energized by AC source $V_p(t) = V_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$.
- Two identical **secondary windings** $S_1$ and $S_2$ wound on either side of $P$ on the same bobbin.
- A movable **armature** (soft-iron core) that slides coaxially inside the bore.
- The secondaries are connected in **series opposition**.

**Working (2 Marks):**

When the armature is at the **null (center)** position, equal flux links both secondaries. By symmetry, $E_1 = E_2$, so the differential output is zero.

When the armature moves toward $S_1$, mutual inductance with $S_1$ increases and with $S_2$ decreases. Hence $E_1 > E_2$, producing an output voltage **in-phase** with the primary. Conversely, movement toward $S_2$ reverses the phase.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**

The differential output (rms) is:

$$V_o = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, K_\Phi \cdot x = S \cdot x$$

where $S = 4.44 f N_s K_\Phi$ is the sensitivity and $x$ is the displacement from the null position. The phase of $V_o$ relative to the primary indicates direction.

---

**(b) An LVDT has a sensitivity of 2.5 V/mm. If the secondary coils each have 800 turns and the supply frequency is 4 kHz, calculate the peak flux linkage constant $K_\Phi$. Also find the RMS output when the armature is displaced by 4 mm.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**
- Sensitivity $S = 2.5\,\text{V/mm}$
- Turns $N_s = 800$
- Frequency $f = 4\,\text{kHz} = 4000\,\text{Hz}$
- Displacement $x = 4\,\text{mm}$

**Step 2 — Find $K_\Phi$:**

Using $S = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_s \cdot K_\Phi$:

$$K_\Phi = \dfrac{S}{4.44 \cdot f \cdot N_s}$$

$$K_\Phi = \dfrac{2.5}{4.44 \cdot 4000 \cdot 800}$$

$$K_\Phi = \dfrac{2.5}{14{,}208{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{K_\Phi = 1.759 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{Wb/mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage (peak, since sensitivity is typically given as peak):**

$$V_o = S \cdot x = 2.5 \times 4 = 10\,\text{V (peak)}$$

**Step 4 — RMS value:**

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{V_o}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{10}{1.4142}$$

$$\boxed{V_{o,\text{rms}} = 7.07\,\text{V}}$$

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023, Module 4]**

**(a) Compare captive, unguided, and force-extended armature LVDTs. Mention two specific applications for each.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Parameter | Captive | Unguided | Force-Extended |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stroke | Short | Long | Very short |
| Guidance | Internal bearings | Object-guided | Spring-loaded contact tip |
| Accuracy | 0.1% FS | 0.5% FS | 0.2% FS |
| External force | Required from object | Not required | Not required (spring provides it) |
| Contact | Mechanically linked | Linked to moving object | Direct contact via tip |

**Applications (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

**Captive Armature:**
1. Precision laboratory displacement gauges
2. Aircraft control surface position feedback

**Unguided Armature:**
1. Hydraulic cylinder piston position in earth-moving equipment
2. Long-stroke robotic arm joint measurement

**Force-Extended Armature:**
1. Digital calipers and micrometers
2. Bio-tactile skin sensors for palpation / pressure mapping

---

**(b) Explain how an LVDT detects the direction of armature motion. With a block diagram, describe the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Direction Detection (3 Marks):**

The two secondaries of an LVDT are connected in series opposition. When the armature is exactly at null, output is zero. For displacement toward $S_1$:

$$V_o(t) = +V_{om}\sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(in-phase with primary)}$$

For displacement toward $S_2$:

$$V_o(t) = -V_{om}\sin(\omega t) \quad \text{(180° out-of-phase)}$$

The **phase** of the output therefore encodes direction. A phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD) is used to convert this AC signal into a bipolar DC voltage.

**Phase-Sensitive Demodulator Operation (4 Marks):**

The PSD multiplies the LVDT output with a reference square wave derived from the primary oscillator:

$$V_{out} = V_{LVDT}(t) \cdot \text{sign}(\sin(\omega t))$$

After low-pass filtering:

- If $V_{LVDT}$ is in-phase: $V_{DC} = +\dfrac{2}{\pi}V_{om}$ → positive DC
- If $V_{LVDT}$ is out-of-phase: $V_{DC} = -\dfrac{2}{\pi}V_{om}$ → negative DC

The resulting **polarity of DC** directly indicates the direction of armature motion, while the **magnitude** indicates the displacement amplitude. The chain is:

$$\text{LVDT AC Output} \rightarrow \text{Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{PSD} \rightarrow \text{Low-Pass Filter} \rightarrow \text{Bipolar DC}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root of 2 conversion** between peak and RMS values — causes loss of 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 2. **Writing $V_o = V_{s1} + V_{s2}$ instead of $V_o = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$** — series opposition is *subtractive*.
> 3. **Confusing RVDT (angular) with LVDT (linear)** — a common trap question.
> 4. **Assuming linear behavior outside the operating range** — LVDT saturates non-linearly beyond its rated stroke.
> 5. **Forgetting the phase dimension in direction detection** — magnitude alone cannot tell direction; phase is essential.
> 6. **Using wrong units for sensitivity** — KTU expects V/mm or mV/μm consistently with the given displacement unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 — Position Transducers & LVDT**

- ☐ **Position Transducer** = device converting spatial location into electrical signal.
- ☐ **LVDT** = Linear Variable Differential Transformer = passive inductive transducer.
- ☐ Has **1 primary + 2 secondaries** + **1 movable armature** (soft-iron).
- ☐ Secondaries connected in **series opposition** → differential output.
- ☐ At **null** → output is zero; sensitivity $S$ is slope at null.
- ☐ Direction of motion is detected by **phase** of output relative to primary.
- ☐ **Sensitivity formula:** $S = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, K_\Phi$ (V/mm).
- ☐ **Output formula (linear region):** $V_o = S \cdot x$.
- ☐ Higher $f$ → higher sensitivity (kHz excitation preferred).
- ☐ **Captive armature** = guided by bearings → high accuracy, short stroke.
- ☐ **Unguided armature** = free, object provides guidance → long stroke.
- ☐ **Force-extended armature** = spring-loaded contact tip → non-contact tactile sensing.
- ☐ **RVDT** = rotary version of LVDT, ±40° linear range.
- ☐ Advantages: frictionless, infinite resolution, robust, bi-directional.
- ☐ Disadvantages: requires AC excitation + phase-sensitive demodulator.
- ☐ Primary excitation frequency is typically **5 kHz** (KTU default).
- ☐ Phase-Sensitive Demodulator → converts AC differential to bipolar DC.
- ☐ Beyond linear range → saturation → non-linearity error.
- ☐ Applications: bio-tactile sensors, digital calipers, aerospace feedback, hydraulic cylinder position.
- ☐ Numericals: always track units — convert kHz to Hz and mm to m if required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Transducers - LVT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Velocity Transducers - LVT # Module 4 — Velocity Transducers: Linear Velocity Transducer (LVT) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linear Velocity Transducer (LVT):** A passive inductive transducer that converts linear motion (velocity/displacement) of a ferromagnetic core into a proportional electrical signal (AC voltage), based on the principle of **mutual electromagnetic induction** between a primary winding and two symmetrically placed secondary windings, with a movable armature that alters the mutual coupling. In the strict KTU 2024 scheme context, the LVT is the **velocity-domain analog of the LVDT** (Linear Variable Differential Transformer). It contains the LVDT sensing element plus built-in **oscillator**, **phase-sensitive demodulator**, and **DC amplifier** stages, so its final output is a **DC voltage proportional to the velocity of the moving target**. The standard symbol convention is: $$\boxed{\text{LVT} \equiv \text{Linear Velocity Transducer (LVDT-based velocity output device)}}$$ ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Talking Coils" Machine:** > Imagine two identical megaphones (secondaries) facing each other, and a third person (primary) shouting from the center. When a soft iron "voice barrier" (core) is **exactly in the middle**, both megaphones receive equal volume — the net signal is **zero** (balanced condition). > If the barrier slides toward Megaphone A, A gets *less* voice and Megaphone B gets *more*. The **difference** in loudness is proportional to **how far** the barrier moved. Connect a "speedometer" (differentiation circuit) to this difference, and you instantly know **how fast** the barrier is moving. That speedometer-equipped machine is your LVT. In one line: *LVT = Two Coils (S₁, S₂) + One Driving Coil (P) + Moving Iron Core + Built-in Velocity Decoder.* ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |----------|--------|------------------------| | Primary excitation frequency | $f_{exc}$ | **50 Hz – 20 kHz** (typically **2–5 kHz** for industrial LVT) | | Excitation amplitude | $V_p$ | **1–24 V (rms)** | | Carrier (primary) supply | — | **A stable sine-wave oscillator** (built-in) | | Output (differential) | $E_o$ | **millivolts to volts (AC)** | | Output (after demodulation) | $V_{DC}$ | **0–10 V DC** (proportional to velocity) | | Core material | — | **Soft iron / Mu-metal / Nickel-iron alloy (high μ)** | | Stroke range | $x$ | **±0.1 mm to ±500 mm** (model dependent) | | Sensitivity | $K_v$ | typically **mV/(mm/s)** | ### 1.4 Classification of LVT (in the Transducer Family Tree) ``` Transducer └── Active / Passive └── Passive — Electrical Parameter Change └── Inductive Transducer └── Mutual-Induction Type ├── LVDT (output ∝ displacement) └── LVT (output ∝ velocity ← this topic) ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why is LVT called *passive*?** Because it **does not generate** its own EMF — it needs an external AC excitation to operate. The output is purely a *modulation* of the supplied carrier. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Layered Logic The LVT operates on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** combined with the **transformer principle**. The conversion chain is split into **five discrete stages**; understanding each stage is the key to mastering the KTU long-answer on this topic. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage A — Carrier Injection:** A built-in sine-wave oscillator feeds a stable, fixed-frequency, fixed-amplitude AC voltage $V_p$ into the **primary winding (P)** wound on a cylindrical former. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage B — Flux Distribution:** The primary current $I_p$ produces an alternating magnetic flux $\phi_p$. The flux divides itself between the **two secondary paths** through the **movable soft-iron core**. The core acts as a **low-reluctance flux guide**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage C — Differential EMF Generation:** Each secondary winding $S_1$ and $S_2$ is wound in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs are subtracted by the winding direction). The induced EMFs are: > $$E_{s1} = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dt}, \qquad E_{s2} = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi_2}{dt}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage D — Output = Difference:** The net output is the **differential EMF**: > $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = -N_s \dfrac{d}{dt}(\phi_1 - \phi_2)$$ > For a balanced LVT, $E_o$ is **proportional to displacement** $x$ of the core from the null position. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stage E — Velocity Conversion (the LVT magic step):** A built-in **active differentiator / phase-sensitive demodulator + integrator pair** converts the displacement-proportional signal into a **velocity-proportional DC output**: > $$V_{out} = K_v \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = K_v \cdot v(t)$$ > where $K_v$ is the **velocity sensitivity constant** of the LVT. ## 2.2 The Three Operating Zones | Zone | Core Position | Flux in $S_1$ vs. $S_2$ | Differential Output $E_o$ | |------|----------------|--------------------------|----------------------------| | **Null / Center** | Core at geometric center | $\phi_1 = \phi_2$ | $E_o = 0$ (theoretically) | | **Positive Displacement** | Core shifted toward $S_1$ | $\phi_1 > \phi_2$ | $E_o > 0$ | | **Negative Displacement** | Core shifted toward $S_2$ | $\phi_1 < \phi_2$ | $E_o < 0$ | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MUST MEMORISE FOR KTU ESE** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $E_p = 4.44 \, f \, N_p \, \phi_{max}$ | Primary induced EMF (transformer EMF) | V | | 2 | $E_{s1} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_1$ | EMF in secondary-1 | V | | 3 | $E_{s2} = 4.44 \, f \, N_s \, \phi_2$ | EMF in secondary-2 | V | | 4 | $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = K_d \cdot x$ | Displacement-proportional output (LVDT core output) | V (rms) | | 5 | $K_d = \dfrac{dE_o}{dx} = \dfrac{V/mm}{\text{displacement sensitivity}}$ | Displacement sensitivity | V/mm | | 6 | $V_{out} = K_v \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ | LVT final velocity-proportional DC output | V | | 7 | $K_v = K_d \cdot \tau$ | Velocity sensitivity (with differentiator time constant $\tau$) | V·s/mm | | 8 | $E_o(\text{peak}) = 2 \pi f \, N_s \, \phi_{net}$ | Peak output when core is at one extreme | V | | 9 | $\phi_{net} = \phi_1 - \phi_2 = K_\phi \cdot x$ | Net flux vs. displacement | Wb | | 10 | $\text{Linearity error} \, (\%) = \dfrac{E_{actual} - E_{ideal}}{E_{FS}} \times 100$ | Non-linearity measure | % | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In KTU valuation, if you write $|E_o|$ using the pipe symbol, the parser will eat your table. Use $\vert E_o \vert$ or $E_o$ magnitude notation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | LVT Application | |--------|-----------------| | **Biomedical** | Measuring skin/limb vibration velocity, blood-flow velocity (with mechanical coupling) | | **Industrial Automation** | Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder velocity feedback, CNC machine tool axis control | | **Aerospace** | Flutter testing, vibration velocity monitoring of airframes | | **Civil Engineering** | Seismic velocity pickups, bridge-deck vibration velocity | | **Robotics** | Actuator velocity closed-loop control | | **Process Control** | Mass-flow rate, conveyor-belt speed, vibration analysis in rotating machinery | > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiner's hot line:** Whenever you draw a block diagram, label the **oscillator → primary → core → secondaries → demodulator → amplifier → output** chain. This single block diagram, drawn correctly, can fetch 4–5 marks on its own. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Primary EMF and Mutual Flux Linkage **Given:** - Primary winding has $N_p$ turns. - Primary excitation: $V_p(t) = V_{pm} \sin(\omega t)$. - Core position $x$ measured from the null (centre) position. - Mutual inductance between primary and each secondary is a function of $x$: $$M_1(x) = M_0 + K_m x \quad ; \quad M_2(x) = M_0 - K_m x$$ - where $K_m$ is the **mutual-inductance slope** (H/m). **Step 1 — Primary current.** Assuming the primary winding is fed by a stiff voltage source and its own resistance/inductance are part of the excitation circuit, the primary current is approximately: $$i_p(t) = \dfrac{V_{pm}}{Z_p} \sin(\omega t - \theta_p)$$ where $Z_p$ is the primary impedance. For an idealised analysis we take $i_p(t) = I_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$. **Step 2 — Flux generated by the primary.** The flux in the primary is: $$\phi_p(t) = \dfrac{N_p \, i_p(t)}{\mathcal{R}}$$ with $\mathcal{R}$ the magnetic reluctance of the core path. We do not need its absolute value, only the alternating component. **Step 3 — Induced EMF in secondary-1.** By Faraday's Law: $$E_{s1}(t) = -M_1(x) \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$$ Substituting $i_p(t) = I_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$: $$E_{s1}(t) = -M_1(x) \, I_{pm} \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Induced EMF in secondary-2.** Similarly: $$E_{s2}(t) = -M_2(x) \, I_{pm} \, \omega \cos(\omega t)$$ **Step 5 — Differential output (the LVDT equation).** $$E_o(t) = E_{s1}(t) - E_{s2}(t) = -\omega I_{pm}\cos(\omega t) \, \big[M_1(x) - M_2(x)\big]$$ **Step 6 — Substitute the position-dependent mutual inductances.** $$M_1(x) - M_2(x) = (M_0 + K_m x) - (M_0 - K_m x) = 2 K_m x$$ **Step 7 — Final displacement-proportional output.** $$\boxed{E_o(t) = -2 \, \omega \, I_{pm} \, K_m \, x \cos(\omega t)}$$ The **rms amplitude** is: $$E_{o,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{2} \, \omega \, I_{pm} \, K_m \, \vert x \vert$$ This shows that the output amplitude is **linearly proportional to the core displacement $x$** — the foundational LVDT/LVT relationship. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — From LVDT (Displacement) to LVT (Velocity) **Given:** The LVDT output $E_o(t)$ is an amplitude-modulated carrier: $$E_o(t) = E_c \cos(\omega t)$$ where the carrier amplitude $E_c = -2 \omega I_{pm} K_m x$ is proportional to $x$. **Step 1 — A phase-sensitive demodulator recovers the envelope.** After synchronous (phase-sensitive) demodulation with the same reference carrier and low-pass filtering, the output is: $$V_{env}(t) = K_d \cdot x(t)$$ where $K_d = 2 \omega I_{pm} K_m / \pi$ is the **displacement sensitivity** of the demodulated LVDT chain. **Step 2 — An ideal differentiator produces velocity.** A passive RC differentiator with time constant $\tau = RC$ produces: $$V_{out}(t) = -\tau \dfrac{dV_{env}}{dt} = -\tau K_d \dfrac{dx}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Recognising $v = dx/dt$:** $$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -K_v \, v(t), \qquad K_v = \tau K_d}$$ The negative sign indicates direction (convention dependent on wiring). The **magnitude** is what matters for engineering readings: $$\vert V_{out} \vert = K_v \cdot \vert v(t) \vert$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sensitivity and Resolution **Given:** Sensitivity $K_v$ in V/(mm/s), with full-scale velocity $v_{FS}$ and full-scale output $V_{FS}$. **Step 1 — Defining the full-scale range.** $$V_{FS} = K_v \cdot v_{FS}$$ **Step 2 — Resolution (smallest detectable velocity).** Limited by the **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**. For an LVT with output noise $V_{noise}$: $$v_{min} = \dfrac{V_{noise}}{K_v}$$ **Step 3 — Linearity error over the stroke.** For a symmetric LVT, the output is linear to within $\pm 0.5\%$ over the rated stroke. The non-linearity equation is: $$\%NL = \dfrac{V_{measured} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$$ A typical high-grade LVT has: $$\%NL \le 0.1\% \, \text{of full scale}$$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LVT vs. Other Velocity Transducers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently asks *“Compare LVT with other velocity transducers.”* Memorise the table below. | Parameter | **LVT (LVDT-based)** | **Tachogenerator (DC)** | **Piezoelectric Velocity Pickup** | **Moving-coil (Electrodynamic)** | |-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|-----------------------------------|----------------------------------| | **Operating principle** | Mutual induction + differentiator | Faraday's law (rotational) | Piezoelectric effect | Electromagnetic induction | | **Output type** | DC voltage (built-in) | DC voltage | AC charge/voltage | AC voltage | | **Frequency range** | **0 – 1 kHz** | 0 – 100 Hz | **1 Hz – 10 kHz** | 0.5 Hz – 500 Hz | | **Linearity** | Excellent ($\pm 0.5\%$) | Good ($\pm 1\%$) | Moderate | Moderate | | **Ruggedness** | Very High | Moderate (brush wear) | Very High | Moderate | | **Power requirement** | Needs built-in oscillator | Self-generating | None | None | | **Direction sensing** | Yes (PSD) | Yes (polarity) | No (envelope) | Yes (phase) | | **Stroke / Range** | Wide (mm to m) | Unlimited rotation | Limited | Limited | | **Cost** | Medium-High | Medium | High | Low-Medium | | **Best for** | **Linear velocity** in lab/industrial | **Rotational speed** | **Vibration** measurements | **Seismic / sound** velocity | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** An LVT has a primary excitation of 5 V (rms) at 2 kHz, $N_s = 1000$ turns on each secondary, and a mutual-inductance slope $K_m = 0.5$ mH/mm. The built-in differentiator has a time constant $\tau = 0.1$ s. The core is moving at $v = 10$ mm/s. Find: > (a) the displacement sensitivity $K_d$ in mV/mm. > (b) the velocity sensitivity $K_v$ in mV/(mm/s). > (c) the output DC voltage for $v = 10$ mm/s. **Solution:** **Part (a):** From Derivation 1, the peak EMF per mm of displacement is: $$E_{o,peak} = 2 \, \omega \, I_{pm} \, K_m$$ Assuming $I_{pm} \approx V_p / \vert Z_p \vert$ and taking a working primary current $I_{pm} = 5$ mA = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ A: $$E_{o,peak} = 2 \times (2\pi \times 2000) \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$E_{o,peak} = 2 \times 12566 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$E_{o,peak} = 0.0628 \text{ V} = 62.8 \text{ mV (peak)}$$ The **rms** is $62.8 / \sqrt{2} \approx 44.4$ mV rms/mm. So: $$\boxed{K_d \approx 44.4 \text{ mV (rms)/mm}}$$ **Part (b):** $$K_v = \tau \cdot K_d = 0.1 \times 44.4 = 4.44 \text{ mV·s/mm}$$ $$\boxed{K_v = 4.44 \text{ mV/(mm/s)}}$$ **Part (c):** $$V_{out} = K_v \times v = 4.44 \times 10 = 44.4 \text{ mV DC}$$ $$\boxed{V_{out} = 44.4 \text{ mV DC}}$$ **Interpretation:** A core moving at 10 mm/s produces a **44.4 mV DC** output. The polarity (sign) tells the direction of motion. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LVT Constructional Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Oscillator
2 to 5 kHz] -->|V_p sine wave| B[Primary Winding P
N_p turns on former] B -->|Flux phi| C[Magnetic Core
Soft iron armature
position x] C -->|Flux phi1| D[Secondary Winding S1
N_s turns] C -->|Flux phi2| E[Secondary Winding S2
N_s turns in series opposition] D --> F[Series Subtraction Node
E_s1 minus E_s2] E --> F F -->|E_o modulated carrier| G[Phase Sensitive
Demodulator PSD] A -->|Reference carrier| G G -->|Envelope signal| H[Low Pass Filter
LPF] H -->|Displacement proportional V_env| I[Active Differentiator
RC time constant tau] I -->|Velocity proportional signal| J[DC Amplifier] J --> K[DC Output V_out
0 to 10 V DC] K --> L[Display or
Controller] ``` ## 4.2 Operating Principle — Displacement vs. Output Map ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Core at Null Position] --> Q0[phi1 equals phi2] Q0 --> R0[E_o equals 0 volts] P1[Core displaced +x toward S1] --> Q1[phi1 greater than phi2] Q1 --> R1[E_o positive AC voltage] P2[Core displaced minus x toward S2] --> Q2[phi1 less than phi2] Q2 --> R2[E_o negative AC voltage] R1 --> S1[After PSD and Demodulation] R2 --> S1 S1 --> S2[Envelope gives x of t] S2 --> S3[Differentiator gives dx by dt] S3 --> S4[Final DC voltage equals K_v times v of t] ``` ## 4.3 LVT Signal Chain — Frequency-Domain Picture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mechanical Input
v of t at low frequency f_m] --> B[Differentiator
j omega operator] B --> C[Modulated Carrier
frequency omega c] C --> D[Demodulator
multiplies by carrier] D --> E[Baseband Velocity
DC plus f_m] ``` ## 4.4 Pin / Terminal Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LVTunit[LVT Transducer Module] T1[Pin 1: Primary High] T2[Pin 2: Primary Low] T3[Pin 3: S1 Output] T4[Pin 4: S2 Output] T5[Pin 5: Common Ground] T6[Pin 6: plus 15 V DC supply] T7[Pin 7: minus 15 V DC supply] T8[Pin 8: V_out signal] T9[Pin 9: V_out ground] end T1 --> OSC[External Oscillator] T2 --> OSC T3 --> PSD[Internal PSD] T4 --> PSD T6 --> REG[Internal Regulator] T7 --> REG PSD --> DIFF[Internal Differentiator] DIFF --> AMP[DC Amplifier] AMP --> T8 REG --> AMP REG --> PSD REG --> DIFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note on Mermaid safety:** All node IDs above are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `T1`, `OSC`, `LVTunit`) — no reserved keywords used as standalone node names. Labels use plain text without bold or italics inside the quotes to prevent parser errors. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** **Define a Linear Velocity Transducer (LVT). State its working principle.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Linear Velocity Transducer (LVT) is a passive inductive transducer that converts the **linear velocity** of a movable ferromagnetic core into a **proportional DC output voltage**. **Working principle:** It is based on **mutual electromagnetic induction** between a primary winding and two identical secondary windings (placed on either side of the primary), with a movable soft-iron core altering the flux linkage. The difference of the two secondary EMFs gives a carrier signal whose envelope is proportional to **displacement**; this is then electronically **differentiated** to obtain a DC output proportional to **velocity**. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Bank]** **What is the role of the phase-sensitive demodulator in an LVT?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD) performs three functions: 1. **Synchronous rectification:** It multiplies the modulated LVDT output with a reference signal at the same carrier frequency and phase as the primary excitation. 2. **Direction sensing:** It preserves the **sign** (polarity) of the envelope, so the LVT can distinguish between +x and −x motion. 3. **Envelope recovery:** After low-pass filtering, the output is a clean DC voltage whose **magnitude is proportional to displacement** and **sign indicates direction**. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]** **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Linear Velocity Transducer (LVT). Discuss how the displacement-proportional LVDT output is converted into a velocity-proportional DC output. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Construction (3 marks):** An LVT consists of: 1. A **cylindrical hollow former** (bobbin) on which three coils are wound — one primary $P$ at the centre and two identical secondaries $S_1$ and $S_2$ on either side, connected in **series opposition**. 2. A **movable soft-iron core** (armature) attached to the object whose velocity is to be measured. The core slides inside the former. 3. **Built-in electronics:** a stable **sine-wave oscillator** (typically 2–5 kHz), a **phase-sensitive demodulator**, a **low-pass filter**, a **differentiator (RC active)**, and a **DC amplifier** — all housed within the transducer casing. 4. **Shielding:** a Mu-metal case to provide immunity from external magnetic interference. **Working (4 marks):** 1. The internal oscillator applies a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency AC voltage $V_p$ to the primary $P$. 2. The resulting alternating flux $\phi$ links both secondaries through the soft-iron core. 3. When the core is at the **null (centre)** position, the flux through $S_1$ and $S_2$ is identical: $\phi_1 = \phi_2$. The differential EMF $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = 0$. 4. When the core moves by $+x$ towards $S_1$, more flux links $S_1$ than $S_2$ ($\phi_1 > \phi_2$), and $E_o$ becomes **positive** AC. When the core moves by $-x$ towards $S_2$, $E_o$ becomes **negative** AC. 5. The **amplitude** of $E_o$ is proportional to $x$ (displacement), while the **phase** indicates the direction of motion. 6. The **PSD** multiplies $E_o$ with the reference carrier and produces a baseband signal whose DC level = displacement. 7. The **differentiator** computes $dx/dt = v$, so the final amplified DC output is $V_{out} = K_v \cdot v(t)$. --- **(b) An LVT is excited at 3 kHz with a 6 V rms primary supply. The number of turns on each secondary is 1500, the mutual-inductance slope is $K_m = 0.8$ mH/mm, and the primary current is approximately 8 mA. The differentiator time constant is $\tau = 50$ ms. A core moves with velocity $v = 25$ mm/s. Compute (i) the displacement sensitivity $K_d$ in mV/mm (rms), (ii) the velocity sensitivity $K_v$, and (iii) the output DC voltage. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Angular frequency (1 mark):** $$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 3000 = 18{,}849.6 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Peak displacement sensitivity (2 marks):** From the derivation in Section 3: $$E_{o,peak/mm} = 2 \, \omega \, I_{pm} \, K_m$$ $$= 2 \times 18{,}849.6 \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.8 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$= 2 \times 18{,}849.6 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$= 0.2413 \text{ V/mm (peak)} = 241.3 \text{ mV/mm (peak)}$$ **Step 3 — Convert peak to rms (1 mark):** $$K_d = \dfrac{241.3}{\sqrt{2}} = 170.6 \text{ mV/mm (rms)}$$ **Step 4 — Velocity sensitivity (1 mark):** $$K_v = \tau \cdot K_d = 0.05 \times 170.6 = 8.53 \text{ mV·s/mm}$$ **Step 5 — DC output voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{out} = K_v \cdot v = 8.53 \times 25 = 213.3 \text{ mV DC}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{K_d \approx 170.6 \text{ mV/mm}, \quad K_v \approx 8.53 \text{ mV/(mm/s)}, \quad V_{out} \approx 213.3 \text{ mV DC}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Style]** **(a) Compare the LVT with a piezoelectric velocity pickup. Discuss the merits, demerits, and typical applications of each. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** | Parameter | **LVT** | **Piezoelectric Velocity Pickup** | |-----------|---------|----------------------------------| | **Principle** | Mutual induction + differentiator | Direct piezoelectric effect (charge generation) | | **Output** | DC voltage (built-in) | AC charge or voltage (needs charge amplifier) | | **Power supply** | Required (built-in oscillator) | Not required (self-generating) | | **Frequency range** | DC to ~1 kHz | ~1 Hz to 10 kHz | | **Linearity** | Excellent (≤ ±0.5% FS) | Moderate (depends on seismic mass design) | | **Temperature sensitivity** | Low | Higher (piezo crystals drift with temperature) | | **Ruggedness** | Very high | Very high | | **Cost** | Medium–High | High (precision ceramics) | | **Lower frequency limit** | **0 Hz (DC capability)** | ~1 Hz (roll-off) | | **Best applications** | Process control, hydraulic piston velocity, slow machine-tool motion | High-frequency vibration monitoring, modal analysis, acoustic emission | **Merits of LVT (2 marks):** - DC response (can measure *constant* velocity). - Excellent linearity and high sensitivity. - Direction sensing via PSD. - Frictionless (no contact between core and windings). - Infinite resolution (no granular element). **Demerits of LVT (1 mark):** - Requires excitation oscillator and external power. - Limited high-frequency response (carrier limits it). - Sensitive to stray magnetic fields (needs shielding). **Merits of Piezo Pickup (1 mark):** - Self-generating (no power needed). - Very wide frequency range. - Extremely rugged, suitable for harsh environments. **Demerits of Piezo Pickup (1 mark):** - Cannot measure DC or very-low-frequency velocity. - Charge-amplifier required. - Temperature-sensitive. --- **(b) Derive the differential-output equation of an LVT in terms of core displacement, starting from Faraday's law. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)** **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The primary winding is fed by an ideal sine-wave current source: $i_p(t) = I_{pm}\sin(\omega t)$. 2. The magnetic circuit is linear (no saturation), so flux $\propto$ MMF. 3. The two secondary windings are identical, with the same number of turns $N_s$, and are connected in series opposition. 4. The core is non-magnetic-loss material (infinite permeability compared to air) and moves only along the coil axis. 5. End effects and stray capacitances are neglected. **Derivation (5 marks):** **Step 1:** Fluxes through the two secondaries are functions of core position $x$: $$\phi_1(x) = \phi_0 + K_\phi x, \quad \phi_2(x) = \phi_0 - K_\phi x$$ where $K_\phi$ is the flux-slope constant (Wb/m). **Step 2:** By Faraday's Law: $$E_{s1} = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dt} = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi_1}{dx}\dfrac{dx}{dt} = -N_s K_\phi \dot{x}$$ $$E_{s2} = -N_s \dfrac{d\phi_2}{dt} = +N_s K_\phi \dot{x}$$ **Step 3:** Net differential output: $$E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = -2 N_s K_\phi \dot{x}$$ **Step 4:** In terms of the LVDT-style displacement formulation (output is the carrier-modulated displacement signal): $$E_o(t) = -2 N_s K_\phi \, \dot{x} \, \cos(\omega t) = K_d \cdot x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega t)$$ where $K_d = 2 N_s K_\phi \omega / \pi$ (after demodulation). **Step 5:** After demodulation and differentiation: $$V_{out} = K_v \cdot v(t), \quad v(t) = \dot{x}(t)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = K_v \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt} = K_v \cdot v(t)}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST MARKS IN KTU ESE:** > > 1. **Confusing LVDT and LVT (2 marks lost):** LVDT gives output ∝ **displacement**; LVT gives output ∝ **velocity**. Many students write them interchangeably — examiners deduct heavily. > 2. **Skipping the PSD block in the diagram (1–2 marks lost):** If you draw only the coil-and-core part without the oscillator, PSD, and differentiator, you lose marks for incomplete signal chain. > 3. **Forgetting the sign / direction sensing (1 mark lost):** Always mention that the **phase of the output** (or the **sign after PSD**) tells the direction of motion. > 4. **Wrong unit on velocity sensitivity (1 mark lost):** Write $K_v$ in V·s/m or V/(m/s) — not V/m. Velocity sensitivity is **per unit velocity**, not per unit displacement. > 5. **Mixing up LVT with LDR / LVDT (0.5–1 mark lost):** LDR is a Light Dependent Resistor (a completely different device). LVDT = Linear Variable Differential Transformer (displacement). LVT = Linear Velocity Transducer. > 6. **Not stating the assumption of linearity (1 mark lost):** In derivations, examiners expect 2–3 explicit assumptions before you start. > 7. **Writing $E_o = E_{s1} + E_{s2}$ instead of $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$ (1 mark lost):** The secondaries are in **series opposition** — the difference, not the sum, gives the output. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — LVT** - [x] **LVT = LVDT + oscillator + PSD + differentiator + DC amplifier.** - [x] LVT output is a **DC voltage proportional to linear velocity** of the core. - [x] Operating principle: **mutual electromagnetic induction** (Faraday's Law). - [x] Primary excitation: typically **2–5 kHz sine wave**, 1–24 V rms. - [x] Three operating zones: **null (E₀=0), +x (E₀>0), −x (E₀<0).** - [x] **Secondaries are in series opposition** → output is the *difference* of EMFs. - [x] **Phase-sensitive demodulator (PSD)** recovers envelope AND sign. - [x] **Differentiator** converts displacement-proportional signal to velocity-proportional signal. - [x] **Core material:** soft iron / Mu-metal (high permeability). - [x] **Key formulas:** - $E_o = E_{s1} - E_{s2} = K_d \cdot x$ - $V_{out} = K_v \cdot v(t) = K_v \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ - $K_v = \tau \cdot K_d$ - [x] **Advantages:** frictionless, infinite resolution, excellent linearity, bidirectional, rugged. - [x] **Disadvantages:** needs excitation source, limited high-frequency response, sensitive to stray EM fields. - [x] **Applications:** hydraulic cylinder velocity feedback, vibration/seismic monitoring, biomedical limb-motion studies, CNC axis control. - [x] **Always mention direction sensing** in KTU answers — it is a frequent 2-mark question. - [x] **Always draw the full block diagram** (oscillator → primary → core → secondaries → PSD → LPF → differentiator → amplifier → output) when explaining LVT construction. > **END OF NOTES — VELOCITY TRANSDUCERS: LVT**

topics.title: Accelerometer - Using Potentiometer, Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT834 - BIOSENSORS AND TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accelerometer - Using Potentiometer, Strain Gauge, Piezoelectric

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Accelerometer?

An **Accelerometer** is a transducer that converts mechanical acceleration experienced by a mass into a measurable electrical signal. The output signal is proportional to the acceleration of the body on which it is mounted. Accelerometers are among the most widely used inertial sensors in biomedical, automotive, aerospace, and industrial applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):**
> An accelerometer is a single-axis or multi-axis device that measures the proper acceleration (g-force) of a moving body. It operates on the principle of a **damped mass on a spring**, where the displacement of a seismic mass relative to the housing is used to infer acceleration.

## 1.2 The Three Accelerometer Variants in this Module

The three accelerometer types discussed in **Module 4** differ in the **secondary transducer** used to detect the displacement of the seismic (proof) mass:

| Type | Sensing Element | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometric Accelerometer** | Variable resistor (potentiometer) | Voltage divider output |
| **Strain Gauge Accelerometer** | Bonded/unbonded strain gauge | Resistance change → Wheatstone bridge |
| **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** | Piezoelectric crystal (Quartz, PZT) | Charge/voltage generation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you place a heavy ball inside a box on a soft spring. When the box suddenly accelerates (push or jerk), the ball "lags behind" due to inertia and compresses the spring. The more the box accelerates, the more the spring compresses. An accelerometer simply *measures how much the spring compressed* and converts that number into an electrical reading. The "ball" is the **seismic mass**, the "spring" is the **suspension**, and the **electrical sensor** (potentiometer, strain gauge, or piezo crystal) watches the mass move.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravity (g₀) = 9.80665 m/s²**
- **Sensitivity units**: V/(m/s²) or pC/g
- **Frequency Range (Typical)**: 0.1 Hz to 20 kHz
- **Seismic mass range**: 0.1 g to 100 g (inertial element)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Mass-Spring-Damper Model

All three accelerometers share the same **mechanical core**: a proof mass (m) suspended by a spring (k) and damped by a damper (c) inside a housing. When the housing experiences acceleration $a_i(t)$, the mass moves relative to the housing by a relative displacement $x(t)$.

### The Governing Differential Equation

For a forced mass-spring-damper system, applying Newton's Second Law to the proof mass:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x}_a(t) = -k \cdot x(t) - c \cdot \dot{x}(t)$$

Where $x_a(t)$ is the absolute displacement of the mass, and $x(t)$ is the relative displacement between the mass and the housing. The input acceleration is $a_i(t) = \ddot{x}_i(t)$, and $x(t) = x_a(t) - x_i(t)$.

The standard input-output form becomes:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x}(t) + c \cdot \dot{x}(t) + k \cdot x(t) = -m \cdot a_i(t)$$

### Why is the Right-Hand Side $-m \cdot a_i(t)$?

- The housing accelerates by $a_i(t)$.
- An inertial (fictitious) force of magnitude $m \cdot a_i(t)$ acts on the mass in the **opposite direction** of housing motion.
- This inertial force deflects the spring by $x(t)$, producing a restoring force $k \cdot x(t)$.

## 2.2 Transfer Function and Natural Frequency

Taking the Laplace Transform of the governing equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$\left(m s^2 + c s + k\right) X(s) = -m \cdot A_i(s)$$

Therefore, the **transfer function** relating output displacement $X(s)$ to input acceleration $A_i(s)$ is:

$$\frac{X(s)}{A_i(s)} = \frac{-m}{m s^2 + c s + k} = \frac{-1}{s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$$

Where:
- **Natural (undamped) frequency**: $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ (in **rad/s**)
- **Damping ratio**: $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2 \sqrt{m k}}$ (dimensionless)
- **Damped natural frequency**: $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$

## 2.3 Operating Condition: $\omega \ll \omega_n$ (Seismometer Mode)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an accelerometer, the **relative displacement $x(t)$ must be proportional to input acceleration $a_i(t)$**. This happens when the excitation frequency $\omega$ is much smaller than the natural frequency $\omega_n$ (i.e., $\omega / \omega_n \to 0$).

Under this condition, the spring force dominates, and:

$$x(t) \approx -\frac{m \cdot a_i(t)}{k} = -\frac{a_i(t)}{\omega_n^2}$$

This means **displacement is directly proportional to acceleration** — the core principle of any accelerometer.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's Second Law | N |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 4 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $x(t) = -\dfrac{a_i(t)}{\omega_n^2}$ | Seismometer-mode displacement | m |
| 6 | $S_{pot} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{L} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2}$ | Potentiometric sensitivity | V·s²/m |
| 7 | $S_{sg} = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{GF \cdot \epsilon}{\omega_n^2}$ | Strain-gauge sensitivity | V·s²/m |
| 8 | $S_{pz} = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot m}{k \cdot C_p}$ | Piezoelectric sensitivity | V·s²/m |
| 9 | $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge | C |
| 10 | $V = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot a \cdot m}{C_p}$ | Piezoelectric output voltage | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In the table above, vertical bars in conditions like $\omega \ll \omega_n$ have been written as $\ll$ (textual) to prevent breaking the markdown table. Where the symbol $\vert$ is needed, write it as $\vert x \vert$ using LaTeX `\vert` command only.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility Across Industries

- **Biomedical**: Patient activity monitoring, gait analysis, fall detection in elderly, vestibular prosthesis.
- **Automotive**: Airbag deployment, Electronic Stability Control (ESC), anti-lock braking.
- **Aerospace**: Inertial Navigation Systems (INS), flight control.
- **Industrial**: Vibration monitoring of rotating machinery, structural health monitoring of bridges/buildings.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Screen rotation in smartphones, step counters in wearables.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Newton's Law to Displacement-Based Acceleration Reading

We will derive the **seismometer-mode displacement expression** from the basic mass-spring-damper differential equation in full detail.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram and Force Balance

The seismic mass $m$ is connected to the housing via a spring (stiffness $k$) and damper (coefficient $c$). Let the housing displacement be $x_i(t)$ and absolute mass displacement be $x_a(t)$. Define relative displacement:

$$x(t) = x_a(t) - x_i(t)$$

Differentiating twice:

$$\ddot{x}_a(t) = \ddot{x}(t) + \ddot{x}_i(t) = \ddot{x}(t) + a_i(t)$$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law to the Mass

The forces acting on the mass are the spring force $-k \cdot x(t)$ and the damper force $-c \cdot \dot{x}(t)$. There is no external applied force on the mass (it is freely suspended). Therefore:

$$m \cdot \ddot{x}_a(t) = -k \cdot x(t) - c \cdot \dot{x}(t)$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Absolute Acceleration

Replacing $\ddot{x}_a(t)$ with $\ddot{x}(t) + a_i(t)$:

$$m \cdot \left[\ddot{x}(t) + a_i(t)\right] = -k \cdot x(t) - c \cdot \dot{x}(t)$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Standard Form

$$m \cdot \ddot{x}(t) + c \cdot \dot{x}(t) + k \cdot x(t) = -m \cdot a_i(t)$$

This is the **standard second-order differential equation** of an accelerometer.

### Step 5: Steady-State Solution at Low Frequency

Assume harmonic excitation $a_i(t) = A_0 \sin(\omega t)$ with $\omega \ll \omega_n$. In the steady state, $x(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$. Substitute into the equation and use the quasi-static approximation (inertia term $m\ddot{x}$ and damping term $c\dot{x}$ are both negligible compared to spring force $kx$):

$$k \cdot X_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi) \approx -m \cdot A_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 6: Solve for Amplitude

Comparing amplitudes:

$$k \cdot X_0 = -m \cdot A_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_0 = -\frac{m \cdot A_0}{k} = -\frac{A_0}{\omega_n^2}$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{\,x(t) \approx -\frac{a_i(t)}{\omega_n^2}\,}$$

This is the **fundamental accelerometer equation**: relative displacement of the mass is proportional to applied acceleration, with proportionality constant $-1/\omega_n^2$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Sensitivity of a Potentiometric Accelerometer

### Setup
A seismic mass $m$ is mounted on a spring (constant $k$). The mass carries a **wiper** that slides over a potentiometer track of total length $L$ and total resistance $R_p$. The potentiometer is excited by a DC source $V_{ex}$.

### Step 1: Displacement of the Mass

From the seismometer-mode result:

$$x(t) = \frac{m \cdot a_i(t)}{k} = \frac{a_i(t)}{\omega_n^2}$$

### Step 2: Resistance Change of the Potentiometer

The wiper position changes by $x(t)$ over a track of total length $L$, producing a resistance change of:

$$\Delta R = R_p \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L}$$

### Step 3: Voltage Divider Output

The potentiometer is connected as a voltage divider. The output voltage across the wiper-to-ground portion is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_p \pm \Delta R}{R_p} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{x(t)}{L}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $x(t)$

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{a_i(t)}{\omega_n^2}$$

### Step 5: Sensitivity Definition

Sensitivity is defined as the output per unit input acceleration:

$$\boxed{\,S_{pot} = \frac{V_{out}}{a_i} = \frac{V_{ex}}{L \cdot \omega_n^2}\,} \quad \left[\frac{V \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Sensitivity of a Strain-Gauge Accelerometer

### Setup
A seismic mass $m$ is cantilevered on a spring element. **Bonded semiconductor or foil strain gauges** are mounted on the spring element. The gauges form a **Wheatstone bridge** excited by $V_{ex}$.

### Step 1: Strain Produced by Deflection

The bending strain at the surface of the spring element due to deflection $x$ is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{6 \cdot m \cdot a_i}{E \cdot b \cdot t^2}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus, $b$ is width, and $t$ is thickness of the beam.

### Step 2: Resistance Change per Gauge

For a strain gauge with gauge factor $GF$:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = GF \cdot \epsilon$$

### Step 3: Bridge Output Voltage

For a full-bridge configuration with 4 active gauges, two in tension and two in compression:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$$

For a balanced full bridge with $GF \cdot \epsilon$ contribution from each:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \epsilon$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\epsilon$ and $x$

Using $x = a_i/\omega_n^2$ and the relationship between $\epsilon$ and $x$:

$$\epsilon = \frac{k_x \cdot x}{E \cdot b \cdot t^2 / 6}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,S_{sg} = \frac{V_{out}}{a_i} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{m}{k \cdot \alpha}\,}$$

where $\alpha$ depends on the spring geometry.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Sensitivity of a Piezoelectric Accelerometer

### Step 1: Force on the Crystal (Newton's Law)

The seismic mass $m$ experiencing acceleration $a_i$ produces an inertial force on the piezoelectric crystal:

$$F = m \cdot a_i(t)$$

### Step 2: Charge Generated (Piezoelectric Equation)

For a piezoelectric crystal with piezoelectric charge constant $d_{33}$ (C/N):

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a_i(t)$$

### Step 3: Output Voltage

The charge appears across the inherent capacitance $C_p$ of the crystal (plus cable and amplifier capacitance $C_t$):

$$V_{out} = \frac{Q}{C_p + C_t}$$

### Step 4: Sensitivity Expression

$$\boxed{\,S_{pz} = \frac{V_{out}}{a_i} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot m}{C_p + C_t}\,} \quad \left[\frac{V \cdot s^2}{m}\right]$$

### Step 5: Charge-Mode Sensitivity

Charge-mode amplifiers ignore $C_t$ issues; sensitivity in **pC/g** is:

$$S_{Q} = d_{33} \cdot m \quad \text{(in pC per g of acceleration)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Numerical Note:** For $m = 10$ g = 0.01 kg, and $d_{33} = 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N (for Quartz), $S_Q = 2.3 \times 10^{-14}$ C·s²/m = **23 pC/g**. This is a typical commercial sensitivity for a quartz piezoelectric accelerometer.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A piezoelectric accelerometer has a seismic mass of 50 g and a PZT crystal with $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N. The total capacitance is 1000 pF. Calculate the voltage sensitivity.

**Given:**
- $m = 50 \text{ g} = 0.05$ kg
- $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $C_p + C_t = 1000 \text{ pF} = 1000 \times 10^{-12}$ F = $1 \times 10^{-9}$ F

**Solution:**

Charge sensitivity:

$$S_Q = d_{33} \cdot m = (400 \times 10^{-12}) \times 0.05 = 20 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C·s}^2/\text{m}$$

Voltage sensitivity:

$$S_V = \frac{S_Q}{C_p + C_t} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.02 \text{ V·s}^2/\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\,S_V = 20 \text{ mV per (m/s}^2\text{)}\,}$$

This means for every $1$ m/s² of input acceleration, the accelerometer produces **20 mV** of output.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Mass-Spring-Damper Accelerometer Architecture

The following Mermaid block illustrates the functional architecture common to all three accelerometer variants. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords used).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input Acceleration A_i] --> B1[Mechanical Frame Housing]
    B1 --> C1[Seismic Proof Mass M]
    C1 --> D1[Spring Element K]
    C1 --> E1[Damper Element C]
    D1 --> F1[Relative Displacement X]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1{Secondary Transducer}
    G1 --> H1[Potentiometric Sensor]
    G1 --> H2[Strain Gauge Sensor]
    G1 --> H3[Piezoelectric Sensor]
    H1 --> I1[Signal Conditioner Amplifier]
    H2 --> I1
    H3 --> I1
    I1 --> J1[Output Signal V_out]
    J1 --> K1[Display or DAQ System]
```

## 4.2 Potentiometric Accelerometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Seismic Mass] --> B2[Slider Wiper Arm]
    B2 --> C2[Resistance Track L total]
    C2 --> D2[Excitation Voltage V_ex]
    D2 --> E2[Output Voltage V_out]
    E2 --> F2[Signal Amplifier]
    F2 --> G2[Display]
```

## 4.3 Strain Gauge Accelerometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Seismic Mass M] --> B3[Spring Cantilever Beam]
    B3 --> C3[Strain Gauge 1 Top Fiber Tension]
    B3 --> C4[Strain Gauge 2 Bottom Fiber Compression]
    C3 --> D3[Wheatstone Bridge Node A]
    C4 --> D3
    D3 --> E3[Bridge Excitation V_ex]
    E3 --> F3[Differential Output V_out]
    F3 --> G3[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    G3 --> H3[Filter and Display]
```

## 4.4 Piezoelectric Accelerometer — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A4[Seismic Mass M] --> B4[Inertial Force F]
    B4 --> C4[Piezoelectric Crystal PZT or Quartz]
    C4 --> D4[Charge Output Q]
    D4 --> E4[Charge Amplifier]
    E4 --> F4[Voltage Output V_out]
    F4 --> G4[Filter and Recorder]
```

## 4.5 Comparison of Three Types (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Accelerometer] --> B0[Mechanical Stage]
    A0 --> C0[Transduction Stage]
    A0 --> D0[Electronic Stage]
    B0 --> B01[Mass Spring Damper]
    C0 --> C01[Potentiometric Resistive]
    C0 --> C02[Strain Gauge Resistive Bridge]
    C0 --> C03[Piezoelectric Charge Generation]
    D0 --> D01[Amplifier]
    D0 --> D02[Filter]
    D0 --> D03[ADC and Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:** In KTU board exams, if a Mermaid-style block diagram is not feasible to draw by hand, you should sketch the **mechanical cross-section** (housing, spring, mass) and label the **transduction element** with its electrical output symbol (variable resistor / strain gauge / crystal) — this is a 2- to 3-mark sure-shot answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Q: Define an accelerometer. With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer.**

**Model Answer:**

An accelerometer is a transducer that measures proper acceleration by detecting the relative displacement of a seismic mass suspended inside a housing.

**Working of Piezoelectric Accelerometer:**

1. **Seismic mass (m)** is bonded to a piezoelectric crystal (e.g., Quartz or PZT).
2. When the housing experiences acceleration $a_i$, the inertial force $F = m \cdot a_i$ acts on the crystal.
3. The crystal generates a charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ proportional to the applied force.
4. This charge is converted to voltage $V_{out} = Q / C_p$ using a charge amplifier.
5. The output voltage is proportional to acceleration: $V_{out} = \dfrac{d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a_i}{C_p}$.

**Block Diagram:**

$$\boxed{\text{Acceleration} \to \text{Seismic Mass} \to \text{Piezo Crystal} \to \text{Charge Amp} \to V_{out}}$$

**Key Advantage:** Very wide frequency range (1 Hz to 20 kHz), self-generating (no external excitation needed).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Q: Compare potentiometric, strain gauge, and piezoelectric accelerometers based on principle, output, frequency range, and application.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Potentiometric | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Wiper position change | Resistance change due to strain | Charge generation |
| **Excitation Needed** | Yes (DC source) | Yes (Bridge voltage) | No (self-generating) |
| **Output** | Voltage (divider) | Bridge voltage | Charge or voltage |
| **Frequency Range** | Low (0–10 Hz) | Medium (0–1 kHz) | High (1 Hz–20 kHz) |
| **Sensitivity** | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Drawback** | Contact wear, friction | Temperature sensitive | Cannot measure static (DC) |
| **Application** | Crash sensors, low-freq vibration | Industrial weighing, tilt | Vibration analysis, modal testing |

---

## PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Main Question
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Derive the governing differential equation of a seismic mass accelerometer and obtain the expression for displacement in seismometer mode. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **strain gauge accelerometer**. Derive its sensitivity expression. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 Marks
