| Automated Immunoassay Analyzers | Pushes reagent vials into reading chambers |
| Microfluidic Biochips | Drives micro-valves to control fluid micro-channels |
| Sterile Packaging Lines | Seals blister packs with precise force |
| Prosthetics & Rehabilitation Robotics | Powers lightweight artificial limbs |
| Bioreactor Control | Operates large butterfly/sanitary valves |

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

The following are standard ISO 5599 / ISO 4414 metrics relevant to pneumatic actuator sizing and selection:

- **Working Pressure (P):** typically **4 to 8 bar (400 to 800 kPa)** standard industrial supply; biomedical micro-systems often use **0.5 to 2 bar**.
- **Bore Diameter (D):** **8 mm to 320 mm** for industrial cylinders.
- **Stroke Length (L):** **10 mm to 2000 mm**.
- **Operating Temperature:** **-10 °C to +80 °C** for standard seals (NBR/FKM).
- **Filtration Standard:** **ISO 8573-1 Class 5.5.4** (≤ 40 µm particulate, ≤ 5 mg/m³ oil, dew point ≤ 3 °C).
- **Compressibility of Air:** at 20 °C, **κ = 1.4** (ratio of specific heats).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why compressibility matters:** Unlike hydraulic fluid, air is highly compressible (≈ 1:1000 ratio vs oil). This is the single most important reason pneumatic actuators are **position-soft** (cannot hold precise position without braking) and **speed-elastic** (load changes affect velocity). This property is heavily tested in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

A pneumatic actuator system is a **causal chain** from energy source to mechanical work. Let us break this down into the canonical 7-stage energy flow:

### Stage 1 — Air Generation (Energy Origin)
- The **compressor** converts electrical/mechanical energy into pneumatic (pressure) energy.
- Reciprocating, rotary screw, and scroll compressors are common; for bio-clean rooms, **oil-free scroll compressors** are mandatory to prevent contamination.

### Stage 2 — Storage and Stabilization
- A **reservoir (receiver tank)** dampens pressure pulsations and provides buffer volume for peak demands.
- Critical for systems with intermittent high-flow demand (e.g., batch bio-reactors).

### Stage 3 — Air Treatment (The "FRL" Triad)
This is the **most heavily tested** stage in KTU papers. The acronym **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator**.

| FRL Component | Function | Why Critical for Bio-Systems |
|---|---|---|
| **Filter** | Removes particulates (down to 5 µm), water aerosols, oil mist | Prevents valve clogging and sensor contamination |
| **Regulator** | Reduces and stabilizes downstream pressure (e.g., 8 bar → 4 bar) | Ensures repeatable actuator force |
| **Lubricator** | Atomizes oil into the airstream to lubricate moving valve parts | Reduces wear; *omitted in clean food/pharma lines* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clean-room bio-pharma note:** When the pneumatic actuator directly contacts a medical/pharmaceutical product, the **lubricator is removed** and **dry, oil-free, sterile-filtered air** is mandated.

### Stage 4 — Distribution and Isolation
- **Manifold blocks** distribute treated air to multiple actuators.
- **Isolation valves (ball or gate valves)** allow maintenance of individual branches without shutting down the whole system.

### Stage 5 — Directional Control
- A **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** routes pressurized air to either the bore side or the rod side of the cylinder, dictating extension/retraction.
- Classified by **number of ports** and **number of positions** (e.g., 5/2 valve = 5 ports, 2 positions; 5/3 valve adds a center position for locking or floating).

### Stage 6 — Flow / Speed Control
- **Needle valves** or **throttle-check valves** regulate exhaust (meter-out) or inlet (meter-in) airflow, controlling piston velocity.
- For bidirectional control, a **dual flow control valve** is used.

### Stage 7 — Actuation and Exhaust
- The **actuator element** (cylinder or motor) converts pressure energy to motion.
- Exhaust air is passed through a **muffler (silencer)** to reduce noise (typically **75–85 dB** reduced to **< 60 dB**).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Architecture

| Design Choice | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| Why compress air and not just use electricity? | Pneumatic systems are **intrinsically safe** in explosive/flammable environments (no sparks), and are **overload-tolerant** (air leaks out instead of burning out the motor). |
| Why use FRL before the valve? | Dirt/water destroys valve spools costing ~₹15k–₹80k. Filter + regulator + lubricator protect the entire downstream. |
| Why not just use hydraulics? | Hydraulics offer higher force density but require leak-free seals (contamination risk in bio labs), fire-resistant fluids, and are heavier. |
| Why include a muffler? | OSHA / factory norms mandate < 85 dB continuous exposure; unmuffled exhaust can exceed **110 dB**. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 7 equations — they cover > 90% of KTU numerical questions on pneumatic actuators.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Output Force | $F = P \cdot A$ | N | Pressure × Effective piston area |
| 2 | Effective Cylinder Area (Bore-side) | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | m² | Full bore area |
| 3 | Effective Cylinder Area (Rod-side) | $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$ | m² | Reduced area (annulus) |
| 4 | Piston Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Flow rate / Area |
| 5 | Air Consumption per Cycle | $V_c = (A_1 \cdot L_1) + (A_2 \cdot L_2)$ | m³ | Sum of both stroke volumes |
| 6 | Theoretical Power Output | $P_{out} = F \cdot v = P \cdot Q$ | W | Pressure × Flow |
| 7 | Air Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{P_1 \cdot Q}{R \cdot T_1}$ | kg/s | Real-gas mass flow (R = 287 J/kg·K for air) |

**Where:**
- $D$ = cylinder bore diameter (m)
- $d$ = piston rod diameter (m)
- $L_1, L_2$ = stroke lengths in each direction (m)
- $P$ = gauge pressure (Pa)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $T_1$ = upstream absolute temperature (K)

**Critical Note on Force Asymmetry:**

$$F_{extend} = P \cdot A_1 = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

$$F_{retract} = P \cdot A_2 = P \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$

Because $A_1 > A_2$, the cylinder produces **more force on extension than retraction** for the same supply pressure. This is a **favourite KTU trick question**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pneumatic actuators are the **workhorses of factory automation** — they are responsible for approximately **35–40% of all linear motion actuators installed in manufacturing globally** (industry estimate). In bio-engineering, they are uniquely valuable because:

1. **Sterility compatibility** — air does not contaminate biological samples the way oil leaks from hydraulics can.
2. **Temperature insensitivity** — works in cold rooms (bioreactor storage) where hydraulic fluid would thicken.
3. **Lightweight & portable** — enables wearable prosthetics and surgical robots.
4. **Fail-safe** — when air supply is cut, the actuator returns to a defined default (spring-return or gravity-return), critical for safety in bio-hazard labs.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Derivation: Output Force in a Double-Acting Cylinder

A **double-acting cylinder** has two ports — air can be admitted to either side of the piston, generating force in both directions. Let us derive the force asymmetry rigorously.

### Given:
- Supply gauge pressure: $P_g$ (Pa)
- Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa
- Cylinder bore diameter: $D$ (m)
- Piston rod diameter: $d$ (m)
- Stroke length: $L$ (m)

### Step 1 — Establish Absolute Pressure

A pressure gauge reads the pressure above atmospheric. To use the ideal gas / force equation, we need absolute pressure.

$$P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$$

### Step 2 — Effective Area on the Bore Side (Piston Rod Side Sealed)

When pressurized air pushes the rod out, the effective piston area equals the full circular area:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

### Step 3 — Effective Area on the Rod Side (Bore Side Sealed, Pushed Back)

When pressurized air pushes the rod back, only the annular (ring-shaped) area is exposed to pressure, because the rod occupies the centre:

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} - \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$

### Step 4 — Theoretical Force (Ignoring Friction)

By Newton's second law, force equals pressure times area:

$$F_{extend} = P_g \cdot A_1 = P_g \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

$$F_{retract} = P_g \cdot A_2 = P_g \cdot \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Force Ratio (Asymmetry)

$$\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{D^{2} - d^{2}}{D^{2}} = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{2}$$

This ratio is always **less than 1** and approaches 1 as $d \ll D$.

### Step 6 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Problem)

**Problem:** A double-acting cylinder has bore $D = 50$ mm and rod diameter $d = 20$ mm. Supply pressure is $P_g = 6$ bar. Calculate the theoretical extending and retracting forces.

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm = $0.050$ m
- $d = 20$ mm = $0.020$ m
- $P_g = 6$ bar = $6 \times 10^{5}$ Pa

**Solution — Extension Force:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F_{extend} = (6 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3}) = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$

**Solution — Retraction Force:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi [(0.050)^{2} - (0.020)^{2}]}{4} = \frac{\pi [0.0025 - 0.0004]}{4} = 1.6493 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F_{retract} = (6 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.6493 \times 10^{-3}) = 989.6 \text{ N}$$

**Force Ratio:**

$$\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = \frac{989.6}{1178.1} = 0.8401 = 1 - (0.4)^{2} = 1 - 0.16 = 0.84 \;\;\checkmark$$

This matches the formula $1 - (d/D)^{2}$ perfectly. The rod-side force is **84% of the bore-side force** for a 20 mm rod in a 50 mm bore.

## 3.2 Derivative Derivation: Cylinder Piston Velocity and Air Consumption

### Given:
- Volumetric flow rate to the bore side: $Q$ (m³/s)
- Stroke length: $L$ (m)
- Cylinder bore: $D$ (m)

### Step 1 — Piston Velocity from Continuity

The volumetric flow entering the cylinder must equal the rate of volume swept by the piston:

$$Q = A \cdot v \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$$

### Step 2 — Stroke Time

Time to complete one stroke:

$$t_{stroke} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \cdot A}{Q} = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q}$$

### Step 3 — Air Consumption per Complete Cycle

In a full extension + retraction cycle, the cylinder consumes air on **both** sides. Since the rod-side volume is smaller, the total air per cycle is:

$$V_{cycle} = A_1 \cdot L + A_2 \cdot L = L(A_1 + A_2)$$

Substituting:

$$V_{cycle} = L \left[ \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} + \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} \right] = \frac{\pi L}{4} \left[ 2D^{2} - d^{2} \right]$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Problem:** For the cylinder above (D = 50 mm, d = 20 mm, L = 200 mm), supply pressure = 6 bar. The piston completes 10 cycles per minute. Calculate the air consumption at atmospheric conditions (free air delivery).

**Solution:**

$$V_{cycle} = \frac{\pi \times 0.200}{4} [2 \times (0.050)^{2} - (0.020)^{2}]$$

$$V_{cycle} = (0.1571) \times [0.0050 - 0.0004] = (0.1571) \times (0.0046)$$

$$V_{cycle} = 7.225 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} = 0.7225 \text{ L per cycle}$$

**Cycles per minute:** 10 → **per minute consumption** = $7.225$ L/min at 6 bar.

**Converting to free air delivery (atmospheric):**

Using Boyle's Law (isothermal compression): $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$

$$V_{atm} = V_{6 bar} \times \frac{(6 + 1.013)}{1.013} = 0.7225 \times 6.92 = 5.00 \text{ L per cycle at atm}$$

$$V_{atm,per\,min} = 5.00 \times 10 = 50.0 \text{ L/min} \approx 0.05 \text{ m}^{3/min}$$

This is a realistic figure for a small industrial cylinder; **sizing of compressor reservoirs** depends critically on this calculation.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Cylinder Type Selection

| Selection Criterion | Single-Acting | Double-Acting | Tandem Cylinder |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force direction | One direction (spring return) | Both directions | Both, with **2× force** |
| Air consumption | Lower (one side only) | Higher (both sides) | Highest |
| Position control | Poor (spring drift) | Good with brake | Excellent |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Bio-application | Sample ejection | Reagent dispensing | High-force pressing |


## 4.4 Component Interaction Map — Signal vs Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ControlLayer[Control Layer Low Power]
        C1[Controller PLC or Microcontroller]
        C2[Sensor Feedback Position or Pressure]
    end
    subgraph SignalLayer[Signal Layer Electrical]
        S1[Solenoid Coil 24 V DC]
        S2[Relay or Driver]
    end
    subgraph PowerLayer[Power Layer Pneumatic]
        P1[Directional Control Valve Spool]
        P2[Compressed Air Supply]
        P3[Actuator Cylinder]
        P4[Mechanical Output]
    end
    C1 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> P1
    P2 --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> C2
    C2 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the **closed feedback loop** — the controller (e.g., a PLC) sends a 24 V signal to the solenoid, which shifts the DCV spool, which admits air to the cylinder, which moves the load, whose position is sensed and fed back to the controller. This is the classic **electro-pneumatic control loop** used in modern bio-automation.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the major components of a typical pneumatic actuator circuit and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~1 mark per major point):**

A typical pneumatic actuator circuit consists of the following major components:

1. **Compressor and Receiver Tank** (1 mark): The compressor converts electrical/mechanical energy into pressurized air; the receiver tank stores air and stabilizes supply pressure.

2. **FRL Unit (Filter, Regulator, Lubricator)** (1 mark): The filter removes particulates and moisture; the regulator maintains constant downstream pressure; the lubricator injects oil mist for valve lubrication.

3. **Directional Control Valve (DCV) and Actuator (Cylinder/Motor)** (1 mark): The DCV routes air to the actuator; the actuator converts pneumatic energy into linear or rotary mechanical motion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **exhaust muffler** and **flow control valves** — these are also graded components. A complete answer mentions *at least 6 components* for full marks.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between single-acting and double-acting pneumatic cylinders. Give one bio-medical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|---|---|---|
| Air ports | One (spring returns piston) | Two (air drives both ways) |
| Force direction | One direction only | Both directions |
| Air consumption | Lower | Higher |
| **Bio-medical use** | Sample ejection in ELISA plate washer (1.5 marks) | Reagent dispensing pump in immunoassay analyzer (1.5 marks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts — typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the components and working of a typical pneumatic actuator circuit used in bio-automation. List its advantages and disadvantages.**

#### Part (a) — Components and Working (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (4 marks):**

The pneumatic circuit, from air source to mechanical output, consists of:

**1. Air Supply Section (1.5 marks):**
- **Compressor** (electric/diesel-driven) — generates compressed air.
- **Receiver tank** — stores and stabilizes air.
- **Safety relief valve** — protects against over-pressure.

**2. Air Treatment Section — FRL Unit (2 marks):**
- **Filter** — removes dust, water aerosols, oil mist (down to 5 µm).
- **Regulator** — reduces and stabilizes supply pressure (e.g., 8 bar → 4 bar).
- **Lubricator** — injects fine oil mist to lubricate valve spools.

**3. Control and Actuation Section (2.5 marks):**
- **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — typically a 5/2 or 5/3 solenoid-operated valve that routes air to either side of the cylinder.
- **Flow control valves (throttle-check)** — regulate piston speed via meter-out or meter-in control.
- **Double-acting cylinder** — converts pressure into linear motion.
- **Exhaust mufflers** — reduce noise from exhaust air.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The compressor draws in atmospheric air, compresses it to working pressure (typically 6–8 bar), and stores it in the receiver tank.
2. The treated air passes through the FRL unit, where it is filtered, pressure-regulated, and lubricated.
3. On receiving an electrical signal from the controller, the solenoid shifts the DCV spool, connecting the supply port to one side of the cylinder while venting the other side to atmosphere.
4. The pressure differential across the piston produces a force $F = P \cdot A$, driving the rod outward (extension) or inward (retraction).
5. Flow control valves meter the air, setting piston speed; exhaust air is silenced by mufflers.
6. The mechanical output performs work — for example, pushing a reagent vial into an analyzer's read-head.

#### Part (b) — Advantages and Disadvantages (7 marks)

**Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Intrinsic Safety (1 mark):** No sparks, no fire risk — ideal for explosive bio-solvent environments.
2. **Cleanliness (1 mark):** Air does not contaminate samples; oil-free options available for sterile pharma.
3. **Simplicity and Cost (1 mark):** Fewer components, low maintenance, low capital cost (₹8k–₹50k typical).
4. **High Speed and Long Stroke (1 mark):** Piston speeds up to 1–2 m/s; strokes up to 2 m achievable.

**Disadvantages (3 marks):**

1. **Position Inaccuracy (1 mark):** Air compressibility makes precise position control difficult without mechanical brakes.
2. **Lower Force Density (1 mark):** Limited to ~6–8 bar safely; cannot match hydraulic force output.
3. **Noise and Air Consumption (1 mark):** Exhaust noise (up to 110 dB) and high compressed-air cost in continuous operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write **only advantages** or **only disadvantages** in part (b). The question explicitly demands *both* — full 7 marks require **at least 4 advantages and 3 disadvantages** with brief justifications. Writing a list without explanation caps your marks at 4/7.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A double-acting pneumatic cylinder has a bore diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 35 mm. The supply pressure is 5 bar. Calculate: (a) the extending and retracting forces, and (b) the air consumption per cycle for a stroke of 250 mm. Discuss the suitability of this pneumatic actuator for a bio-medical packaging line.**

#### Part (a) — Force Calculations (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Bore: $D = 80$ mm = $0.080$ m
- Rod: $d = 35$ mm = $0.035$ m
- Supply pressure: $P_g = 5$ bar = $5 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- Stroke: $L = 250$ mm = $0.250$ m

**Effective Areas (2 marks):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} = \frac{\pi [(0.080)^{2} - (0.035)^{2}]}{4}$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.0064 - 0.001225)}{4} = 4.0645 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Extending Force (2.5 marks):**

$$F_{extend} = P_g \cdot A_1 = (5 \times 10^{5}) \times (5.0265 \times 10^{-3}) = 2513.3 \text{ N} \approx 2.51 \text{ kN}$$

**Retracting Force (2.5 marks):**

$$F_{retract} = P_g \cdot A_2 = (5 \times 10^{5}) \times (4.0645 \times 10^{-3}) = 2032.3 \text{ N} \approx 2.03 \text{ kN}$$

**Force Ratio (bonus, not required):**

$$\frac{F_{retract}}{F_{extend}} = \frac{2.03}{2.51} = 0.809 = 1 - (0.4375)^{2} \;\;\checkmark$$

#### Part (b) — Air Consumption and Bio-Medical Suitability (7 marks)

**Air Consumption per Cycle (3 marks):**

$$V_{cycle} = L \cdot (A_1 + A_2) = 0.250 \times (5.0265 + 4.0645) \times 10^{-3}$$

$$V_{cycle} = 0.250 \times 9.091 \times 10^{-3} = 2.273 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} = 2.273 \text{ L per cycle at 5 bar}$$

**Converting to Free Air Delivery (atmospheric):**

$$V_{atm} = V_{cycle} \times \frac{(5 + 1.013)}{1.013} = 2.273 \times 5.93 = 13.48 \text{ L per cycle at atm}$$

**Suitability for Bio-Medical Packaging Line (4 marks):**

The cylinder is **highly suitable** for bio-medical packaging applications for the following reasons:

1. **Adequate Force Output (1 mark):** ~2.5 kN extension force is sufficient for sealing blister packs, capping vials, and forming tray seals (typical sealing force: 0.5–3 kN).

2. **Clean Operation (1 mark):** With oil-free air supply, no contamination risk to pharmaceutical products — meets **GMP and FDA 21 CFR Part 11** standards.

3. **Speed and Repeatability (1 mark):** Stroke of 250 mm completes in ~0.25–0.5 s at typical piston speeds, supporting production rates of 60–120 cycles/minute.

4. **Sterile Compatibility (1 mark):** Air does not support microbial growth and can be **0.2 µm sterile-filtered** before contact with sterile barrier systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Part (b):** Students often calculate air consumption but **forget the unit conversion from gauge pressure to atmospheric pressure**. The question asks for "air consumption per cycle" — by engineering convention, this is **free air delivery (FAD)** in litres at atmospheric pressure. Forgetting to multiply by the pressure ratio costs **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this checklist 30 minutes before your exam for a final revision sweep.**

- [ ] A **pneumatic actuator** uses compressed air to produce linear or rotary motion.
- [ ] The two main types are **linear (cylinder)** and **rotary (air motor)**.
- [ ] A typical circuit has **7 functional stages**: generation → storage → treatment → distribution → control → speed regulation → actuation.
- [ ] **FRL = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator** is the mandatory air treatment triad.
- [ ] **Regulator** stabilizes downstream pressure; **Filter** removes contaminants; **Lubricator** injects oil (omitted in clean pharma).
- [ ] **DCV (Directional Control Valve)** is denoted as **n/m** (n = ports, m = positions); common: **5/2** and **5/3**.
- [ ] **Force on extension** uses full bore area $A_1 = \pi D^{2}/4$.
- [ ] **Force on retraction** uses annular area $A_2 = \pi(D^{2}-d^{2})/4$.
- [ ] **Force asymmetry**: $F_{retract} < F_{extend}$ always, ratio = $1 - (d/D)^{2}$.
- [ ] **Piston velocity** $v = Q/A$.
- [ ] **Air consumption per cycle** $V_{cycle} = L(A_1 + A_2)$ at supply pressure; convert to FAD via $P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$.
- [ ] **Standard supply pressure**: 4–8 bar industrial; 0.5–2 bar bio-microfluidic.
- [ ] **Key advantages**: clean, intrinsically safe, fast, low cost, simple, fail-safe.
- [ ] **Key disadvantages**: positionally soft, noisy, lower force, requires air treatment.
- [ ] **Speed control** is via **meter-out** (throttle exhaust — preferred) or **meter-in** (throttle inlet).
- [ ] **Mufflers** reduce exhaust noise from ~110 dB to < 60 dB.
- [ ] **Spring-return** cylinders are single-acting; air-driven in both directions = double-acting.
- [ ] **Bio-medical use cases**: ELISA plate washers, immunoassay analyzers, sterile vial capping, microfluidic valve actuation, prosthetic limbs, bioreactor valve automation.
- [ ] **Compressibility of air** is the root cause of position inaccuracy — understand it cold.
